Our evaluation shows that VEGA achieves competitive goal progress while reducing collisions by 33.0% and improving obstacle clearance by 17.9% over the strongest baseline on VEGABench, while improving success by at least 150.0%, reducing collisions by at least 66.7%, and improving obstacle clearance by at least 60.0% in real-world trials. Ultimately, we demonstrate that video-derived geometric supervision provides a scalable and effective signal for training obstacle-aware navigation VLAs. The code and benchmark will be released at the time of publication.

AI.tldr: VEGA trains navigation VLAs from unlabeled egocentric videos by reconstructing scene geometry to generate obstacle-aware trajectories, reducing collisions by up to 66.7% in real-world trials.
AI.motivation: Internet-scale egocentric videos are abundant but lack obstacle-aware trajectories conditioned on explicit navigation goals, making them unusable for direct policy learning.
AI.method: VEGA reconstructs local scene geometry from monocular video, samples navigation goals (text/image/spatial waypoints), generates obstacle-aware trajectories using geometry, and trains a flow-matching VLA policy. Geometry is used only during training.
AI.result: On VEGA-Bench: 33.0% fewer collisions, 17.9% better obstacle clearance. In real-world: ≥150.0% higher success, ≥66.7% fewer collisions, ≥60.0% better obstacle clearance vs. strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: Video-derived geometric supervision is a scalable and effective signal for training obstacle-aware navigation VLAs.

title: CEO-Bench: Can Agents Play the Long Game?
summary: Language model agents are becoming proficient executors at isolated, short-horizon tasks such as software engineering and customer service. Yet real-world challenges require a combination of sophisticated skills that remain largely untested in agents: (1) navigating long horizons amid uncertainty; (2) acquiring information in noisy environments; (3) adapting to a changing world; (4) orchestrating multiple moving parts toward a coherent goal. We introduce CEO-Bench, which evaluates these capabilities together by simulating a representative real-world task: operating a startup for 500 days. An agent manages pricing, marketing, budgeting, and many other aspects of a fictional company through a programmable Python interface, operating in the same environment and facing the same challenges as a human CEO. Success demands analyzing noisy, interconnected business databases, translating signals into sound strategy, and coordinating many decisions with programming. The strongest agents write sophisticated code that simulates customer cohorts to forecast future cash and mines negotiation history to uncover hidden customer preferences. Even so, most state-of-the-art models struggle in this environment. Only Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 finish above the $1M starting balance, and neither consistently turns a profit. CEO-Bench takes a first step toward measuring the intelligence required to drive sustained, adaptive progress over time.

AI.tldr: CEO-Bench is a new benchmark simulating 500 days of startup operations to evaluate language model agents on long-horizon, noisy, adaptive, multi-objective decision-making. Even the best models (Claude Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5) barely stay above the initial $1M balance and rarely turn a profit.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks test agents on isolated, short-horizon tasks, but real-world challenges require long-term planning, information gathering under noise, adaptation, and orchestrating multiple subsystems toward a coherent goal.
AI.method: CEO-Bench simulates a startup operating for 500 days via a programmable Python interface. Agents manage pricing, marketing, budgeting, etc., just as a human CEO would, using noisy business databases and negotiation history to make strategic decisions.
AI.result: Most state-of-the-art models struggle; only Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 finish above the $1M starting balance, and neither consistently turns a profit.
AI.conclusion: CEO-Bench provides a first step toward measuring intelligence required for sustained, adaptive progress over time, revealing significant gaps in current agents' capabilities.

title: Reasoning as Intersection: Consensus-Frame Alignment for Visual Focus in Video-MLLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but applying outcome-only rewards to video multimodal large language models (Video-MLLMs) provides limited guidance on which visual evidence should support the answer. Inspired by multisensory integration, where consistent cues can enhance the salience and reliability of perceptual estimates, we introduce Consensus Frame GRPO (CF-GRPO), a temporal-annotation-free process-level reward framework for evidence-aware video reasoning. CF-GRPO constructs a consensus frame prior from intrinsic video cues, including temporal coverage, scene-transition cues, and query-conditioned visual relevance. It then computes a model-side frame-use score from visual and response representations and optimizes their agreement through the Consensus Frame Reward (CFR). With salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening, CFR provides a high-contrast reward signal without requiring human temporal annotations. Experiments show that VideoCFR achieves competitive performance across complex video reasoning benchmarks and improves several metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines, while the consensus prior provides an interpretable view of the evidence frames emphasized during training. The implementation is available at https://github.com/1Pansy/VideoCFR.

AI.tldr: CF-GRPO is a process-level reward framework for video multimodal LLMs that uses consensus frame prior from intrinsic video cues (temporal coverage, scene transitions, visual relevance) to guide evidence-aware reasoning without temporal annotations, achieving competitive results on video reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Outcome-only rewards for Video-MLLMs provide limited guidance on which visual evidence supports answers, lacking temporal-annotation-free process-level supervision for evidence-aware reasoning.
AI.method: CF-GRPO constructs a consensus frame prior from temporal coverage, scene-transition cues, and query-conditioned visual relevance; computes model-side frame-use scores from visual/response representations; optimizes agreement via Consensus Frame Reward (CFR) with salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance on complex video reasoning benchmarks, improves multiple metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines, and yields interpretable evidence frames.
AI.conclusion: The consensus frame prior provides an effective, annotation-free process reward signal for video reasoning, and the approach is open-sourced.

title: Artemis: Anatomy-Resolved inTervention for Eliminating Multimodal NeuroImage confounderS
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging, integrating functional connectivity from fMRI and structural connectivity from DTI, enables non-invasive analysis of brain networks using graph neural networks. However, demographic factors such as age and sex systematically confound the relationship between brain connectivity and clinical outcomes, causing GNNs to exploit spurious shortcuts rather than learning causally invariant representations. While recent causal GNN methods introduce causality at the graph-modeling level, their causal mechanisms remain domain-agnostic without accounting for the real-world confounders inherent in clinical neuroimaging data. Moreover, brain networks are constructed from atlas-based parcellations where each region exhibits distinct sensitivity to demographic factors, necessitating region-aware adjustment. We propose Artemis, a region-level causal framework that bridges this gap with causal intervention at each brain region independently by learning region-specific confounder representations with lightweight parameters. Our adjustment comprehensively utilized the multimodal functional and structural features for graph reasoning as a plug-in module compatible with arbitrary GNN backbones. Experiments on three benchmarks, ADNI for disease diagnosis, OASIS for dementia staging, and HCP for sex classification, demonstrate consistent improvements over representative GNN-based baselines. Multiple supporting experiments further demonstrate statistical significance and neuroscientific interpretability.

AI.tldr: Artemis is a region-level causal framework for multimodal neuroimaging that adjusts for demographic confounders (age, sex) at each brain region independently, improving GNN-based brain network analysis across disease diagnosis, dementia staging, and sex classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Demographic factors like age and sex systematically confound the relationship between brain connectivity and clinical outcomes, causing GNNs to exploit spurious shortcuts. Existing causal GNN methods are domain-agnostic and fail to account for real-world confounders in clinical neuroimaging data, nor do they handle region-specific sensitivity to demographic factors.
AI.method: Artemis performs causal intervention at each brain region independently by learning region-specific confounder representations with lightweight parameters. It serves as a plug-in module that comprehensively utilizes multimodal functional (fMRI) and structural (DTI) features for graph reasoning, compatible with arbitrary GNN backbones.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmarks—ADNI (disease diagnosis), OASIS (dementia staging), and HCP (sex classification)—show consistent improvements over representative GNN-based baselines. Additional experiments demonstrate statistical significance and neuroscientific interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Artemis effectively bridges the gap between causal graph modeling and real-world neuroimaging confounders through region-aware causal intervention, achieving robust and interpretable improvements in multimodal brain network analysis.

title: Reconstruction Limits for Repeated Differentially Private Aggregates: A Cramer-Rao Perspective on Query Geometry
summary: Repeated differentially private (DP) releases are often evaluated by transcript length or cumulative privacy accounting. We show that these quantities do not by themselves determine local reconstruction risk. For Gaussian-calibrated repeated statistical queries, the key object is the nuisance-profiled Fisher geometry of the release sequence: repetition helps only when new releases create identifiable directions after nuisance variables are removed. Thus, release geometry determines what can be locally identified, while the privacy accountant determines how precisely those directions can be estimated. We develop this principle in two settings. For labeled-target reconstruction with fixed-background IN/OUT averages, repeated copies collapse to a single target-versus-background contrast. The best linear unbiased estimator attains the Cramer-Rao bound, and additional copies provide only averaging gain; under Basic Composition this gain is dominated by the $Θ(L^2\log L)$ noise penalty, whereas zCDP/RDP-style Gaussian accounting makes the risk order-flat. For static permutation-invariant releases, labels remain unidentified, but feature diversity can make the sorted participating multiset locally identifiable. For polynomial moments and smooth thresholds, the useful number of releases is governed by the balance between newly exposed eigendirections and accountant-induced noise growth. These results provide a local, mechanism-specific benchmark for value leakage in repeated private sensing and analytics.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that local reconstruction risk in repeated differentially private releases depends not just on privacy accounting but on the Fisher geometry of the release sequence—repetition helps only when new releases create identifiable directions after removing nuisance variables.
AI.motivation: To understand what determines local reconstruction risk in repeated differentially private releases, beyond just transcript length or cumulative privacy accounting.
AI.method: The authors analyze Gaussian-calibrated repeated statistical queries using nuisance-profiled Fisher geometry, studying two settings: labeled-target reconstruction with fixed-background IN/OUT averages, and static permutation-invariant releases.
AI.result: For labeled-target reconstruction, repeated copies collapse to a single contrast; under Basic Composition the noise penalty dominates, while zCDP/RDP accounting makes risk order-flat. For permutation-invariant releases, feature diversity enables local identifiability of the sorted participating multiset, with useful release number governed by the balance between new eigendirections and noise growth.
AI.conclusion: Release geometry determines what can be locally identified, while the privacy accountant determines precision—providing a local, mechanism-specific benchmark for value leakage in repeated private sensing and analytics.

title: Beyond Algorithms: Conceptual Innovation in Medical Imaging AI
summary: Artificial intelligence has driven rapid progress in medical imaging research, producing increasingly sophisticated algorithms and steady improvements on benchmark tasks. However, this algorithm-centric trajectory has also revealed a growing imbalance: while computational methods advance rapidly, the conceptual foundations that define imaging tasks, evaluation metrics, and clinical meaning sometimes remain underexamined. In this Perspective, we distinguish algorithmic innovation, which focuses on improving computational implementations and performance within a fixed problem definition, from conceptual innovation, which reframes what problems are posed, how success is measured, and why an approach is clinically relevant. We argue that prevailing incentive structures, training pathways, and publication norms disproportionately reward algorithmic novelty, particularly for early-career researchers, while at times undervaluing conceptual contributions that are essential for scientific maturation and clinical translation. Through representative examples from medical imaging AI, we show how insufficient conceptual grounding can lead to misaligned objectives, fragile generalization, and limited real-world impact. We conclude with actionable recommendations for researchers, mentors, reviewers, and journals to better recognize, support, and integrate conceptual innovation alongside algorithmic advances.

AI.tldr: This Perspective argues that medical imaging AI research overemphasizes algorithmic innovation (improving methods within fixed problem definitions) while undervaluing conceptual innovation (reframing problems, metrics, and clinical relevance), leading to misaligned objectives and limited real-world impact, and proposes actionable recommendations to rebalance both types of innovation.
AI.motivation: The authors identify a growing imbalance in medical imaging AI: algorithmic methods advance rapidly while the conceptual foundations defining tasks, evaluation metrics, and clinical meaning remain underexamined, leading to misaligned objectives, fragile generalization, and limited real-world clinical impact.
AI.method: The paper distinguishes algorithmic innovation (improving computational implementations within fixed problem definitions) from conceptual innovation (reframing what problems are posed, how success is measured, and why an approach is clinically relevant), then analyzes how prevailing incentive structures, training pathways, and publication norms disproportionately reward algorithmic novelty, supported by representative examples from medical imaging AI.
AI.result: The analysis shows that insufficient conceptual grounding leads to misaligned objectives, fragile generalization, and limited real-world impact. The current system, especially for early-career researchers, disproportionately incentivizes algorithmic novelty at the expense of conceptual contributions essential for scientific maturation and clinical translation.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide actionable recommendations for researchers, mentors, reviewers, and journals to better recognize, support, and integrate conceptual innovation alongside algorithmic advances to achieve more meaningful clinical translation.

title: Point-Cloud-Assistant Localized Statistical Channel Prediction by Tangent Gaussian Splatting
summary: Accurate, site-specific channel information is crucial for optimizing next-generation wireless networks. Among various approaches, localized statistical channel modeling (LSCM), which models the channel multipath angular power spectrum (APS) from the reference signal received power (RSRP) measurement, has emerged as a state-of-the-art method tailored for efficient network optimization. However, despite its effectiveness, LSCM cannot predict APS at the vast majority of locations where no measurements are available, which significantly restricts its applicability in large-scale, real-world scenarios. To address this challenge, we present \emph{point-cloud-assisted tangent Gaussian splatting} (PC-TGS), the first framework to \emph{extrapolate} APS to unmeasured outdoor grids by integrating sparse radio measurements with dense LiDAR-based geometry. PC-TGS represents environmental scatterers as anisotropic 3D Gaussians, initialized and refined through a relaxed-mean reparameterization of the raw point cloud. A tangent-plane projection accurately maps each Gaussian into the local angular domain, while a depth-aware electromagnetic splatting process aggregates their contributions. To ensure practical deployment, we derive a closed-form Gaussian-weighted average (GWA) for APS bin integration and provide a provable error bound. { Evaluations on a LiDAR-scanned city-scale dataset (5M points, 6,310 RSRP samples) demonstrate that PC-TGS achieves better APS and RSRP prediction performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines and faster inference time for APS extrapolation task. These results highlight the potential of PC-TGS to enable geometry-aware and data-efficient channel prediction in large-scale wireless digital twins.

AI.tldr: PC-TGS is a framework that extrapolates angular power spectrum (APS) to unmeasured outdoor locations by combining sparse radio measurements with LiDAR point clouds, using anisotropic 3D Gaussians and tangent-plane projection, achieving superior performance on a city-scale dataset.
AI.motivation: Localized statistical channel modeling (LSCM) cannot predict APS at locations without measurements, limiting its applicability in large-scale real-world scenarios. The goal is to extrapolate APS to unmeasured outdoor grids.
AI.method: PC-TGS represents environmental scatterers as anisotropic 3D Gaussians initialized from LiDAR point clouds via relaxed-mean reparameterization. A tangent-plane projection maps each Gaussian into the local angular domain, and depth-aware electromagnetic splatting aggregates contributions. A closed-form Gaussian-weighted average (GWA) is derived for APS bin integration with a provable error bound.
AI.result: On a city-scale LiDAR dataset (5M points, 6,310 RSRP samples), PC-TGS achieves better APS and RSRP prediction performance than state-of-the-art baselines, with faster inference time for APS extrapolation.
AI.conclusion: PC-TGS enables geometry-aware, data-efficient channel prediction for large-scale wireless digital twins, addressing the extrapolation limitation of prior LSCM methods.

title: N(CO)$^2$: Neural Combinatorial Optimization with Chance Constraints to Solve Stochastic Orienteering
summary: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) offers a promising alternative to traditional heuristic-based methods for solving complex graph optimization problems by proposing to learn heuristics through data. This class of problems frequently arises in automation, as it can be used to model a variety of applications. While NCO has been extensively studied for deterministic combinatorial optimization problems, there are only a few works that aim to solve stochastic combinatorial optimization problems. In this work, we present N(CO)$^2$: Neural Combinatorial Optimization with Chance cOnstraints to solve the Stochastic Orienteering Problem (SOP) without the use of hand-crafted heuristics. By integrating a reinforcement learning (RL) framework, the model optimizes path selection under uncertainty, effectively balancing exploration and exploitation. Empirical results demonstrate that our method generalizes well across diverse SOP instances, achieving competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art mixed-integer linear program (MILP) for the task. The proposed approach reduces human effort in heuristic design while enabling adaptive and efficient decision-making in uncertain environments.

AI.tldr: N(CO)$^2$ is an RL-based neural method for solving the Stochastic Orienteering Problem without hand-crafted heuristics, achieving competitive performance with MILP baselines.
AI.motivation: Most NCO research focuses on deterministic problems, while stochastic combinatorial optimization remains underexplored. The authors aim to solve the Stochastic Orienteering Problem (SOP) without manual heuristic design.
AI.method: The approach integrates a reinforcement learning (RL) framework with chance constraints (N(CO)$^2$) to optimize path selection under uncertainty, balancing exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: The method generalizes well across diverse SOP instances and achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art MILP solvers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach reduces human effort in heuristic design while enabling adaptive and efficient decision-making in uncertain environments.

title: DeFAb: A Verifiable Benchmark for Defeasible Abduction in Foundation Models
comment: 33 pages, 14 figures, 23 tables. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb ; code and evaluation harness: https://github.com/PatrickAllenCooper/blanc
summary: A rule-based logic solver resolves every instance in our benchmark in under 50 microseconds with 100% accuracy; the best frontier language model reaches 65% at best and drops to 23.5% under rendering-robust evaluation (worst case over four surface renderings). We introduce DeFAb (Defeasible Abduction Benchmark), a dataset and generation pipeline that converts four decades of publicly funded knowledge bases into formally grounded instances for defeasible abduction: constructing hypotheses that explain anomalies by overriding defaults while preserving unrelated expectations. Because every hypothesis must pass polynomial-time checks for valid derivation, conservativity, and minimality, DeFAb makes logical rigor the instrument for measuring creativity and theoretical reasoning, scoring the disciplined construction of theory revisions rather than fluent but theory-destroying prose. The pipeline pairs taxonomic hierarchies (OpenCyc, YAGO, Wikidata) with behavioral property graphs (ConceptNet, UMLS) to produce 372,648+ instances across 33.75M materialized rules from 18 sources, in three levels with polynomial-time verifiable gold standards. Four frontier models do not reliably internalize defeasible reasoning: rendering-robust Level 2 accuracy is 7.8-23.5%; chain-of-thought variance (~36 pp) exceeds any inter-model gap; and a matched contamination control isolates a +19.4 pp Level 3 gap. We further release DeFAb-Hard (a 235-instance Level 3 difficulty variant; best model 53.3% vs 100% symbolic) and CONJURE (a kernel-verified transformative-creativity variant of 560 Lean 4/Mathlib instances whose gold answers are definitions the proof kernel did not previously contain, judge-free verifier; a pilot finds zero novel concepts). The same verifier doubles as an exact reward for preference optimization (DPO, RLVR/GRPO). Released under MIT at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb.

AI.tldr: DeFAb is a benchmark for defeasible abduction (explaining anomalies by overriding defaults) with 372,648+ logically grounded instances from 18 knowledge sources. Frontier LLMs perform poorly (7.8-23.5% under robust evaluation), while a symbolic solver achieves 100% accuracy in microseconds. Also releases DeFAb-Hard and CONJURE (Lean 4/Mathlib creativity variant).
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether LLMs can perform disciplined theory revision (creative but logically rigorous reasoning) rather than fluent but unsound prose. Existing benchmarks lack formal grounding for defeasible abduction.
AI.method: Converts four decades of public knowledge bases (OpenCyc, YAGO, Wikidata, ConceptNet, UMLS) into a pipeline generating 372,648+ instances with polynomial-time verifiable gold standards. Has three difficulty levels. Also includes CONJURE for kernel-verified transformative creativity in Lean 4.
AI.result: A symbolic rule-based solver achieves 100% accuracy under 50μs. Frontier LLMs: rendering-robust Level 2 accuracy 7.8-23.5%; chain-of-thought variance (~36 pp) exceeds inter-model gaps; contamination control reveals +19.4 pp Level 3 gap. Best model on DeFAb-Hard: 53.3% vs 100% symbolic. CONJURE pilot found zero novel concepts from LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier LLMs do not reliably internalize defeasible reasoning. Logically rigorous benchmarks are necessary to measure true theoretical reasoning and creativity. DPO/RLVR/GRPO can use the same verifier as exact reward.

title: Domain Generalizable Adaptation of 3D Vision-Language Models via Regularized Fine-Tuning
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Domain adaptation remains a central challenge in 3D vision, especially for multimodal foundation models that align 3D point clouds with visual and textual data. While these models demonstrate strong general capabilities, adapting them to downstream domains with limited data often leads to overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we introduce ReFine3D, a regularized fine-tuning framework designed for domain-generalizable tuning of 3D large multimodal models (LMMs). ReFine3D combines selective layer tuning with two targeted regularization strategies: multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds and text diversity through synonym-based prompts generated by large language models. Additionally, we incorporate point-rendered vision supervision and a test-time augmentation mechanism with confidence-based aggregation to further enhance robustness. Extensive experiments across different 3D domain generalization benchmarks show that ReFine3D improves base-to-novel class generalization by 1.36%, cross-dataset transfer by 2.43%, robustness to corruption by 1.80%, and few-shot accuracy by up to 3.11%, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods with minimal added computational overhead.

AI.tldr: ReFine3D is a regularized fine-tuning framework for 3D multimodal models that mitigates overfitting and catastrophic forgetting via selective layer tuning, multi-view consistency, text diversity from LLM-generated synonyms, point-rendered vision supervision, and test-time augmentation, achieving SOTA results across multiple 3D domain generalization benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Domain adaptation is challenging for 3D multimodal foundation models; adapting them to downstream domains with limited data causes overfitting and catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: ReFine3D combines selective layer tuning with multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds, synonym-based text diversity via LLMs, point-rendered vision supervision, and test-time augmentation with confidence-based aggregation.
AI.result: Improves base-to-novel class generalization by 1.36%, cross-dataset transfer by 2.43%, robustness to corruption by 1.80%, and few-shot accuracy by up to 3.11% over prior SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: ReFine3D effectively enhances domain generalization for 3D large multimodal models with minimal added computational overhead.

title: A Link between Shock-wave Theory and Symmetry-reduced Stochastic Gradient Descent for Artificial Neural Networks
comment: Accepted to the 35th International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks (ICANN) 2026
summary: We develop a mathematically explicit link between shock-wave theory and the symmetry-quotiented learning dynamics of stochastic gradient descent, drawing on differential geometry, Lie group theory, and fluid mechanics. Specifically, after quotienting parameter symmetries and applying local-entropy coarse-graining, the effective dynamics satisfy a viscous Hamilton--Jacobi equation on the quotient manifold. Moreover, under the assumption that the raw parameter dynamics can be summarized by a gradient field on the quotiented space, the gradient of the coarse-grained loss function obeys a Burgers-type equation, and shock formation can be established rigorously. We apply our theory to multilayer perceptrons, convolutional neural networks, Transformers, and mean-field networks, and show that they obey the Hamilton--Jacobi or Burgers-type equations. We conjecture that this framework also yields practical diagnostics for deep learning. In architectures such as Transformers, raw parameter norms are often distorted by symmetry redundancy and may therefore be misleading, whereas symmetry-corrected quotient observables provide a principled basis for monitoring, forecasting, and controlling training-phase transitions.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a mathematical connection between shock-wave theory and SGD learning dynamics, showing that after quotienting symmetries and coarse-graining, the effective dynamics follow viscous Hamilton-Jacobi and Burgers-type equations, with applications to MLPs, CNNs, Transformers, and mean-field networks.
AI.motivation: Parameter symmetries in deep learning distort raw parameter norms (especially in Transformers), making training dynamics difficult to monitor and predict; the authors seek a principled mathematical framework to remove symmetry redundancy and reveal the true underlying dynamics.
AI.method: The authors apply differential geometry, Lie group theory, and fluid mechanics to quotient parameter symmetries, then use local-entropy coarse-graining to derive effective dynamics on the quotient manifold, yielding viscous Hamilton-Jacobi and Burgers-type equations.
AI.result: They show that MLPs, CNNs, Transformers, and mean-field networks obey the derived Hamilton-Jacobi or Burgers-type equations, establishing shock formation rigorously under gradient field assumptions on the quotiented space.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry-corrected quotient observables offer a principled basis for monitoring, forecasting, and controlling training-phase transitions, potentially yielding practical diagnostics for deep learning.

title: Secret key-distribution over networks with node-based adversarial errors
summary: We study the multiple key-cast problem in network coding under active node-based adversaries. In multiple key-cast, a source generates independent secret keys to be securely and reliably delivered to designated terminal subsets. The network adversary can observe \(\ell_o\) nodes, inject additive or overwrite errors into \(\ell_e\) nodes, and simultaneously observe and corrupt \(\ell_{oe}\) nodes, while having full knowledge of the topology and coding operations. Adversarial models of similar nature, however, where corruption and eavesdropping is done on edges instead of nodes, have seen previous studies in the context of secure multicast network-coding. The work at hand builds on and extends these studies to address the challenges in node-based adversaries in the context of (multiple) key distribution. For single-source networks where every node is d-vertex connected from the source, we show that perfectly secure multiple key-cast under additive and overwrite error models is asymptotically achievable at the key-capacity of \(d-\ell_o-\ell_e-2\ell_{oe}\). We then extend our analysis to networks where only terminal nodes satisfy this connectivity requirement, while intermediate nodes may be only partially connected. For these topologies, we develop coding schemes that achieve secure and reliable multiple key-cast capacities determined by the source vertex-connectivity and additional structural properties of the network. Finally, we show that our results generalize to multi-source settings, ensuring perfect secrecy even if the adversary observes all but one source node, and establish that our constructions apply directly to secure multicast network coding and to network secret-sharing scenarios. As part of our studies, we improve the security guarantee of a central scheme in [Zhang et al., IEEE Trans. Comm., 2023] addressing parallel-edge networks, from weak-security to perfect-security.

AI.tldr: This paper studies secure multiple key-cast in network coding under active node-based adversaries, establishing achievable key-capacity bounds and coding schemes under various adversarial models and network connectivity conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of secure key distribution in network coding under node-based (rather than edge-based) adversaries who can observe, inject errors, and corrupt nodes, extending prior work on secure multicast network coding.
AI.method: The paper analyzes node-vertex connectivity requirements, develops coding schemes for single-source networks with d-vertex connectivity, extends to partially connected intermediate node topologies, and generalizes to multi-source settings. It also improves an existing scheme from weak-security to perfect-security.
AI.result: Achieves perfectly secure multiple key-cast at key-capacity of d - ℓ_o - ℓ_e - 2ℓ_oe for d-vertex connected networks. Extends capacity results to partially connected networks and multi-source settings.
AI.conclusion: The results generalize to secure multicast network coding and network secret-sharing, with improvements over prior work in security guarantees.

title: Technical Report for ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Leveraging DINOv3 for Robust Outdoor Scene Understanding in Field Robotics
summary: The GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Field Robotics evaluates dense semantic segmentation of off-road imagery over a fine-grained taxonomy of 64 classes and 11 evaluated non-void coarse categories. We present the first-place solution to this challenge. Our solution comprises two complementary improvements: (a) a network-level design that combines a self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, a ViT-Adapter, and a Mask2Former mask-classification decoder, together with a coarse-category auxiliary loss on the global [CLS] token; and (b) an inference-time aggregation strategy based on multi-scale and horizontal-flip test-time augmentation and an ensemble of the top three checkpoints selected using Codabench scores. Our method achieves an official composite score of 76.57%, consisting of 69.32% fine-class mIoU and 83.81% category-level mIoU, and ranks first on the final phase leaderboard: www.codabench.org/competitions/14257/#/results-tab.

AI.tldr: A first-place solution for GOOSE 2D semantic segmentation challenge using a DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, ViT-Adapter, Mask2Former decoder, auxiliary coarse loss, and inference-time ensemble (multi-scale TTA + top-3 checkpoints), achieving 76.57% composite score.
AI.motivation: To address the difficult problem of fine-grained semantic segmentation of off-road imagery (64 classes, 11 coarse categories) in the GOOSE challenge, requiring both fine-grained discrimination and coarse-level accuracy.
AI.method: (a) Network: self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone + ViT-Adapter + Mask2Former decoder + coarse-category auxiliary loss on [CLS] token; (b) Inference: multi-scale & horizontal-flip TTA + ensemble of top-3 checkpoints selected by Codabench scores.
AI.result: Official composite score 76.57% (69.32% fine-class mIoU, 83.81% category-level mIoU), ranked 1st on final leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Combining a strong self-supervised ViT backbone with adapter-based fine-tuning, mask-classification decoding, auxiliary coarse supervision, and careful inference-time aggregation yields state-of-the-art results for fine-grained off-road semantic segmentation.

title: EH-FedSAG: Variance-Reduced Federated Learning with Energy-Aware Participation in Energy-Harvesting IoT
summary: Federated learning (FL) in energy-harvesting (EH) networks is challenged by intermittent and stochastic energy arrivals that lead to unstable device participation across training rounds, and by high communication costs under limited energy budgets, reducing overall training efficiency. This paper studies FL under a slot-based EH model and proposes EH-FedSAG, a server-memory-based variance-reduced method. We compare EH-FedSAG with vanilla EH-FedAvg under the same multi-channel orthogonal multiple-access uplink model and within a unified simulation framework that captures battery charging, local computation cost, and transmission cost under different energy-arrival probabilities. Performance is assessed in terms of test accuracy over training rounds for both homogeneous and heterogeneous data distributions. The results show that EH-FedSAG consistently achieves higher test accuracy than EH-FedAvg in the considered settings, while exhibiting substantially lower training variance. The advantage of EH-FedSAG is more pronounced under scarce energy availability and non-independent/identically-distributed data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes EH-FedSAG, a variance-reduced federated learning method for energy-harvesting networks that outperforms EH-FedAvg in test accuracy and training stability, especially under scarce energy and non-i.i.d. data.
AI.motivation: Federated learning in energy-harvesting networks suffers from unstable device participation due to intermittent energy arrivals and high communication costs under limited energy budgets, which reduce training efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose EH-FedSAG, a server-memory-based variance-reduced method under a slot-based EH model, and compare it with vanilla EH-FedAvg under a multi-channel orthogonal multiple-access uplink model with a unified simulation framework capturing battery charging, local computation, and transmission costs.
AI.result: EH-FedSAG consistently achieves higher test accuracy and substantially lower training variance than EH-FedAvg, with more pronounced advantages under scarce energy availability and non-i.i.d. data distributions.
AI.conclusion: EH-FedSAG is an effective approach for improving FL performance in energy-harvesting networks, particularly under challenging conditions of limited energy and heterogeneous data.

title: Task Allocation and Motion Planning in Dynamic, Cluttered Environments via CBBA and Graphs of Convex Sets
comment: 15 pages single column, 10 figures, AIAA-Scitech 2027 Submission
summary: Multi-agent task planning in cluttered, dynamic environments requires assigning tasks to agents while simultaneously determining safe, time-efficient trajectories through the environment. When tasks are dynamic, such as rendezvous objectives, allocation decisions depend not only on which agent is best suited for a task, but also on when and where that task can be reached. This paper presents a solution to this problem, which combines Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) for trajectory optimization with the Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) for distributed task allocation. In our approach, GCS finds optimal trajectories through dynamic environments using a time-extended (3D+time) configuration space. At the same time, CBBA coordinates task assignments across agents, enabling informed decision-making in a moving environment. We then connect allocation and planning to allow the agents to avoid collisions in the 3D+time configuration space and provide accurate time estimates for task completion. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in simulated cluttered environments with static and dynamic tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper combines Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) for trajectory optimization with Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) for distributed task allocation to solve multi-agent task planning in dynamic, cluttered environments.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent task planning in cluttered, dynamic environments requires simultaneously assigning tasks to agents and determining safe, time-efficient trajectories, especially when tasks are dynamic (e.g., rendezvous objectives).
AI.method: The approach integrates GCS (for optimal trajectory planning in 3D+time space) with CBBA (for distributed task coordination), connecting allocation and planning to enable collision avoidance and accurate time estimates.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness in simulated cluttered environments with both static and dynamic tasks.
AI.conclusion: The combined GCS-CBBA approach enables informed decision-making, collision avoidance, and accurate task completion time estimation in moving environments.

title: Optimizing Lithium Production Decisions under Geological, Demand, and Pricing Uncertainties: A POMDP Framework for Multi-Objective Decision Making
summary: Decision making in lithium production is challenging, whether from an investor's perspective or a strategic production standpoint. Determining which mines to open and when to open them involves not only geological and price uncertainties, but also complexities around the choice of extraction method, from direct lithium extraction to hard rock mining. Prior work explored models of this problem and different methods to optimize mining decisions; these models did not account for uncertainty in pricing, uncertainty in demand, or different mining technologies to extract lithium. Incorporating different pricing models and extraction technology into these models enables more robust strategies for determining not only when and where to open a mine, but also which method of production to pursue. We frame the problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and solve using belief state planning methods to get optimal decision making. In our study, we show that POMDP solvers outperform human inspired heuristics by dynamically adapting to shifting lithium price regimes (static, linear, exponential, and stochastic) through belief state planning and explicit uncertainty management. By optimally sequencing exploration, production, and technology choice, the framework achieves higher demand fulfillment and more balanced economic environmental outcomes over the projects lifetime in all different pricing and deposit scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper frames lithium mining decisions as a POMDP with belief state planning, incorporating price uncertainty, demand uncertainty, and multiple extraction technologies. It shows POMDP solvers outperform human heuristics across pricing regimes, achieving better demand fulfillment and balanced economic-environmental outcomes.
AI.motivation: Lithium production decisions are complex due to geological, price, and demand uncertainties plus choice of extraction methods. Prior models lacked pricing uncertainty, demand uncertainty, and technology differentiation, leading to suboptimal strategies.
AI.method: The problem is framed as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and solved using belief state planning methods to optimize mine opening timing, location, and extraction technology selection.
AI.result: POMDP solvers outperform human-inspired heuristics by dynamically adapting to shifting lithium price regimes (static, linear, exponential, stochastic). The framework achieves higher demand fulfillment and more balanced economic-environmental outcomes across all pricing and deposit scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating pricing models and extraction technologies into mining decision models enables robust strategies for when/where to open a mine and which production method to pursue, with explicit uncertainty management via belief state planning.

title: Data-Forcing Distillation: Restoring Diversity and Fidelity in Few-Step Video Generation
summary: Recent progress has shown promise in distilling multi-step video diffusion models into efficient few-step students. Among them, Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and its successor DMD2 achieved strong generation quality and fast convergence. However, due to the nature of the reverse Kullback--Leibler (KL) objective, these methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: a substantial drop in sample diversity, and visibly over-saturated outputs that deviate from real-video appearance. In this work, we propose Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD), a simple post-training framework that restores diversity and fidelity in DMD with only a single-line of code change. At its core is the teacher score discrepancy to guide the student toward the real-data distribution, pulling it to missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from problematic modes absent in real data (avoiding over-saturation). We provide an in-depth theoretical analysis of our framework and validate our approach on text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive video generation. With only 100--300 steps of finetuning, DFD effectively restores diversity and fidelity on both Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B model, resolving the over-saturation artifacts with significantly better video dynamics and appearance, and even outperforms the teacher model.

AI.tldr: Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD) is a simple post-training framework that fixes diversity loss and over-saturation in DMD-based video distillation by using a teacher score discrepancy to guide the student toward real-data distribution, requiring only one line of code change and 100-300 finetuning steps.
AI.motivation: Existing DMD/DMD2 methods suffer from mode collapse (reduced sample diversity) and over-saturated outputs that deviate from realistic video appearance due to the reverse KL objective.
AI.method: DFD introduces a teacher score discrepancy term that guides the student model toward the real-data distribution, pulling it toward missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from over-saturated problematic modes.
AI.result: Validated on text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive generation using Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B models; restores diversity and fidelity with only 100-300 finetuning steps, resolves over-saturation, improves video dynamics and appearance, even outperforms the teacher.
AI.conclusion: DFD is a remarkably simple (one-line code change) yet effective fix for key failure modes in video distillation, achieving better-than-teacher quality.

title: Attribution-Guided and Coverage-Maximized Pruning for Structural MoE Compression
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale compute efficiently, yet remain expensive to deploy due to their substantial memory footprint and inference overhead. Prior compression methods mainly operate at the expert level, either removing entire experts or ranking experts by coarse-grained importance scores. However, such expert-wise decisions are often too coarse to capture fine-grained redundancy, leading to misallocated pruning budgets and limited compression. To address this problem, we observe that information within MoE experts is highly concentrated in a small subset of channels, leaving substantial redundancy even in experts deemed important. Based on this observation, we propose a structural pruning framework tailored for MoE models. Our method reformulates prune-ratio allocation as a channel-score coverage maximization problem and solves it efficiently using an attribution-based approximation. Experiments on DeepSeek and Qwen MoE models show that our method preserves model accuracy under 50% or 25% structured pruning when combined with 4-bit quantization. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, our approach reduces memory footprint by 5.27$\times$ and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a structural pruning framework for MoE models that compresses channels (not experts) using attribution-based channel-score coverage maximization, achieving 5.27× memory reduction with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: MoE models are expensive to deploy due to large memory and inference cost. Existing expert-level compression methods are too coarse-grained and fail to capture fine-grained channel-level redundancy.
AI.method: Reformulates prune-ratio allocation as a channel-score coverage maximization problem, solved via attribution-based approximation. Prunes channels structurally within experts rather than removing entire experts.
AI.result: Preserves accuracy under 50-25% structured pruning with 4-bit quantization. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, achieves 5.27× memory reduction and outperforms SOTA baselines across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Channel-level structural pruning is more effective than coarse expert-level pruning for compressing MoE models, capturing fine-grained redundancy while maintaining performance.

title: Intrinsic 4D Gaussian Segmentation from Scene Cues
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Includes supplementary material. Preprint
summary: Dynamic 4D Gaussian Splatting reconstructs deforming scenes with high fidelity and is increasingly adopted as a representation for dynamic 3D scenes. Putting such a scene to use, for editing, manipulation or motion analysis, first requires segmenting it: grouping the Gaussian primitives into coherent objects. Current pipelines obtain this grouping by importing 2D masks from foundation models such as SAM and lifting or distilling them into the Gaussian representation. In dynamic scenes these masks must be generated across many frames and views, which is costly, and the resulting segmentation can depend strongly on the quality and consistency of those external masks. We ask how much object-level structure can instead be recovered from the Gaussians themselves, and propose Intrinsic-GS, a training-free, mask-free method that builds a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives from appearance, orientation, scale, deformation-trajectory and non-learned rendered-boundary cues. The graph is partitioned with Leiden community detection, requiring no foundation model and no learned feature field. On the standard 4D Gaussian segmentation benchmarks, Neu3D and HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS recovers substantial object structure without mask supervision, reaching 0.746 mIoU on Neu3D and 0.575 on HyperNeRF; on Neu3D, a geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU, matching SAM-supervised TRASE. On HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS runs 12.5x faster than the mask-generation and feature-rendering stages used by mask-supervised pipelines. These results suggest that much of the segmentation signal is already encoded in the Gaussians themselves, offering a fast, mask-free direction for 3D and 4D Gaussian segmentation that may also point toward more generalizable, robust segmentation in settings where external masks are unreliable or expensive.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic-GS is a training-free, mask-free method that segments dynamic 4D Gaussian scenes by building a sparse affinity graph from intrinsic Gaussian cues (appearance, geometry, deformation) and applying Leiden community detection, achieving competitive results (0.746 mIoU on Neu3D, 0.575 on HyperNeRF) while running 12.5x faster than mask-supervised pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current dynamic 4D Gaussian segmentation pipelines rely on importing 2D masks from foundation models like SAM across many frames and views, which is costly and quality-dependent. The authors ask how much object-level structure can be recovered from the Gaussians themselves without external supervision.
AI.method: Intrinsic-GS builds a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives using cues from appearance, orientation, scale, deformation-trajectory, and non-learned rendered-boundary cues, then partitions it with Leiden community detection — no foundation model or learned feature field required.
AI.result: Achieves 0.746 mIoU on Neu3D and 0.575 on HyperNeRF without mask supervision; a geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU on Neu3D, matching SAM-supervised TRASE; runs 12.5x faster than mask-generation and feature-rendering stages of supervised pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Much of the segmentation signal is already encoded in the Gaussians themselves, offering a fast, mask-free direction for 3D/4D Gaussian segmentation that may generalize better where external masks are unreliable or expensive.

title: Rotatable Antenna-Enhanced Secure Integrated Sensing and Communications Under Imperfect CSI
summary: A rotatable antenna (RA)-enhanced secure integrated sensing and communications system is investigated, where an RA-based transceiver simultaneously communicates with legitimate users and senses a target that is regarded as a potential eavesdropper. Under imperfect eavesdropping channel state information (CSI), a max-min data rate optimization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the transmit beamforming, artificial noise (AN) covariance matrix, and transmit/receive boresights of RAs, subject to the maximum information leakage and minimum sensing power constraints. To address the highly non-convex problem, the information leakage and sensing power constraints are transformed into convex ones via S-Procedure method and Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, respectively. Subsequently, an alternating optimization algorithm is developed to decompose the reformulated problem into two subproblems. In particular, the transmit beamforming and AN covariance matrix are optimized by utilizing successive convex approximation and semi-definite relaxation methods, while the RA boresights are obtained by invoking the particle swarm optimization. Simulation results show that the RA-based scheme significantly outperforms the benchmarks, and offers enhanced robustness against imperfect CSI with the increase of the maximum rotation range.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an RA-enhanced secure ISAC system where an RA-based transceiver communicates with users while sensing a target (potential eavesdropper). Under imperfect CSI, a max-min data rate optimization problem is solved via alternating optimization, S-Procedure, Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, SCA, SDR, and PSO methods. Results show RA significantly outperforms benchmarks with enhanced robustness against imperfect CSI.
AI.motivation: To enhance physical layer security in integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) systems by leveraging rotatable antennas (RAs) to improve both communication secrecy and sensing performance, especially under imperfect eavesdropping channel state information.
AI.method: Formulates a max-min data rate optimization problem jointly optimizing transmit beamforming, artificial noise (AN) covariance, and RA boresights. Transforms non-convex constraints using S-Procedure and Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. Solves via alternating optimization with SCA, SDR, and particle swarm optimization (PSO).
AI.result: The RA-based scheme significantly outperforms benchmark systems and offers enhanced robustness against imperfect CSI, particularly as the maximum rotation range of RAs increases.
AI.conclusion: Rotatable antennas provide substantial performance gains for secure ISAC systems, with improved robustness to channel estimation errors.

title: As You Wish: Mission Planning with Formal Verification using LLMs in Precision Agriculture
summary: Though robotic systems are now being commercialized and deployed in various industries, many of these systems are highly specialized and often require an advanced skill set to operate and ensure they perform as instructed. To mitigate this problem, we recently introduced a mission planner leveraging LLMs to synthesize mission plans in precision agriculture based on mission descriptions provided in natural language. While the system demonstrates impressive performance, it also suffers from the inherent ambiguities of natural language. In this paper, we extend our system to address this issue by introducing multiple feedback loops in the planning architecture that leverage linear temporal logic (LTL) to ensure the mission planning system meets the specifications formulated by the user while still using natural language. To mitigate potential bias, this is achieved by using two different commercial LLMs in charge of the specification and verification subtasks. Through extensive experiments, we highlight the strengths and limitations of integrating mission verification into a fully autonomous pipeline, particularly regarding an LLM's ability to generate valuable LTL formulas, and show how our proposed implementation addresses and solves these challenges.

AI.tldr: This paper extends an LLM-based mission planner for precision agriculture by adding multiple feedback loops using linear temporal logic (LTL) to resolve natural language ambiguities, employing two separate commercial LLMs for specification and verification to reduce bias.
AI.motivation: Robotic systems are often highly specialized and require advanced skills to operate; natural language-based mission planning suffers from inherent ambiguities that need to be addressed.
AI.method: The authors introduce multiple feedback loops leveraging linear temporal logic (LTL) into the planning architecture, using two different commercial LLMs—one for specification and one for verification—to mitigate bias and ensure user specifications are met.
AI.result: Extensive experiments highlight strengths and limitations of integrating mission verification into a fully autonomous pipeline, particularly regarding LLMs' ability to generate valuable LTL formulas, and show how the proposed implementation addresses these challenges.
AI.conclusion: The proposed feedback-loop architecture with dual-LLM specification and verification effectively addresses natural language ambiguities in mission planning for precision agriculture, though limitations remain in LLM-generated LTL formula quality.

title: ForecastBench-Sim: A Simulated-World Forecasting Benchmark
comment: 15 pages, 5 main figures, 6 appendix figures. Spotlight presentation at Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence / Workshop on AI Forecasting, ICML 2026
summary: Forecasting benchmarks for general-purpose AI systems usually inherit the constraints of the real world: outcomes resolve slowly, tail events are rare, and counterfactual questions are difficult to score. We introduce ForecastBench-Sim, a simulated-world forecasting benchmark built on game rollouts from Freeciv, a turn-based strategy game modelled on the Civilization series. Forecasters receive a fixed world report (a structured snapshot of the current game state) and answer questions about hidden future states; the benchmark then continues the simulation and scores forecasts. Because the world is simulated, the same setup can generate continuous or binary forecasting questions at arbitrary time horizons, paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal questions, and resolved examples of rare or disruptive outcomes. We describe the benchmark pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, and release artifacts, and report validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot. ForecastBench-Sim is intended to complement real-world forecasting benchmarks by providing controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ForecastBench-Sim, a simulated-world forecasting benchmark based on Freeciv game rollouts, designed to provide controlled, immediately resolvable forecasting tasks that complement real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world forecasting benchmarks suffer from slow outcome resolution, rare tail events, and difficulty scoring counterfactual questions. A simulated environment can overcome these limitations.
AI.method: The benchmark uses Freeciv (a turn-based strategy game) to generate game rollouts. Forecasters receive structured world reports and answer questions about hidden future states; the simulation continues and forecasts are scored. It supports continuous/binary questions, intervention worlds for causal queries, and rare event examples.
AI.result: The paper describes the benchmark pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, validation slices from model evaluations, and an anonymized human pilot.
AI.conclusion: ForecastBench-Sim is intended to complement real-world forecasting benchmarks by providing controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

title: Vines-DB: An RGB image dataset for multi-species ornamental vine segmentation
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure. Source data repository: OSF (DOI: 10.17605/OSF.IO/YJHCK)
summary: The Vines-DB dataset contains 1,218 original high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species collected under field conditions at the Utah Agricultural Experiment Station's Greenville Research Farm in Logan, Utah, USA. The dataset was generated from 168 individual vine plants that were transplanted in 2022 and photographed repeatedly across multiple months during the 2023 and 2024 growing seasons (July-October). Images were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro equipped with a 48 MP camera between 10:00 AM and 12:00 PM under daylight. Vines were grown on 1.2m x 2.4m trellises and photographed from a distance of 1m against black or white Styrofoam backdrops to improve contrast and reduce background noise. The dataset includes Akebia quinata, Campsis radicans, Hydrangea anomala petiolaris, Lonicera x heckrottii, Campsis x tagliabuana 'Madame Galen', Parthenocissus quinquefolia, and Wisteria floribunda. All original images were manually annotated in Roboflow by trained annotators to produce polygon-based instance segmentation masks for eight classes, including seven species and background. After preprocessing and data augmentation, the working dataset was expanded to 2,307 images for model development and evaluation. The augmented dataset was divided into 2,019 training images, 192 validation images, and 96 test images using stratified sampling to maintain balanced representation. Vines-DB supports the development and evaluation of deep learning models for multi-class instance segmentation in precision horticulture and urban ecology. The dataset enables applications such as automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping. In addition, repeated monthly imaging of the plants captures temporal variation in canopy development and plant appearance, increasing the dataset's utility for segmentation benchmarking under realistic field conditions.

AI.tldr: The Vines-DB dataset is a collection of 1,218 high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species captured under field conditions, manually annotated for instance segmentation, augmented to 2,307 images, and split into training/validation/test sets to support deep learning models for precision horticulture and urban ecology applications.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of publicly available, high-quality annotated datasets for ornamental vine species under realistic field conditions, enabling automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping in precision horticulture and urban ecology.
AI.method: 1,218 original RGB images of 168 vine plants (7 species) were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro (48 MP) under daylight, using black/white Styrofoam backdrops. Images were manually annotated in Roboflow to create polygon-based instance segmentation masks. Data augmentation expanded the dataset to 2,307 images, which were split via stratified sampling into 2,019 training, 192 validation, and 96 test images.
AI.result: A dataset of 2,307 augmented images with polygon-based instance segmentation masks covering 7 vine species plus background, with balanced stratified splits for model development and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Vines-DB provides a valuable resource for developing and benchmarking multi-class instance segmentation models under realistic field conditions, capturing temporal variation in canopy development and supporting applications in precision horticulture and urban ecology.

title: Fisher Width: A Geometric Measure of Complexity on Statistical Manifolds
summary:
Gaussian width is a central geometric complexity measure in high-dimensional probability, compressed sensing, convex optimization, and learning theory. It quantifies the average extent of a set along random directions, thereby capturing the effective dimension of constraint sets, hypothesis classes, and descent cones. However, this notion is intrinsically Euclidean. Statistical models instead carry a natural Riemannian geometry induced by the Fisher information metric, where directions are scaled according to statistical distinguishability rather than ambient Euclidean length.
  We introduce Fisher width, a Fisher-geometric analogue of Gaussian width for statistical manifolds. At a parameter point $θ$, Fisher width replaces the Euclidean identity by the local metric tensor $G(θ)^{1/2}$, measuring the Gaussian width of the Fisher-rescaled set. This makes the resulting quantity sensitive to local statistical curvature and invariant under smooth reparameterizations.
  We develop the basic theory of Fisher width, showing that it retains key structural features of Gaussian width, including concentration, metric perturbation stability, and spectral comparison bounds with the Euclidean baseline, while also capturing anisotropic geometric effects invisible to Euclidean measures. As an application, we prove a generalization bound for Fisher-Lipschitz hypothesis classes and propose computable estimators, which we evaluate empirically on MNIST across three model classes.
  Fisher width is to statistical manifolds what Gaussian width is to Euclidean convex bodies. This work lays the foundation for studying complexity and learning on curved statistical manifolds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Fisher width, a Riemannian generalization of Gaussian width for statistical manifolds, which replaces the Euclidean metric with the Fisher information metric to capture local statistical curvature and reparameterization invariance. The authors develop its theory (concentration, stability, spectral bounds) and apply it to derive generalization bounds for Fisher-Lipschitz hypothesis classes, with empirical validation on MNIST.
AI.motivation: Gaussian width is a fundamental complexity measure but is intrinsically Euclidean. Statistical models have a natural Riemannian geometry (Fisher information metric) where directions are scaled by statistical distinguishability, not Euclidean length. The authors aim to bridge this gap by introducing a Fisher-geometric analogue.
AI.method: Fisher width is defined by rescaling sets with the local metric tensor G(θ)^{1/2} (the Fisher information matrix square root) instead of the Euclidean identity, making it sensitive to local statistical curvature and invariant under smooth reparameterizations.
AI.result: Fisher width retains key Gaussian width properties (concentration, metric perturbation stability, spectral comparison bounds) while capturing anisotropic geometric effects. It yields generalization bounds for Fisher-Lipschitz classes, with empirical evaluation on MNIST across three model classes.
AI.conclusion: Fisher width is to statistical manifolds what Gaussian width is to Euclidean convex bodies, laying the foundation for studying complexity and learning on curved statistical manifolds.

title: Pushing the Limits: Unlocking the Potential of Faster-than-Nyquist Signaling
summary: Faster-than-Nyquist (FTN) signaling is gaining attention as a smart way to pack more data into limited spectrum by intentionally breaking the traditional symbol-spacing rules. This article takes a fresh look at FTN's potential to boost capacity, examining how performance varies across different acceleration factors and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) definitions. Beyond the theory, we explore what it takes to make FTN work in practice, such as dealing with power amplifier constraints, managing high peak-to-average power, and designing practical coding strategies. We also highlight real-world issues like spectrum sharing, short-packet communication, and receiver complexity. With applications ranging from low-latency links to integrated sensing and satellite systems, FTN offers a compelling path forward for future wireless technologies.

AI.tldr: FTN signaling boosts spectral efficiency by breaking Nyquist spacing rules, with analysis of performance across SNR definitions, practical implementation challenges (PAPR, coding, amplifier constraints), and applications in low-latency, sensing, and satellite systems.
AI.motivation: To address spectrum scarcity by packing more data into limited bandwidth through faster-than-Nyquist signaling, while examining both theoretical capacity gains and practical feasibility.
AI.method: Analyzes FTN performance across different acceleration factors and SNR definitions; explores practical implementation issues including power amplifier constraints, peak-to-average power management, and coding strategies.
AI.result: FTN shows capacity gains under various acceleration factors and SNR conditions; practical challenges (PAPR, receiver complexity, amplifier constraints) are identified and discussed.
AI.conclusion: FTN signaling presents a compelling path for future wireless technologies including low-latency links, integrated sensing, and satellite systems, but requires addressing real-world implementation issues like spectrum sharing and short-packet communication.

title: On-Manifold Variational Learning with Heat-Kernel Priors
summary: Learning unsupervised representations of medical imaging cohorts can reveal clinically meaningful prototypes without expert labels, which are often noisy and fail to capture true pathological heterogeneity. However, existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, producing prototypes that drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as the number of sub-populations grows. We propose a manifold-anchored variational framework built on a geometry-aware Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, whose M-step selects each sub-population prototype as the graph medoid with the highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, ensuring that every prototype remains on-manifold. A Dirichlet energy regularizer enforces geometric smoothness of the latent space, and a per-sub-population uncertainty score enables label-free quality assessment. \rev{The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool that extends standard EM and applies readily to other latent-variable models beyond this setting.} On cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks, our framework attains the highest accuracy among all compared methods, produces the sharpest prototypes reported to date, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where all baselines degenerate.

AI.tldr: A manifold-anchored EM variational framework that learns on-manifold prototypes for unsupervised medical imaging representation, surpassing baselines in accuracy, sharpness, and stability at large sub-population counts.
AI.motivation: Existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, causing prototypes to drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as sub-populations grow. Noisy expert labels also fail to capture true pathological heterogeneity.
AI.method: Proposes a geometry-aware EM algorithm where the M-step selects prototypes as graph medoids with highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, plus a Dirichlet energy regularizer and per-sub-population uncertainty score.
AI.result: On cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks, the framework achieves highest accuracy, sharpest prototypes, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where all baselines degenerate.
AI.conclusion: The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool extending standard EM, applicable to other latent-variable models beyond this setting.

title: Controlled Out-of-Band Device-to-Device Communication in Cellular Networks Using a Backup Channel in Television White Space
comment: Published in: 2023 18th International Conference on Emerging Technologies (ICET)
summary: In this article, we address the problem of spectrum scarcity in cellular networks (CNs). We propose a backup channel (BuC) for cellular users (CUs) located in the same macro-cell under the control of a single macro base station (eNB). This BuC operates in television white space and is detected by the CUs through a cognitive radio energy-detection channel-sensing technique with a certain probability of success. When all regular channels with the cellular eNB are occupied, the CUs within the same coverage area of the macro eNB can utilize the sensed BuC to establish a controlled out-of-band device-to-device link for communication. The BuC bypasses the eNB for data communication and reduces the burden on the core of the CN. This leads to improved cellular eNB capacity. In the proposed system model, each CU and eNB is equipped with two antennas for communication in two separate bands, i.e., cellular and TV bands. Simulations show significant reductions in the blocking probability and probability of call delay.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using TV white space as a backup channel (BuC) for cellular users in the same macro-cell, detected via cognitive radio energy-detection sensing. When regular channels are full, CUs can use the BuC to establish out-of-band D2D links, bypassing the eNB to reduce core network burden and improve eNB capacity. Simulations show reduced blocking probability and call delay.
AI.motivation: To address spectrum scarcity in cellular networks by leveraging underutilized TV white space as backup channels, reducing congestion and improving network capacity.
AI.method: A backup channel (BuC) in TV white space is detected by CUs using cognitive radio energy-detection channel sensing. When regular channels are occupied, CUs can use the BuC to establish controlled out-of-band D2D links, bypassing the eNB. Each CU and eNB has dual antennas for cellular and TV bands.
AI.result: Significant reductions in blocking probability and probability of call delay.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BuC scheme effectively alleviates spectrum scarcity, improves eNB capacity, and reduces call blocking and delay in cellular networks.

title: DREAM-Chunk: Reactive Action Chunking with Latent World Model
summary: Action chunking has become a common interface for vision-language-action (VLA) models, enabling low-frequency policy inference to drive high-frequency robot execution. However, once an action chunk is committed, its open-loop execution can be brittle under stochastic dynamics, hardware execution errors, and partial observability. We propose DREAM-Chunk, a test-time scaling method that augments chunking-based policies with a lightweight latent world model, without requiring additional policy fine-tuning. At test time, DREAM-Chunk samples multiple candidate action chunks, rolls out their predicted latent futures, and selects actions from the chunk whose predicted state best matches the observed rollout. In this way, DREAM-Chunk uses additional test-time computation to cover multiple plausible stochastic futures and improve reactivity during long-horizon chunk execution. On the Kinetix benchmark, DREAM-Chunk improves robustness under increasing action noise and benefits from larger candidate sample sizes, especially when demonstrations contain corrective behaviors. We further validate DREAM-Chunk on four manipulation tasks across two robot platforms and two VLA policies under various sources of stochasticity. Across simulation and hardware experiments, DREAM-Chunk improves the robustness of action-chunking policies in stochastic dynamics.

AI.tldr: DREAM-Chunk is a test-time scaling method that uses a lightweight latent world model to sample, simulate, and select the best action chunk, improving robustness of VLA policies under stochastic dynamics without requiring policy fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Action chunking in VLA models suffers from brittle open-loop execution under stochastic dynamics, hardware errors, and partial observability. The authors aim to improve robustness without additional policy fine-tuning.
AI.method: DREAM-Chunk augments chunking-based policies with a lightweight latent world model. At test time, it samples multiple candidate action chunks, rolls out their predicted latent futures, and selects the action chunk whose predicted state best matches the observed rollout.
AI.result: On the Kinetix benchmark, DREAM-Chunk improves robustness under increasing action noise and benefits from larger candidate sample sizes, especially when demonstrations contain corrective behaviors. It is validated on four manipulation tasks across two robot platforms and two VLA policies.
AI.conclusion: DREAM-Chunk improves the robustness of action-chunking policies in stochastic dynamics across simulation and hardware experiments, enabling more reactive long-horizon execution.

title: What Must Generalist Agents Remember?
summary: This paper develops a formal account of what generalist agents must store in memory in order to act near-optimally across multiple environments and goals. It shows that when two domains share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions, any uniformly near-optimal policy must induce distinct memory distributions at that bottleneck. The result yields a separation theorem: sufficiently successful agents cannot rely only on current state observations, but must preserve domain-relevant information in memory. The paper further shows that if an agent's memory contains enough information to estimate values for related goals, then that memory can be used to approximately reconstruct the agent's local transition dynamics. Together, these results characterize memory as the substrate that supports domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning for generalist agents.

AI.tldr: This paper formally proves that generalist agents operating across multiple environments must use memory to store domain-relevant information, especially when different domains share observations but require different actions. It shows memory enables domain disambiguation, transition model reconstruction, and planning.
AI.motivation: To provide a formal account of what information generalist agents must store in memory to act near-optimally across diverse environments and goals, and to prove that memory is necessary when observational bottlenecks create action conflicts.
AI.method: Formal analysis proving a separation theorem showing that uniformly near-optimal policies must induce distinct memory distributions at observational bottlenecks when domains share observations but require incompatible optimal actions. Additional proofs show memory enables value estimation and transition dynamics reconstruction.
AI.result: A separation theorem establishing that sufficiently successful agents cannot rely solely on current state observations but must preserve domain-relevant information in memory. Memory with sufficient value information can approximately reconstruct local transition dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Memory serves as the critical substrate for domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning in generalist agents operating across multiple environments.
