  Together these constitute ten design principles for an agent-first internet, one in which agents are first-class citizens whose integration requires renegotiating the web's foundational social contract across access, economics, and content.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a redesign of the web across three layers (access, economics, content) to accommodate AI agents as legitimate first-class citizens, introducing ten design principles including agent-optimized content, token-based subscriptions, and the Agent Text Markup Language (ATML) to counter epistemic recursion.
AI.motivation: The web was built for human consumers, but AI agents are increasingly acting as intermediaries. Current approaches (blocking, CAPTCHAs, treating agent access as extraction) are inadequate. The paper argues the web's foundational assumption is invalidated and needs principled redesign.
AI.method: Proposes ten design principles across three layers: (1) Access layer: agent identification metadata in HTTP, dual-layer architecture for human and agent content; (2) Economic layer: intent-based tier framework, token-based subscription, commissioned content economy; (3) Content layer: ATML (four-level human supervision tier), cryptographic provenance chain to counter epistemic recursion.
AI.result: A framework of ten design principles for an "agent-first internet" where agents are first-class citizens with legitimate access, economic obligations mirroring human proxies, and content provenance mechanisms to prevent detachment from human ground truth.
AI.conclusion: The web's social contract must be renegotiated across access, economics, and content layers to integrate AI agents legitimately rather than treating them as adversaries.

title: Hallucination Detection and Correction in Medical VLMs via Counter-Evidence Verification
summary: Vision-Language models (VLMs) reliability in medical diagnosis is challenged by trust-undermining hallucinations. Existing hallucination detection approaches mainly focus on identifying factual inconsistencies between generated text and reference data. While some studies analyze where models attend in images, they seldom verify whether such attention truly reflects the visual evidence supporting the generated text. To address this gap, we propose Co}unter-Evidence Verification (CoEV), a training-free plug-and-play framework that detects and corrects hallucinations through evidence-based factual consistency verification. CoEV performs bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, testing whether each statement is supported by its corresponding evidence region, and assigns each statement into a four-quadrant diagnostic map capturing combinations of text factuality and visual grounding. CoEV detects hallucinated content and serves as a post hoc refinement tool, correcting hallucinations without retraining. Extensive experiments on four medical datasets show that CoEV combats hallucinations in VLMs.For hallucination detection, CoEV consistently outperforms existing methods, improving average PR-AUC and ROC-AUC by 3.0% and 3.9% absolute points respectively, with notable gains of up to 18.5% in specific VQA scenarios. For hallucination correction, it improves Micro-F1 by up to 12.5%, reduces hallucination rates by over 11.9% on medical report generation, and also boosts medical VQA accuracy. These results show that CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations, providing clinicians with dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: CoEV is a training-free framework that detects and corrects hallucinations in medical VLMs by performing bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, improving detection by up to 18.5% and correction by up to 12.5%.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination detection approaches focus on factual inconsistencies between text and reference data but rarely verify whether model attention truly reflects visual evidence supporting generated text.
AI.method: CoEV performs bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, assigning statements into a four-quadrant diagnostic map combining text factuality and visual grounding, serving as a training-free plug-and-play framework.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods by 3.0% (PR-AUC) and 3.9% (ROC-AUC) on average, with up to 18.5% improvement in VQA scenarios. For correction, improves Micro-F1 by 12.5%, reduces hallucination rates by 11.9% on medical report generation.
AI.conclusion: CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations, providing clinicians with dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis.

title: From Sparse Features to Trustworthy Proxies: Certifying SAE-Based Interpretability
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are increasingly used to extract interpretable features from language models (LMs), yet a central question remains: when can an SAE-based explanation be treated as a faithful view of an underlying frozen LM We study this through a post-hoc generalization framework that certifies the LM via a sparse proxy, obtained by replacing a native hidden activation with its pretrained SAE reconstruction. Our framework derives an upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four measurable quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity. We interpret this certificate as an operational criterion for explanatory faithfulness. In particular, a non-vacuous bound indicates that the extracted sparse features retain meaningful predictive information, while small reconstruction and mismatch errors indicate that the proxy remains behaviorally close to the original model. Empirically, we show that the bound becomes non-vacuous on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B at practical sample sizes. A detailed layerwise analysis of Llama-3-8B reveals a strong depth dependence, with later layers becoming much easier to certify, associated with both stronger local fidelity and weaker downstream error amplification. Finally, through feature-shuffling ablations, we show that the decomposition distinguishes genuine semantic alignment from mere statistical sparsity, providing a useful diagnostic for when SAE-based explanations become less reliable.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a post-hoc generalization framework to certify when sparse autoencoder (SAE)-based explanations faithfully represent language models, deriving an upper bound on expected risk using four measurable quantities. The framework is validated on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B, revealing depth-dependent faithfulness in Llama-3-8B and providing diagnostics for unreliable SAE explanations.
AI.motivation: To address the central question of when SAE-based explanations can be treated as faithful views of frozen language models, by providing a formal certification framework rather than relying on heuristic evaluation.
AI.method: The paper proposes a post-hoc generalization framework that certifies the LM via a sparse proxy (replacing native hidden activation with SAE reconstruction). It derives an upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity.
AI.result: The bound becomes non-vacuous on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B at practical sample sizes. Layerwise analysis of Llama-3-8B shows strong depth dependence, with later layers easier to certify due to stronger local fidelity and weaker downstream error amplification. Feature-shuffling ablations distinguish genuine semantic alignment from statistical sparsity.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an operational criterion for explanatory faithfulness: a non-vacuous bound indicates meaningful predictive information retention, while small reconstruction and mismatch errors indicate behavioral closeness. It also offers a useful diagnostic for when SAE-based explanations become less reliable.

title: Generating Natural and Expressive Robot Gestures through Iterative Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback using LLMs
summary: Expressive gestures are essential for natural and effective communication, complementing speech when verbal cues alone are insufficient (e.g., pointing). For social robots such as the humanoid Pepper, producing natural and expressive movements is critical for improving human-robot interaction (HRI) and long-term acceptance. However, generating gestures remains challenging due to reliance on expert-authored animations, resulting in rigid behaviors that are impractical for dynamic and diverse environments. Alternatively, machine learning approaches often struggle to capture perceived naturalness, becoming increasingly challenging with more degrees of freedom. Consequently, producing expressive robot gestures requires a system that can adapt to the environment while adhering to social norms and physical constraints. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) enable dynamic code generation, offering new opportunities for runtime gesture synthesis from natural language. In this paper, we integrate ChatGPT into the humanoid robot Pepper to generate co-speech gestures aligned with conversational output. While this baseline enables flexible gesture generation, the resulting motions are often perceived as stiff and unnatural. To address this limitation, we introduce an iterative reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) system that finetunes gesture generation based on user evaluations, leveraging an iterative user study to compare Pepper's generated gestures. Our results show that RLHF improved the LLM's co-speech generative capabilities, producing more expressive, relevant and fluid movements.

AI.tldr: This paper integrates ChatGPT with the humanoid robot Pepper to generate co-speech gestures from natural language, and introduces an iterative RLHF system to improve gesture expressiveness, relevance, and fluidity based on user feedback.
AI.motivation: Expressive gestures are essential for natural human-robot interaction, but generating them remains challenging due to reliance on expert-authored animations (rigid, impractical) and machine learning limitations in capturing naturalness, especially with high degrees of freedom.
AI.method: The authors integrate ChatGPT into the Pepper robot for baseline co-speech gesture generation, then introduce an iterative reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) system that finetunes gestures based on user evaluations through iterative user studies.
AI.result: RLHF improved the LLM's co-speech generative capabilities, producing more expressive, relevant, and fluid movements compared to the baseline LLM-only approach.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs with iterative human feedback (RLHF) is an effective approach for generating more natural and adaptive robot gestures, improving HRI quality and user acceptance.

title: Analysing drivers and interdependencies in European electricity markets using XAI
summary: Electricity markets are inherently complex systems characterised by strong nonlinearities, high-dimensional interactions, and increasing interdependence across regions. While deep neural networks (DNNs) have demonstrated strong predictive capabilities for electricity prices, their lack of interpretability limits their usefulness for understanding the underlying drivers of price formation. This paper addresses this gap by combining DNN models with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to analyse the determinants of electricity prices across 39 European bidding zones. We employ SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to quantify feature contributions and apply and extend SSHAP, an aggregation framework to improve interpretability in high-dimensional settings. The analysis identifies that renewable energy sources, particularly solar, play a disproportionately important role in price formation despite their lower share in total power generation. Gas prices remain a dominant and consistent driver across electricity markets, while interconnections significantly shape price dynamics, highlighting the strong interdependence of European electricity systems. In addition, a synthetic EU-wide electricity market is constructed to explore the counterfactual scenario of a fully integrated market with a single price.

AI.tldr: This paper combines deep neural networks with explainable AI (SHAP) to analyze electricity price determinants across 39 European bidding zones, finding that renewables (especially solar) disproportionately impact prices, gas prices are a consistent driver, interconnections shape price dynamics, and a synthetic EU-wide market with a single price is explored.
AI.motivation: Deep neural networks can predict electricity prices well but lack interpretability, limiting understanding of what drives price formation in complex, interdependent European electricity markets.
AI.method: Combines DNN models with XAI techniques (SHAP for feature contributions, extended SSHAP for high-dimensional interpretability); constructs a synthetic EU-wide market for counterfactual analysis.
AI.result: Renewables (especially solar) play a disproportionately important role in price formation despite lower generation share; gas prices are a dominant consistent driver; interconnections significantly shape price dynamics, showing strong European interdependence.
AI.conclusion: XAI-enhanced DNNs effectively uncover key price drivers in European electricity markets, with implications for renewable integration and market integration policies.

title: SCOPE-FL: A Strategy-proof Chain-based Optimal pareto efficient Federated Learning System
summary: Hierarchical Federated Learning (HFL) enables scalable collaborative model training across distributed devices while preserving data privacy. However, existing HFL client selection mechanisms suffer from a fundamental strategic inefficiency. By prioritizing stability over Pareto efficiency (PE), they produce suboptimal resource allocations, and without strategy proofness (SP), participants are incentivized to misrepresent their true preferences, both failures degrading system overall welfare in the Pareto sense in practice. To address it, we propose SCOPE-FL (Strategy-proof Chain-based Optimal pareto efficient Federated Learning), a synchronous HFL framework that formulates client selection as a two-sided school choice problem solved through the Top Trading Cycle (TTC) algorithm that simultaneously guarantees PE and SP. For reward distribution, SCOPE-FL employs a scalable Shapley value approximation based on One-Round Reconstruction (OR), ensuring compensation proportional to each client's contribution. The entire mechanism executes via blockchain smart contracts, providing the tamper-proof environment required for the SP guarantees to hold in practice. A comprehensive evaluation on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 demonstrates that SCOPE-FL outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, including DA, IAS, and other methods across model accuracy, convergence rate, and reward efficiency, while achieving communication latency comparable to DA and blockchain overhead significantly lower than DA at scale.

AI.tldr: SCOPE-FL is a hierarchical federated learning framework that uses Top Trading Cycle algorithm for strategy-proof and Pareto-efficient client selection, combined with Shapley value-based reward distribution via blockchain smart contracts, outperforming existing methods on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Existing HFL client selection mechanisms suffer from two key failures: they prioritize stability over Pareto efficiency (leading to suboptimal resource allocations) and lack strategy proofness (allowing participants to misrepresent preferences), both degrading overall system welfare.
AI.method: Formulates client selection as a two-sided school choice problem solved via Top Trading Cycle (TTC) algorithm, uses One-Round Reconstruction (OR) for scalable Shapley value approximation, and executes the full mechanism through blockchain smart contracts for tamper-proof strategy-proofness.
AI.result: Outperforms DA, IAS and other state-of-the-art methods on model accuracy, convergence rate, and reward efficiency across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets, with comparable communication latency to DA and lower blockchain overhead than DA at scale.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE-FL successfully addresses strategic inefficiencies in HFL client selection by simultaneously guaranteeing Pareto efficiency and strategy proofness through TTC-based matching, with practical blockchain execution ensuring real-world applicability.

title: HALOMI: Learning Humanoid Loco-Manipulation with Active Perception from Human Demonstrations
summary: Human demonstrations, which can be collected at scale and naturally capture active hand-eye coordination, are a promising data source for learning humanoid loco-manipulation. However, directly transferring human demonstrations to humanoids requires a precise world-frame tracking controller, which is often brittle under Out-of-Distribution(OOD) targets, while human-to-humanoid gaps persist in both egocentric observation and action execution. To address these challenges, we present HALOMI, a scalable framework for learning humanoid loco-manipulation with active perception from human demonstrations. HALOMI extends Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) with egocentric sensing to collect ego-view and wrist-view observations along with head-hand trajectories at scale. We further propose a manifold-constrained controller that plans in a learned latent behavior manifold to enable precise and robust head-hand tracking in the world frame. To bridge the human-to-humanoid gap, we perform ego-view alignment and introduce a controller-aware reference trajectory adaptation to reduce mismatch in both observation and action execution. We validate HALOMI on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot with an actuated neck across five real-world tasks involving navigation, grasping, bimanual manipulation, whole-body coordination, and dynamic behaviors. Across the three quantitatively evaluated tasks, HALOMI achieves an average success rate of 85\%, while additional qualitative demonstrations show its ability to support dynamic tossing and deep-squat grasping.

AI.tldr: HALOMI is a scalable framework for learning humanoid loco-manipulation from human demonstrations, using egocentric sensing, manifold-constrained control, and cross-domain alignment (observation + action) to bridge human-to-humanoid gaps, achieving 85% average success across real-world tasks on a Unitree G1 robot.
AI.motivation: Human demonstrations offer scalable, natural hand-eye coordination data for humanoid loco-manipulation, but direct transfer suffers from: (1) brittle world-frame tracking under OOD targets, and (2) human-to-humanoid gaps in egocentric observation and action execution.
AI.method: (1) Extends UMI with egocentric sensing (ego-view + wrist-view) for data collection. (2) Manifold-constrained controller for robust head-hand tracking in world frame. (3) Ego-view alignment + controller-aware reference trajectory adaptation to bridge human-to-humanoid gaps.
AI.result: Validated on Unitree G1 with actuated neck across 5 real-world tasks (navigation, grasping, bimanual, whole-body, dynamic). 85% average success rate on 3 quantitatively evaluated tasks; dynamic tossing and deep-squat grasping qualitatively demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: HALOMI effectively scales humanoid loco-manipulation learning from human demonstrations by addressing tracking robustness and cross-domain mismatch, enabling diverse real-world behaviors.

title: Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics: A Formal Theory of Social Intelligence Emergence Through Long-Term Interaction
summary: Current conversational AI systems have made significant progress in language generation, personalization, and long-context interaction. However, most existing methods model social behavior through isolated components such as emotion modeling, memory retrieval, or persona conditioning, lacking a unified framework to explain the emergence of stable social relationships and social intelligence in long-term human-AI interaction.To address this, we propose the Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics Framework (HACD-H), a formal model of human-AI interaction as a self-organizing social cognitive system. HACD-H integrates emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into a unified dynamical framework and introduces principles including multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics.We construct a conversational dataset with approximately 14,700 interaction turns and develop a theory-driven empirical evaluation framework. Results reveal a hierarchy of temporal persistence in social cognition, stable relational attractors, phase-transition-like developmental patterns, and a structured social cognitive energy landscape. Social intelligence shows a significant negative correlation with social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001), and interaction trajectories exhibit progressive energy reduction over time.These findings suggest that social intelligence emerges from long-term social cognitive coevolution rather than isolated conversational capabilities. HACD-H provides a unified theoretical foundation for modeling adaptive human-AI social interaction and developing socially intelligent AI systems.

AI.tldr: HACD-H proposes a unified dynamical framework (Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics) modeling social relationships in long-term human-AI interaction as a self-organizing cognitive system, validated on ~14,700 interaction turns, revealing attractors, phase transitions, and energy landscapes in social cognition.
AI.motivation: Existing conversational AI systems model social behavior through isolated components (emotion, memory, persona), lacking a unified framework to explain how stable social relationships and social intelligence emerge in long-term human-AI interaction.
AI.method: HACD-H integrates emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into a unified dynamical system, introducing multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics. A dataset of ~14,700 turns and a theory-driven empirical evaluation are constructed.
AI.result: Social intelligence negatively correlates with social cognitive energy (r=-0.391, p<0.001). Found: hierarchy of temporal persistence, stable relational attractors, phase-transition-like patterns, decreasing energy over time.
AI.conclusion: Social intelligence emerges from long-term coevolution, not isolated capabilities. HACD-H offers a unified foundation for adaptive human-AI interaction and socially intelligent AI.

title: Spiking Pyramid Wavelet Transformation for High-efficient and Low-energy Image Restoration
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) have garnered significant interest in computer vision due to their potential for efficiency and biological inspiration. While spiking CNN-based methods have shown promise for image restoration (IR) tasks, their performance is constrained by the inherent receptive field limitations of CNN operations. In the paper, we explore the benefits of discrete wavelet transformation and propose a spiking pyramid wavelet-based model (SPWM) for high-efficient and low-energy target. Specifically, we develop a spiking dual pyramid wavelet (SDPW) block to model long-range dependency and exploit the properties of the degradation in the wavelet domain. Experimental results on several benchmarks demonstrate that SPWM significantly lowers computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining image quality. Our method showcases the potential of SNNs in the field of IR, offering new insights for future applications of resource-limited devices.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Spiking Pyramid Wavelet-based Model (SPWM) for image restoration that combines spiking neural networks with discrete wavelet transformation to achieve efficient, low-energy image restoration with long-range dependency modeling.
AI.motivation: Spiking CNN-based methods for image restoration are limited by the receptive field constraints of CNN operations, and there is a need for more efficient, low-energy alternatives suitable for resource-limited devices.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Spiking Pyramid Wavelet-based Model (SPWM) featuring a Spiking Dual Pyramid Wavelet (SDPW) block that leverages discrete wavelet transformation to model long-range dependencies and exploit degradation properties in the wavelet domain.
AI.result: Experimental results on several benchmarks show that SPWM significantly reduces computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining competitive image quality.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the potential of SNNs in image restoration and provides new insights for future applications on resource-constrained devices.

title: LLMZero: Discovering Adaptive Training Strategies for RL Post-Training via LLM Agents
summary: RL post-training strategies are dataset-dependent and reveal a recurring empirical pattern: capacity parameters accumulate monotonically across stages, while regularization parameters predominantly oscillate in response to shifting training dynamics. This distinction matters because fixed schedules commit all parameters to fixed trajectories and therefore cannot express the non-stationary exploration-exploitation tradeoffs that regularization must track; the principle provides actionable design rules for multi-stage training. We discover this through LLMZero, a system where LLM agents search over training trajectories via tree search, diagnosing pathologies at each checkpoint and proposing coordinated multi-parameter transitions. Across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, LLMZero discovers strategies that improve over the base model by 9% to 140% relative and over grid search by 6% to 15% relative, consistently outperforming random search and the skill-based agent. The structural principle transfers across tasks, providing an explanation for why discovered strategies take qualitatively different forms yet share similar parameter dynamics.

AI.tldr: LLMZero uses tree search to discover RL post-training strategies, finding that capacity parameters accumulate monotonically while regularization parameters oscillate, leading to improved performance across 4 GRPO tasks.
AI.motivation: Fixed schedules in RL post-training commit all parameters to fixed trajectories, failing to capture non-stationary exploration-exploitation tradeoffs. The authors aim to discover actionable design rules for multi-stage training.
AI.method: LLMZero employs LLM agents that search over training trajectories via tree search, diagnosing pathologies at each checkpoint and proposing coordinated multi-parameter transitions.
AI.result: Across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, discovered strategies improve over the base model by 9%-140% and over grid search by 6%-15%, consistently outperforming random search and skill-based agents.
AI.conclusion: The structural principle (capacity parameters accumulate monotonically, regularization parameters oscillate) transfers across tasks and explains why discovered strategies share similar parameter dynamics despite qualitatively different forms.

title: Space Is Intelligence: Neural Semigroup Superposition for Riemannian Metric Generation
summary: Traditional approaches place intelligence in the agent, whether as a learned policy or a search procedure. We instead place intelligence in the space itself: a scene induces a Riemannian metric on the configuration manifold, and action reduces to following the geodesics of that metric rather than invoking a separate planner or collision checker. A single Encoder-Router network realizes this idea through three complementary parameter groups -- frame parameters that orient the generators, modulation parameters that govern their spatial propagation, and basic coefficients that determine their strength. These groups combine through a shared semigroup-superposition mechanism to produce a single Riemannian metric field, yielding a compact architecture whose geometry scales naturally with scene complexity. Trained on a single two-obstacle scene, the model demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization across unseen obstacle configurations, with orders-of-magnitude separation between collision-free and obstacle-penetrating path costs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes embedding intelligence into the environment's geometry rather than the agent, using a Riemannian metric field induced by scenes. An Encoder-Router network with three parameter groups generates this metric, enabling geodesic-based motion without separate planning. The model shows strong zero-shot generalization after training on a simple two-obstacle scene.
AI.motivation: Traditional motion planning places intelligence in the agent (learned policies or search procedures). The authors aim to shift this paradigm by embedding intelligence directly into the environment's geometric structure, eliminating the need for separate planners or collision checkers.
AI.method: The method uses an Encoder-Router network with three complementary parameter groups: frame parameters (orient generators), modulation parameters (govern spatial propagation), and basic coefficients (determine strength). These combine via a semigroup-superposition mechanism to produce a single Riemannian metric field. Action then follows geodesics of this metric.
AI.result: Trained on a single two-obstacle scene, the model achieves robust zero-shot generalization across unseen obstacle configurations, with orders-of-magnitude separation between collision-free and obstacle-penetrating path costs.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that embedding intelligence in scene geometry via Riemannian metrics is a viable alternative to traditional agent-centric planning, with strong generalization properties and a compact architecture that scales naturally with scene complexity.

title: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
summary: To achieve deeper safety alignment for large language models (LLMs), recent efforts have studied how to push safety interventions earlier into the pretraining stage, primarily by filtering unsafe data or rewriting it into safer forms. We argue that pretraining-stage alignment should go beyond making the data safe: LLMs may compose seemingly benign knowledge and capabilities into unsafe behaviors. To this end, we propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, a pretraining-stage alignment method which regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring directly into language modeling, establishing a foundational capability that is subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training. Our experiments with 1.7B models pretrained on FineWeb-Edu show that Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces the success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Complementary to our real-world experiments, we also introduce a fully controlled synthetic environment, MedSafetyWorld, with a clear definition of safety and a reasoning structure under which models can easily generalize unsafe behaviors from safe data. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld further demonstrate a clear advantage of Safety Reflection Pretraining in preventing models from acting on unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data, compared with data filtering and rewriting. Taken together, our findings suggest that pretraining alignment should not only make the training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Safety Reflection Pretraining, which inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring into language modeling, showing improved safety against inference-stage and finetuning attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing pretraining-stage safety alignment focuses on making data safe (filtering/rewriting unsafe data), but LLMs can still compose seemingly benign knowledge into unsafe behaviors. The authors argue alignment should go beyond data safety to shape model behaviors.
AI.method: Safety Reflection Pretraining regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora, integrating self-monitoring directly into language modeling. This is established during pretraining and subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training.
AI.result: Experiments with 1.7B models on FineWeb-Edu show improved safety classification accuracy and reduced success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld show advantage over data filtering and rewriting in preventing unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining alignment should not only make training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data. Safety Reflection Pretraining offers a promising direction for deeper safety alignment.

title: MOLAR: Learning Multimodal Molecular Representations from Noisy Labels
summary: Motivation: Noisy labels are a common challenge in molecular property prediction because molecular annotations are often obtained from assays, curated databases, or weak annotation pipelines rather than directly observed clean biological states. Treating recorded labels as reliable supervision can cause models to memorize corrupted observations and learn misleading molecular evidence. In multimodal molecular representation learning, this issue can be amplified by graph-text fusion or alignment, which may propagate label-induced errors across modalities. Results: We propose MOLAR, a noise-aware framework for learning multimodal molecular representations from noisy labels. MOLAR separates latent clean-property inference from recorded-label observation: graph and text views contribute residual evidence to a clean-property distribution, and a categorical label-observation channel maps this distribution to recorded labels for training. This formulation derives posterior label reliability and modality-specific molecular evidence from the model. Experiments on naturally noisy molecular benchmarks and controlled label-flipping benchmarks show that MOLAR consistently outperforms representative baselines. Visualization analyses further show that MOLAR provides interpretable reliability and modality-evidence diagnostics.

AI.tldr: MOLAR is a noise-aware multimodal framework for molecular property prediction that separates clean-property inference from noisy label observations, outperforming baselines on noisy benchmarks and providing interpretable reliability diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels are common in molecular property prediction due to unreliable annotation sources (assays, databases, weak pipelines), and these errors can propagate across modalities in graph-text multimodal learning, causing models to memorize corrupted patterns.
AI.method: MOLAR separates clean-property inference from recorded-label observation: graph and text views contribute residual evidence to a clean-property distribution, and a categorical label-observation channel maps this distribution to recorded labels for training, enabling posterior label reliability estimation.
AI.result: MOLAR consistently outperforms representative baselines on naturally noisy molecular benchmarks and controlled label-flipping benchmarks, with visualizations showing interpretable reliability and modality-evidence diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: Separating clean-property inference from noisy label observations with modality-specific evidence is an effective strategy for robust multimodal molecular representation learning.

title: ZiMPedance: Impedance-Aware ZMP Modeling and Control for Payload Carrying with Quadruped Robots
summary: Load transportation with quadruped robots is strongly affected by the dynamics of the physical interface between the robot and the load. Passive spring-based arms reduce weight and complexity compared to active manipulators, but their spring-damper dynamics can introduce oscillatory forces that degrade locomotion stability. This paper derives an extended Zero Moment Point (ZMP) formulation that includes passive payload-interface dynamics, relating stiffness, damping, and payload mass to the stability margin. The analysis shows that underdamped configurations can resonate with locomotion harmonics. Based on this insight, we augment a Single Rigid Body Dynamics model with passive subsystem dynamics and integrate it into a Model Predictive Control framework. In simulation, the proposed controller reduces stability violations by up to $10\times$, from $7.0\%$ to $0.7\%$, and increase locomotion efficiency by lowering horizontal ground reaction force effort by up to $15\%$ compared to a nominal baseline. Hardware experiments with a $2\,\mathrm{kg}$ payload show stable locomotion under pull-release disturbances where the nominal controller fails. The same model also enables end-effector tracking through passive arm dynamics without direct arm actuation.

AI.tldr: This paper extends ZMP formulation to include passive payload-interface dynamics and integrates it into MPC for quadruped robots, reducing stability violations by 10× and improving efficiency by 15%.
AI.motivation: Load transportation with quadrupeds is affected by passive spring-based arm dynamics (oscillatory forces), but existing controllers ignore these dynamics, degrading locomotion stability.
AI.method: Derives an extended ZMP formulation including stiffness, damping, and payload mass; augments SRBD model with passive subsystem dynamics; integrates into MPC framework.
AI.result: Simulation: stability violations reduced from 7.0% to 0.7% (10× improvement), horizontal ground reaction force effort reduced by 15%. Hardware: stable locomotion under pull-release disturbances where baseline fails.
AI.conclusion: Modeling passive payload-interface dynamics in MPC significantly improves stability and efficiency for quadruped load transportation, and enables end-effector tracking without direct arm actuation.

title: User as Engram: Internalizing Per-User Memory as Local Parametric Edits
summary:
Personal memory in a language model is two problems: content and reasoning skill. The brain keeps the two apart (a sparse, local engram in the hippocampus for each episode, a slow neocortex for the shared skills that interpret it), so a new fact need not overwrite everything else. Most personalization today keeps a user's facts outside the weights, in a natural-language memory file or a retrieval index. When facts are written into the model instead, the standard recipe is the per-user LoRA adapter, which does the opposite of the brain, folding content and skill into one global weight delta. Writing a user's facts as a LoRA contaminates text unrelated to them; writing the same facts as local Engram rows leaves it mathematically untouched, resulting in a roughly 33,000x smaller memory footprint.
  We therefore propose User as Engram: store a user's content as surgical edits to the hash-keyed memory table of an Engram model, and carry the reasoning skill in one shared adapter. This layered design matches per-user LoRA's direct recall while delivering 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average, and never makes a single user worse at reasoning than the untouched base. The edit is a glass box: writing a fact switches on its lookup at exactly the trigger, adds the value the answer needs, leaves every other position unchanged to the last bit, and fails if written into the wrong layer. Because different users' facts land in disjoint hash slots, their edits compose: many users live in one shared table at once, stacking additively and losslessly, where a per-user LoRA, a single global weight delta, admits only one. Upon retrieval, a per-user Engram table does not grow with the population the retriever must search, so past ~100 facts it overtakes a retrieval pipeline on a 2.5x larger model.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "User as Engram," storing user-specific facts as surgical edits to a hash-keyed memory table (Engram model) while keeping reasoning skills in a shared adapter. This avoids contamination from per-user LoRA, delivers 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy, never degrades base model performance, supports lossless composition of many users, and outperforms retrieval pipelines beyond ~100 facts.
AI.motivation: Current personalization methods either store facts outside model weights (retrieval/index) degrading reasoning, or write them as per-user LoRA adapters, which contaminates unrelated text by folding content and skill into one global weight delta, unlike the brain's separation of episodic memory (hippocampus) and skills (neocortex).
AI.method: Store user-specific content as surgical edits to the hash-keyed memory table of an Engram model (local, sparse edits), while carrying reasoning skill in one shared adapter. This layered design separates content (Engram rows) from skill (adapter), enabling glass-box editing where writes only affect the triggered lookup position.
AI.result: Matches per-user LoRA's direct recall, delivers 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average, never degrades any user's reasoning abilities, achieves ~33,000x smaller memory footprint, supports lossless additive composition of many users in one table, and outperforms retrieval pipelines (on 2.5x larger model) beyond ~100 facts.
AI.conclusion: User as Engram provides a scalable, composable, and non-degrading alternative to per-user LoRA for personalization, aligning with the brain's separation of episodic content and reasoning skills, with practical benefits in memory efficiency and multi-user support.

title: LandslideAgent with Multimodal LandslideBench: A Domain-Rule-Augmented Agent for Autonomous Landslide Identification and Analysis
summary: Intelligent landslide hazard interpretation is critical for disaster prevention, yet current paradigms struggle to simultaneously extract visual features and high-level geoscientific semantics, while general-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) suffer from perceptual limitations and domain hallucinations in complex geological scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose an instruction-driven agentic framework comprising three components. First, LandslideBench, a multimodal fine-grained dataset with seven subtype labels, high-resolution imagery, pixel-level masks, and high-quality textual descriptions, is constructed via multi-VLM cross-validation and interactive annotation. Then, LandslideVLM, a landslide-oriented VLM, is fine-tuned via LoRA on LandslideBench to enhance geological semantic understanding. Finally, LandslideAgent, a domain rule-enhanced agent taking LandslideVLM as its cognitive backbone, employs a dual-rule controller incorporating structured report metadata constraints and cross-validation identification constraints to regulate automated tool invocation. Experiments demonstrate that LandslideBench provides effective baselines across five mainstream models on fine-grained classification and semantic segmentation. LandslideVLM achieves accuracy improvements of 10.96%, 32.87%, and 15.91% on landslide discrimination, fine-grained classification, and semantic description quality, respectively. LandslideAgent further enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference, realizing full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes LandslideAgent, an instruction-driven framework integrating a landslide-specific VLM (LandslideVLM) fine-tuned on a new multimodal benchmark (LandslideBench) with dual-rule control logic for automated, full-process landslide interpretation from remote sensing data.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle to jointly extract visual features and high-level geoscientific semantics, and general VLMs suffer from perceptual limits and domain hallucinations in complex geological settings.
AI.method: (1) LandslideBench: multimodal dataset with 7 subtypes, high-res imagery, pixel masks, and descriptions via multi-VLM cross-validation. (2) LandslideVLM: LoRA fine-tuned VLM on LandslideBench for geological understanding. (3) LandslideAgent: domain rule-enhanced agent with dual-rule controller (structured report metadata + cross-validation constraints) for automated tool invocation.
AI.result: LandslideVLM improves landslide discrimination (+10.96%), fine-grained classification (+32.87%), and semantic description quality (+15.91%). LandslideAgent enables autonomous multi-source spatial inference.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis, outperforming five mainstream models on fine-grained classification and segmentation baselines.

title: P$^2$CE: Model-Agnostic Plausible Pareto-Optimal Counterfactual Explanations
comment: Under review in the Machine Learning journal
summary: The increasing use of machine learning algorithms in social applications has raised concerns about fairness and transparency, leading to the development of counterfactual explanations. These explanations supports individuals to understand and potentially alter unfavorable decisions in areas such as loan applications, job selections, and more, by providing actionable changes to input features that would lead to a desired outcome. Existing methods often struggle to balance feasibility, plausibility, and computational efficiency. To address this, we introduce P$^2$CE, an algorithm for generating plausible Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations, offering users a diverse set of optimal trade-offs between different notions of feasibility. P$^2$CE employs an auxiliary isolation forest outlier detector to ensure that explanations are in accordance with the data distribution and leverages SHAP values to obtain optimal results with short computing times, regardless of the underlying model. Our algorithm was empirically evaluated on three datasets, demonstrating superior performance in terms of both solution quality and computational efficiency compared to related techniques.

AI.tldr: Introduces P²CE, an algorithm that generates diverse, plausible, and Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations using isolation forest and SHAP values, achieving better quality and efficiency than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Addressing the need for counterfactual explanations that balance feasibility, plausibility, and computational efficiency in fair and transparent ML systems.
AI.method: P²CE algorithm combining isolation forest for outlier detection (ensuring data-distribution alignment) with SHAP values for efficient optimization, producing a Pareto-optimal set of counterfactual explanations.
AI.result: Empirically outperformed related techniques in both solution quality and computational efficiency across three datasets.
AI.conclusion: P²CE effectively generates plausible, diverse, and computationally efficient counterfactual explanations with optimal trade-offs between feasibility notions.

title: C-ARC: Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering for Non-Repetitive LiDAR Sensors
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication. 8 pages, 7 figures
summary: Real-time LiDAR clustering identifies structures in point clouds, which is an essential prerequisite for many mobile robotics algorithms. Current methods are mostly developed for repetitive mechanical LiDAR sensors. Recently, the use of non-repetitive LiDAR sensors is strongly increasing due to their small cost and form factor. Such non-repetitive Risley prism-based sensors violate two key assumptions of repetitive mechanical sensors: structured scan lines and well-defined frame boundaries. Their Rhodonea-curve trajectories produce non-uniform point distributions, and the absence of a rotation cycle renders conventional scan line indexing inapplicable. To meet such new requirements, we developed C-ARC, a Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering framework that maintains a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window, decoupling high-frequency point insertion from on-demand cluster retrieval. This is crucial for key functionalities like SLAM or tracking. An adaptive range grid resolution mechanism calibrates grid dimensions at initialization using an exponential control loop, balancing the sparsity-collision trade-off without prior knowledge of the scanning pattern. Implemented as an open-sourced single-threaded C++17 library, C-ARC produces real-time cluster output at 20 Hz on commodity hardware for the Livox Mid-360. Evaluation on the Livox Avia identifies unbounded cell occupancy as the primary limitation for sensors with strongly concentrated scan patterns. The adaptive resolution mechanism additionally improves clustering quality for existing grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.

AI.tldr: C-ARC is a Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering framework for non-repetitive LiDAR sensors (e.g., Risley prism-based). It uses a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window with an adaptive resolution grid, achieving real-time (20 Hz) clustering on commodity hardware.
AI.motivation: Non-repetitive LiDAR sensors (Risley prism-based) are increasingly used due to low cost and small form factor, but they violate key assumptions of traditional clustering methods designed for repetitive mechanical sensors: structured scan lines and well-defined frame boundaries. Their Rhodonea-curve trajectories cause non-uniform point distributions and lack rotation cycles, making conventional scan line indexing inapplicable.
AI.method: C-ARC maintains a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window to decouple high-frequency point insertion from on-demand cluster retrieval. It employs an adaptive range grid resolution mechanism that calibrates grid dimensions at initialization using an exponential control loop, balancing the sparsity-collision trade-off without prior knowledge of the scanning pattern.
AI.result: Implemented as an open-sourced single-threaded C++17 library, C-ARC produces real-time cluster output at 20 Hz on commodity hardware for the Livox Mid-360. Evaluation on the Livox Avia identifies unbounded cell occupancy as the primary limitation for sensors with strongly concentrated scan patterns. The adaptive resolution mechanism also improves clustering quality for existing grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.
AI.conclusion: C-ARC effectively addresses the clustering challenges posed by non-repetitive LiDAR sensors, enabling real-time performance critical for SLAM and tracking applications, while the adaptive grid resolution method generalizes to improve other grid-based clustering approaches.

title: TxBench-PP: Analyzing AI Agent Performance on Small-Molecule Preclinical Pharmacology
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) agents promise to accelerate drug discovery by compressing interpretation and decision-making loops, but practical deployment requires trusted evaluation on realistic program decisions. We introduce TherapeuticsBench Preclinical Pharmacology (TxBench-PP), a verifiable benchmark for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology and the first focused slice of a broader TherapeuticsBench effort across drug-discovery stages and therapeutic modalities. TxBench-PP tests whether agents can recover accurate conclusions from real-world assay data rather than memorized facts from literature. The benchmark contains 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, spanning mechanism-of-action (MoA) and pharmacodynamic (PD) reasoning, compound-target engagement, causal target validation, developability and safety, and translational efficacy. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers graded deterministically. Across 16 model-harness configurations, comprising 11 models and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The strongest configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi, passed 59.3\% of endpoint attempts (178/300; 95\% CI, 51.1-67.6), followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3\% (166/300; 47.0-63.6).

AI.tldr: TxBench-PP is a verifiable benchmark for evaluating AI agents on real-world preclinical pharmacology decision-making tasks. Across 16 model configurations and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered accurate conclusions, with the best (Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi) passing only 59.3% of endpoint attempts.
AI.motivation: AI agents promise to accelerate drug discovery but require trusted evaluation on realistic program decisions rather than memorized literature facts. Existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of real-world preclinical pharmacology reasoning.
AI.method: The authors built TxBench-PP with 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, covering MoA/PD reasoning, target engagement, causal validation, developability/safety, and translational efficacy. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers for deterministic grading. They tested 16 model-harness configurations (11 models, 4,800 trajectories).
AI.result: No system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi achieved 59.3% (178/300; 95% CI: 51.1-67.6), followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3% (166/300; 95% CI: 47.0-63.6), indicating substantial room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents are insufficient for reliable deployment in preclinical pharmacology decision-making. TxBench-PP provides a rigorous, verifiable benchmark to drive progress, and the results establish baselines highlighting the gap between current capabilities and practical requirements.

title: BrainFusionNet: a deep learning and XAI model to understand local, global, and sequential features of MRI images for improved brain tumour detection
summary: The noise of Magnetic Resonance Imaging MRI poses challenges for Deep Learning DL when tumor boundaries are obscured tumor location and appearance are complex Therefore we develop BrainFusionNet that combines Convolutional Neural Networks CNNs Vision Transformers ViT and Gated Recurrent Units GRUs to extract spatial contextual and sequential features from MRI images for improved brain tumor classification Furthermore explainable AI such as SHAP LIME and GradCAM are integrated to visualise and highlight image regions that contribute to BrainFusionNets decisionmaking process The proposed BrainFusionNet model is evaluated on two publicly available MRI datasets Kfold validation suggests 98 accuracy on both datasets The model was compared with the six stateoftheart SOTA CNNs and transfer learning Among the SOTA CNNs DenseNet121 and VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 96 The novelty of BrainFusionNet is that the hybrid model effectively extracts local and global features from MRI images even in smallscale tumor regions and small tumor sizes The model has a balanced sequential CNN architecture to capture lowlevel and deeperlayer features a customized ViT that captures local features stabilizes gradient flow and reduces the risk of vanishing gradients during MRI image training The CNN and ViT outputs are fed into a GRU for final classification Furthermore we analyze pixel intensities to determine whether MRI image quality affects image classification Our findings are very novel in image interpretation as we found that the distribution of pixel intensities in MRI images affects DL performance

AI.tldr: BrainFusionNet combines CNN, ViT, and GRU with explainable AI (SHAP, LIME, GradCAM) for brain tumor classification, achieving 98% accuracy on two public MRI datasets.
AI.motivation: MRI noise, obscured tumor boundaries, and complex tumor appearance challenge deep learning models, motivating a need for better feature extraction and interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes BrainFusionNet integrating CNNs for spatial features, ViT for contextual features, and GRUs for sequential features. Explainable AI (SHAP, LIME, GradCAM) is used for visualization. Pixel intensity analysis is also conducted.
AI.result: 98% accuracy via K-fold validation on two datasets, outperforming SOTA CNNs (DenseNet121, VGG16 at 96%).
AI.conclusion: The hybrid model effectively extracts local and global features even in small tumors; pixel intensity distribution affects DL performance.

title: Measurement noise limits the advantage of nonlinear models over linear models in biomedical prediction
summary: On biomedical tabular data, flexible models such as deep networks, gradient-boosted trees, and kernel methods are repeatedly matched or beaten by linear and logistic regression given the same features. The usual reaction is to treat this as a model-side shortfall, to be fixed with more data, a better architecture, or tuning, on the assumption that the nonlinear structure is there and the model has failed to capture it. We argue that these fixes cannot help when the binding limit is the measurement rather than the model, as it frequently is in biomedicine. Additive noise blurs the population-optimal predictor, and because blurring removes a function's fine, rapidly varying detail before its broad shape, it erases nonlinear structure faster than linear structure. A degree-$k$ interaction is attenuated by the $k$-th power of feature reliability, while the linear part is attenuated only once. At the reliabilities typical of biomedical measurement, the nonlinear advantage can vanish even when the underlying biology is strongly nonlinear, and what the noise removes cannot be recovered by a larger cohort or a more flexible model, only by better measurement. The nonlinearity is hidden, not absent, and a tie between linear and flexible models is not by itself a verdict on the biology. These pieces are classical, drawn from measurement-error statistics, psychometrics, and Gaussian analysis, and we assemble them into an exact excess-risk identity. Measurement reliability is one of three conditions, alongside sample size and feature representation, that must align for a flexible model to help, and together they leave only a narrow window that most biomedical tasks fall outside. Across 140 UK Biobank tasks, the gap between flexible and linear models, where it exists, carries the predicted noise signature, and the three conditions can be separated by intervention but not by a benchmark alone.

AI.tldr: Linear models often match or beat flexible models on biomedical tabular data because measurement noise erases nonlinear structure faster than linear structure; this advantage cannot be recovered by more data or better models—only by better measurement.
AI.motivation: To explain why flexible models (deep networks, gradient-boosted trees, kernel methods) repeatedly fail to outperform linear/logistic regression on biomedical tabular data, challenging the assumption that the shortfall is model-side.
AI.method: Combines classical ideas from measurement-error statistics, psychometrics, and Gaussian analysis into an exact excess-risk identity; validates empirically on 140 UK Biobank tasks.
AI.result: Measurement reliability is a binding constraint: a degree-k interaction is attenuated by the k-th power of feature reliability. Across 140 UK Biobank tasks, the gap between flexible and linear models shows the predicted noise signature, and three conditions (reliability, sample size, feature representation) must all align for flexible models to help—a narrow window most biomedical tasks fall outside.
AI.conclusion: A tie between linear and flexible models does not imply linear biology; nonlinearity can be hidden by noise. The proposed framework identifies measurement reliability as a fundamental bottleneck that cannot be circumvented by cohort size or model complexity, only by improved measurement.

title: A High-accuracy Event-based Underwater SLAM System
summary: While event cameras offer immense potential for underwater SLAM, existing Time Surface (TS)-based methods prove highly unreliable when deployed underwater. Fluctuating camera velocities severely degrade TS imaging quality, while wide stereo baselines and repetitive underwater textures induce critical matching failures, frequently triggering system failure. To overcome these challenges, we develop the first high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system. A structure-aware metric for TS is designed based on structure tensor coherence and gradients to quantitatively evaluate TS structural information density. By decoupling the optimal TS generation into two distinct stages based on system initialization, Bayesian Optimization(BO) first predicts an optimal prior TS sequentially before initialization while we set an asynchronous online local searching method periodically to obtain appropriate TS in real-time during the tracking stage. We use the prior disparity to guarantee precise data association and "latest-observation-first'' triangulation mechanism to realize stable triangulation. As a benchmark for these solutions and a resource for the community, we also contribute UWE, the first high-quality real-world underwater event dataset containing variable camera motions, complex textures and different trajectory features. Extensive evaluations on public datasets and UWE show the competitive accuracy performance of the proposed SLAM system compared to the state-of-the-art event-based method. The code and data will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: First high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system addressing TS imaging degradation and matching failures via BO-based optimal TS generation, structure-aware metric, prior disparity, and online parameter tuning; introduces UWE dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing TS-based event camera methods are unreliable underwater due to fluctuating velocities degrading TS imaging, wide stereo baselines, and repetitive textures causing matching failures and system crashes.
AI.method: 1) Structure-aware TS metric based on structure tensor coherence/gradients. 2) Two-stage optimal TS generation: BO predicts prior TS before initialization, asynchronous online local search tunes TS during tracking. 3) Prior disparity for precise data association. 4) "Latest-observation-first" triangulation mechanism.
AI.result: Competitive accuracy on public datasets and the proposed UWE dataset compared to SOTA event-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Proposed system achieves reliable high-accuracy event-based underwater SLAM; code and dataset will be open-sourced.

title: X+Slides: Benchmarking Audience-Conditioned Slide Generation
summary: Automatically generating slide decks from source documents is an important application of large language models (LLMs). Existing benchmarks primarily assess slide completeness and technical depth, while overlooking the target audience as a critical real-world factor. For instance, specialists demand rigorous proofs, whereas decision-makers prioritize actionable conclusions. To bridge this gap, we introduce X+Slides, a benchmark specifically designed for audience-conditioned slide generation. Built on a diverse corpus spanning 113 topics and seven presentation scenes, X+Slides employs a dynamic evaluation framework constructed from 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes. By assigning audience-specific utility weights to the same source-grounded probes, X+Slides reports four complementary metrics: Audience Coverage measures how much audience-essential information is conveyed, Domain-wise Coverage shows which information types are covered, Efficiency measures delivered utility per unit of attention cost, and Correctness verifies whether slide claims are supported by the source. Experiments on DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, and NotebookLM show that current systems can recover a substantial but still incomplete part of audience-essential information: at $τ_A=0.7$, DeepPresenter reaches a best Audience Coverage of 0.714, SlideTailor reaches 0.594, and the NotebookLM ablation reaches 0.853 while showing clear grounding differences. These results indicate that visual quality and broad topic coverage should not be treated as evidence support without source-grounded evaluation.

AI.tldr: X+Slides is a new benchmark for audience-conditioned slide generation that evaluates not just completeness but how well slides serve specific target audiences (e.g., specialists vs. decision-makers), using 8K+ source-grounded probes and four metrics (Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, Correctness).
AI.motivation: Existing slide generation benchmarks focus on technical depth and completeness but ignore the target audience a critical real-world factor where specialists need proofs and decision-makers need conclusions.
AI.method: Built on 113 topics across 7 presentation scenes, X+Slides uses 8,133 deduplicated source-grounded probes with audience-specific utility weights, and reports four metrics: Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, and Correctness.
AI.result: At τ_A=0.7, DeepPresenter scores 0.714, SlideTailor 0.594, and NotebookLM ablation 0.853 in Audience Coverage, showing current systems recover substantial but incomplete audience-essential information.
AI.conclusion: Visual quality and broad topic coverage should not be equated with evidence support; source-grounded evaluation is essential for reliable slide generation assessment.

title: Moving Beyond Diversity: Visual Token Pruning as Subspace Reconstruction for Efficient VLMs
summary: Despite their remarkable performance, Vision Language Models (VLMs) incur substantial computational overhead due to the large number of visual tokens. While diversity maximization has become a dominant strategy for token reduction, existing methods rely on cosine-based normalized similarity that discards magnitude information, failing to faithfully approximate the original feature representation and leading to suboptimal performance, particularly on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks. In this paper, we introduce SPARE, a subspace reconstruction method that reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem and explicitly minimizes reconstruction error. By iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals, SPARE performs reconstruction-driven pruning beyond angular diversity. Moreover, we reveal a counterintuitive anti-relevance phenomenon: tokens with lower image-text relevance score can better preserve contextual information. Based on this finding, we incorporate anti-relevance into SPARE as an additional selection criterion to promote context-aware token selection. Extensive experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that SPARE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with strong gains on compositional tasks. When applied to LLaVA, SPARE removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of the baseline performance, all in a fully training-free manner.

AI.tldr: SPARE is a training-free token pruning method for VLMs that reformulates token reduction as a column subset selection problem, using reconstruction error minimization and an anti-relevance criterion to preserve contextual information, achieving up to 94% token reduction while retaining 95% performance.
AI.motivation: Existing token reduction methods rely on cosine-based normalized similarity that discards magnitude information, failing to faithfully approximate original feature representations and leading to suboptimal performance, especially on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks.
AI.method: SPARE reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem, iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals for reconstruction-driven pruning. It also incorporates an anti-relevance phenomenon—tokens with lower image-text relevance scores better preserve contextual information—as an additional selection criterion.
AI.result: SPARE achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple VLMs and benchmarks, with strong gains on compositional tasks. Applied to LLaVA, it removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of baseline performance, fully training-free.
AI.conclusion: SPARE demonstrates that reconstruction-driven token pruning with anti-relevance criteria is more effective than cosine-based diversity maximization, offering a practical training-free solution for reducing VLM computational overhead.

title: Signature filtering: a lightweight enhancement for statistical watermark detection in large language models
summary: Statistical watermarks help organizations attribute large language model (LLM) outputs, yet existing detectors often struggle when watermark signals are weak, texts are repetitive, or watermarks are edited. We propose signature filtering, a detection-time module that enhances watermark detection without modifying watermark embedding and text generation. It learns a small set of ``signature'' tokens whose presence makes watermark tests unreliable, and removes these tokens before detection. The signatures are obtained by solving a mixed-integer linear program on a small training set, with constraints that maximize the true positive rate. We additionally derive finite-sample and asymptotic bounds under several attacker models (color-blind, color-adaptive, and distributionally correlated). On four well-known watermark families (Kgw, Sweet, Unigram, Exp), four benchmark corpora (C4, MBPP, HumanEval, Code-Search-Net), and six LLMs (Opt-1.3b, Opt-6.7b, Llama2-13b, Llama3.1-8b, Qwen2.5-14b, Phi-3-medium-14b), 2- and 3-gram signatures raise detection rates in weak-signal and low-entropy settings from 8~31% without filtering to 78~99% with filtering, while keeping false positives controllable and often negligible. In stress tests where we scramble sentences and perturb 25~50% of tokens by dilution, deletions, and substitutions, 2-gram filters for Kgw-style watermarks preserve most of the clean-text detection gains, often matching or outperforming the advanced WinMax watermark detector. Signature filtering thus provides a simple, scalable, and model-agnostic add-on to strengthen watermark-based provenance checks for LLM text in information processing workflows.

AI.tldr: Signature filtering is a detection-time module that removes unreliable "signature" tokens to improve LLM watermark detection, raising detection rates from 8-31% to 78-99% in weak-signal settings across multiple models and watermark families.
AI.motivation: Existing watermark detectors struggle when watermark signals are weak, texts are repetitive, or watermarks are edited.
AI.method: Learns a small set of "signature" tokens via a mixed-integer linear program that maximizes true positive rate, then removes these tokens before detection. Includes finite-sample and asymptotic bounds under multiple attacker models.
AI.result: Detection rates improved from 8-31% to 78-99% on four watermark families, four benchmarks, and six LLMs. 2-gram filters also preserved detection gains under 25-50% perturbation stress tests, matching/outperforming WinMax.
AI.conclusion: Signature filtering is a simple, scalable, and model-agnostic add-on that strengthens watermark-based provenance checks for LLM text.

title: Object-Centric Residual RL for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real VLA Enhancement
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can generalize across diverse manipulation tasks, but their imitation-learning-based policies remain brittle in precise physical interactions due to compounding execution errors; Can a reinforcement learning policy trained purely in simulation improve the robustness of real-world VLAs zero-shot? Residual RL, which learns a corrective policy on top of a frozen VLA, offers a natural framework, but existing approaches face a fundamental sim-to-real dilemma: privileged-state methods require lossy distillation for deployment; image-based methods suffer from the visual domain gap; and real-world RL is costly and unsafe. We propose an object-centric residual RL framework that refines VLA actions using object poses, enabling a compact observation space that transfers consistently between simulation and reality. To align the two domains, we additionally replay the same teleoperation demonstrations in simulation to train a sim counterpart of the real-world VLA. The residual RL policy is trained only in simulation with pose noise injection and dropout, and transfers zero-shot to the real robot. Across five manipulation tasks on a real Franka Research 3 (FR3) robot, our method improves the success rate from 42% to 76% zero-shot, and the improved rollouts can be further reused to retrain the base VLA for self-improvement without additional teleoperation. Project page: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/articles/object-centric-residual-rl/

AI.tldr: Object-centric residual RL framework improves VLA model robustness zero-shot from simulation to real robot, raising success rates from 42% to 76% across 5 manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: VLA models suffer from brittle execution errors in precise physical interactions due to compounding errors in imitation learning. The goal is to leverage simulation-trained RL to improve real-world VLA robustness without costly real-world RL or lossy distillation.
AI.method: Proposes object-centric residual RL using pose observations (not images) for consistent sim-to-real transfer. Replays teleoperation demos in simulation to train a sim VLA counterpart. Residual policy is trained in simulation with pose noise injection and dropout, then deployed zero-shot on real robot.
AI.result: On Franka FR3 robot across 5 tasks, success rate improved from 42% to 76% zero-shot. Improved rollouts can be reused to retrain base VLA, enabling self-improvement without extra teleoperation.
AI.conclusion: Object-centric residual RL effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap for VLA refinement, offering a safe, cost-effective path to improve real-world manipulation robustness using only simulation data.

title: NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning
summary:
Neurosymbolic semantics is fragmented: classical, fuzzy, probabilistic and neural systems each define truth by their own inductive rules. NeSyCat, extending ULLER, subsumes them under a single inductive definition of truth, parametric in a strong monad and an aggregation structure on truth-values. NeSyCat has so far lacked an account of predicates and functions learned by neural networks.
  We provide NeSyCat Torch as the missing link and interpret computational symbols via neural networks, implementing the framework in probabilistic programming and tensor-based backends. We use the distribution monad for reference semantics and metric evaluation, and complement it by a monad for numerically stable, differentiable training: the lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring. For efficient training in batches, we furthermore employ a batch monad. The axioms are the source code: written once in monad-based do-notation, monadic bind performs marginalisation, lazily pruning unneeded branches.
  On MNIST addition, our HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy, while achieving nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog. However, unlike DeepStochLog, we stay in a uniform framework that applies to many first-order NeSy approaches. Namely, the construction is parametric in the monad; instantiating it with, e.g., the Giry monad extends the approach to continuous probability (working out a neural representation here is left for future work).

AI.tldr: NeSyCat Torch bridges neural networks with neurosymbolic semantics by using monad-based frameworks (distribution, lazy log-tensor, batch monads) to unify classical, fuzzy, probabilistic, and neural truth definitions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on MNIST addition.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic semantics is fragmented across different paradigms (classical, fuzzy, probabilistic, neural). NeSyCat unified them under a single inductive definition but lacked support for neural-network-learned predicates and functions, which this work addresses.
AI.method: The authors introduce NeSyCat Torch, interpreting computational symbols via neural networks in probabilistic programming with tensor backends. They use the distribution monad for reference semantics, a lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring for stable differentiable training, and a batch monad for efficient batch processing. The axioms are written once in monad-based do-notation, with monadic bind handling marginalization and lazy pruning.
AI.result: On MNIST addition, HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in both speed and accuracy, achieving nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog while maintaining a uniform framework applicable to many first-order NeSy approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework is parametric in the monad; instantiating with the Giry monad could extend to continuous probability (future work). This provides a unified, efficient, and extensible approach to neurosymbolic AI.

title: Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification Using Advanced Deep Learning Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Despite recent advancements in deep learning, accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images continues to pose challenges. In this research, we present a comprehensive evaluation of five different convolutional neural networks (CNN) architectures, including a customized baseline model and four pre-trained models - for use in classifying multi-class brain tumors using a clinically-sourced dataset of approximately 10,000 MRI images. We have utilized five different architectures; VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, and EfficientNetB0, which were all tested and trained within an identical experimental framework. Performance was measured by both overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall as a means to measure the clinically-relevant performance of each architecture. We found that EfficientNetB0 had the best overall classification accuracy at 95%, when compared to the other architectures tested; specifically VGG16 (94.37%), VGG19 (92.29%), DenseNet121 (90.91%) and the customized CNN (78.00%). An especially important finding of our research was the considerable improvement in detecting meningiomas; specifically, while simple CNNs could detect meningiomas with a recall rate of approximately 20%, EfficientNetB0 was able to detect meningiomas with a recall rate of 89%. Meningiomas are often difficult to detect because they can appear very subtly on MRI images. Additionally, an interesting finding was that the deeper VGG19 performed worse than the shallower VGG16. This indicates that in many cases the architectural efficiency of a CNN model may be more important than its depth when working with medical images. Overall, EfficientNetB0 appears to provide the optimal trade-off between classification accuracy, number of parameters used in the model and clinically meaningful performance.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five CNN architectures (custom CNN, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0) for brain tumor classification from ~10,000 MRI images, finding EfficientNetB0 achieves the best overall accuracy (95%) and significantly improved meningioma detection (89% recall vs. ~20% for simple CNNs).
