AI.motivation: Accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images remains challenging despite deep learning advances; the authors aim to identify which CNN architecture provides the best clinically-relevant performance for multi-class brain tumor classification.
AI.method: Five CNN architectures (customized baseline, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0) were trained and tested within an identical experimental framework on ~10,000 clinically-sourced MRI images. Performance was measured by overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall.
AI.result: EfficientNetB0 achieved highest accuracy (95%), outperforming VGG16 (94.37%), VGG19 (92.29%), DenseNet121 (90.91%), and custom CNN (78.00%). Notably, EfficientNetB0 improved meningioma detection from ~20% recall (simple CNN) to 89% recall. VGG19 (deeper) performed worse than VGG16 (shallower).
AI.conclusion: Architectural efficiency may be more important than depth in medical image classification. EfficientNetB0 offers the optimal trade-off between accuracy, parameter efficiency, and clinically meaningful performance for brain tumor detection from MRI images.

title: Beyond Prediction: Tail-Aware Scheduling for LLM Inference
summary: LLM serving exhibits extreme length variability, making size-based scheduling difficult in practice. Recent LLM schedulers approximate SJF/SRPT using predicted decode lengths or ranks and primarily report mean-centric metrics such as TTFT and TBT. We show that these prediction-driven policies can be fragile under distribution shifts, bursty arrivals, and GPU memory pressure, while offering limited control over the tail latency (P90-P99) that dominates user experience, even with perfect decode-length knowledge. We introduce a distribution-aware, prediction-free scheduling framework that replaces explicit length prediction with soft priority boosting driven by lightweight statistical signals. Our design co-optimizes scheduling and cache-aware preemption to account for memory-coupled decode dynamics across workload mixes. Evaluated on production and open-source traces, our method reduces P99 TTLT by up to 35-50% relative to SRPT with perfect length knowledge and reduces TTFT by 34-47% across workloads, including reasoning-heavy and chat-heavy tasks. These results demonstrate a robust alternative for optimizing tail latency in online LLM serving.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a prediction-free scheduling framework for LLM serving that uses lightweight statistical signals and memory-aware preemption to improve tail latency, achieving up to 35-50% reduction in P99 TTLT compared to SRPT with perfect knowledge.
AI.motivation: Existing prediction-driven LLM schedulers (approximating SJF/SRPT) are fragile under distribution shifts, bursty arrivals, and GPU memory pressure, while offering poor control over tail latency (P90-P99) that dominates user experience.
AI.method: A distribution-aware, prediction-free scheduling framework that replaces explicit length prediction with soft priority boosting driven by lightweight statistical signals, and co-optimizes scheduling with cache-aware preemption to handle memory-coupled decode dynamics.
AI.result: Reduces P99 TTLT by 35-50% and TTFT by 34-47% relative to SRPT with perfect length knowledge across production and open-source traces, including reasoning-heavy and chat-heavy workloads.
AI.conclusion: The proposed prediction-free approach provides a robust, distribution-aware alternative for optimizing tail latency in online LLM serving, outperforming even oracle-length-knowledge baselines.

title: TactSpace: Learning a Physics-enriched Shared Latent Space for Tactile Sim-to-Real Transfer
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables, accepted into IROS 2026
summary: Tactile sensing provides direct measurements of contact interactions that are essential for robotic manipulation. However, current simulators lack the fidelity to faithfully model the complex deformation and transduction mechanics of tactile sensors, severely hindering sim-to-real transfer in robot learning pipelines. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-modal representation learning framework that aligns heterogeneous tactile modalities within a shared latent space, eliminating the need for accurate raw-signal simulation while preserving relevant contact information. Our approach employs modality-specific encoders to project diverse tactile observations, such as simulated penetration depth and real-world capacitance, into a common embedding space. The model is trained using self- and cross-reconstruction objectives alongside contrastive alignment, encouraging modality-invariant yet information-rich representations. We evaluate the learned embeddings on indenter shape identification, force prediction, and geometric reconstruction tasks, training exclusively in simulation and testing directly on real sensor measurements. Our results demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across physically dissimilar representations. Furthermore, incorporating multi-physics simulation modalities yields more informative embeddings that transfer across diverse downstream tasks, demonstrating a 16.7% reduction in force prediction error and a 45.8% reduction in shape reconstruction error. Finally, we release an efficient Warp-based implementation of a penalty-based tactile simulation model for Isaac Lab, enabling scalable tactile data generation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-modal representation learning framework that aligns simulated and real tactile sensor signals in a shared latent space, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for robotic manipulation tasks, achieving 16.7% lower force prediction error and 45.8% lower shape reconstruction error.
AI.motivation: Current simulators lack the fidelity to accurately model tactile sensor deformation and transduction mechanics, hindering sim-to-real transfer in robot learning pipelines.
AI.method: A multi-modal representation learning framework with modality-specific encoders projects diverse tactile observations (e.g., simulated penetration depth, real-world capacitance) into a common embedding space, trained with self-/cross-reconstruction and contrastive alignment objectives.
AI.result: Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer is achieved. Force prediction error reduced by 16.7%, shape reconstruction error reduced by 45.8% using multi-physics simulation modalities.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal alignment of tactile representations enables effective sim-to-real transfer without accurate raw-signal simulation, and the released simulation model facilitates scalable tactile data generation.

title: Rethinking Reward Supervision: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation
summary: Post-training of reasoning language models is commonly driven by supervised distillation and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Distillation often relies on chain-of-thought annotations that are expensive to obtain and may themselves be noisy, incomplete, or partially incorrect; even when the final solution is correct, an imperfect rationale can interfere with learning. Reinforcement learning with verified rewards, on the other hand, typically compresses evaluative feedback into a scalar signal, obscuring which aspects of a response should be improved. We propose \textbf{Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation}, a framework that incorporates rubrics as structured, fine-grained feedback for on-policy self-distillation. Our method conditions the teacher model on criterion-level rubrics and uses it to provide token-level guidance on the student's own sampled trajectories. This design avoids treating a single reference rationale as the sole supervision target. Instead, rubrics specify what a strong response should satisfy, enabling more fine-grained credit assignment over the reasoning process than scalar reward optimization. We instantiate this framework with a two-stage pipeline that first learns to generate task-specific rubrics and then trains a rubric-guided reasoner. We evaluate on a diverse suite of science reasoning benchmarks and results show that rubric-conditioned self-distillation effectively converts rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance over the reasoning process, surpassing GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average.

AI.tldr: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation uses structured rubrics as fine-grained feedback for on-policy self-distillation in reasoning language models, outperforming GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on science reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods for reasoning LMs have limitations: supervised distillation relies on expensive, potentially noisy chain-of-thought annotations, while reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards compresses feedback into a scalar signal, losing fine-grained information about which aspects need improvement.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) learn to generate task-specific rubrics, (2) train a rubric-guided reasoner via rubric-conditioned on-policy self-distillation. The teacher model uses criterion-level rubrics to provide token-level guidance on the student's own sampled trajectories.
AI.result: Surpasses GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average across diverse science reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-conditioned self-distillation effectively converts rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance over the reasoning process, offering a structured alternative to both scalar reward optimization and noisy distillation targets.

title: Spatially Stratified Distillation for Heterogeneous Radar Place Recognition
comment: IEEE ICRA Workshop on Open Challenges for Rigorous Robot Perception 2026
summary: Scalable, all-weather place recognition increasingly relies on heterogeneous radar place recognition to bridge diverse hardware platforms. A notable application is matching queries from cost-effective 4D automotive radars against high-fidelity reference maps built by dense spinning radars. This process is fundamentally limited by the extreme sparsity (and narrow field-of-view) of the 4D sensor, which captures only a fraction of the structural density present in the spinning radar database. Prior efforts address this issue by unifying different radar signals. That is, projecting both signals into a common representational space. Yet, they suffer performance degradation in multi-session environments. In this paper, we propose spatially-stratified distillation (SSD); a strategy that replaces standard uniform distillation with an asymmetric spatial alignment derived directly from physical radar returns. In regions where both radars exhibit overlapping returns, SSD enforces strong feature alignment. Crucially, in sparse regions where the 4D student lacks returns but the teacher contains valid structure within the shared field of view, SSD applies heavily discounted distillation weights. Extensive evaluations of the recent HeRCULES dataset demonstrate that SSD significantly outperforms prior place recognition methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on its challenging dynamic sequences.

AI.tldr: SSD proposes spatially-stratified distillation for heterogeneous radar place recognition, aligning dense spinning radar (teacher) with sparse 4D automotive radar (student) via asymmetric spatial weighting based on physical radar returns, achieving SOTA on HeRCULES dataset.
AI.motivation: Bridging sparse 4D automotive radar queries (cost-effective, narrow FoV) against dense spinning radar maps is fundamentally limited by extreme sparsity. Prior uniform distillation approaches suffer performance degradation in multi-session environments.
AI.method: SSD replaces standard uniform distillation with asymmetric spatial alignment derived from physical radar returns: (1) strong feature alignment where both radars overlap, (2) heavily discounted distillation weights in sparse regions where the 4D student lacks returns but the teacher has valid structure within the shared FoV.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on the HeRCULES dataset demonstrate SSD significantly outperforms prior place recognition methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on challenging dynamic sequences.
AI.conclusion: Spatially-stratified distillation effectively addresses the modality gap between heterogeneous radars for place recognition, particularly in multi-session settings, by leveraging physical spatial alignment cues rather than uniform distillation.

title: TMR-GGNN: Credit Card Fraud Detection based on Time-Aware Multi-Relational Guided Graph Neural Network
comment: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON), Pages 7
summary: In recent years, credit card fraud detection has faced significant challenges due to highly imbalanced data, evolving fraud patterns, and complex relational structures among transaction entities. To address these issues, this research proposes a novel framework called Timeaware Multi Relational Guided Graph Neural Network (TMR GGNN). Particularly, the proposed TMR GGNN extends the encoder decoder Graph Neural Network GNN architecture by modeling heterogeneous interactions across customers, merchants, devices, and IPs over temporal windows. Subsequently, the proposed TMR GGNN approach constructs a dynamic, multi relational graph and incorporates a time aware relational attention mechanism within the encoder to adaptively weigh the transaction relevance based on temporal proximity and semantic context. Consequently, the decoder employs a contrastive learning module to distinguish between real and synthesized transaction patterns, while improving the models generalization of rare fraud cases. Additionally, to effectively manage severe class imbalances and emphasize discriminative learning, a composite loss function combining Information Noise Contrastive Estimation (InfoNCE) based contrastive loss with Focal Loss is introduced. This integration assists in improving fraud identification while mitigating false negatives.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TMR-GGNN, a Time-aware Multi-Relational Guided Graph Neural Network for credit card fraud detection, which models heterogeneous transaction entities (customers, merchants, devices, IPs) over temporal windows using time-aware relational attention and contrastive learning with a composite loss function (InfoNCE + Focal Loss) to address imbalanced data and evolving fraud patterns.
AI.motivation: Credit card fraud detection faces challenges from highly imbalanced data, evolving fraud patterns, and complex relational structures among transaction entities, requiring more sophisticated models to capture temporal and relational dynamics.
AI.method: TMR-GGNN extends encoder-decoder GNN architecture by: (1) modeling heterogeneous interactions across entities over temporal windows; (2) constructing dynamic multi-relational graphs with time-aware relational attention in the encoder; (3) using contrastive learning in the decoder to distinguish real vs. synthesized transactions; (4) employing a composite loss combining InfoNCE contrastive loss and Focal Loss.
AI.result: The approach improves fraud identification while mitigating false negatives, with better generalization on rare fraud cases through contrastive learning and the composite loss function.
AI.conclusion: TMR-GGNN effectively addresses class imbalance, evolving fraud patterns, and relational complexity in credit card fraud detection by integrating temporal awareness, multi-relational graph modeling, and contrastive learning.

title: Congestion-Aware Robot Tour Planning in Crowded Environments
summary: Autonomous mobile service robots are often required to complete tours that require navigating through a set of locations in an environment. Example domains include guiding people through a shopping mall, delivering packages in a fulfilment centre, or giving guided tours in a museum. However, in crowded environments, the presence of people may negatively impact robot performance. For example, humans will activate robot collision avoidance manoeuvres that slow the robot down. Crowds move stochastically and vary throughout the day. In this paper we present a probabilistic tour planner for crowded environments which explicitly reasons over human congestion. We learn circular linear flow field (CLiFF) maps which predict human trajectories given an initial observation. We then use these predictions to build and solve a Markov decision process online which efficiently routes the robot through the environment. Our approach is scalable enough to re-plan as new people are observed. We evaluate our approach on a real-world crowd dataset in a shopping mall.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic tour planner for crowded environments that uses CLiFF maps to predict human trajectories and solves MDPs online to efficiently route service robots through congested spaces.
AI.motivation: Autonomous service robots navigating crowded environments (e.g., shopping malls, museums) experience performance degradation due to human-triggered collision avoidance and stochastic crowd movements.
AI.method: Learns CLiFF (circular linear flow field) maps to predict human trajectories, then builds and solves Markov decision processes (MDPs) online for efficient routing, with scalable re-planning as new people are observed.
AI.result: Evaluated on a real-world crowd dataset in a shopping mall setting.
AI.conclusion: Explicit probabilistic reasoning over human congestion enables more efficient tour planning for service robots in dynamic, crowded environments.

title: UniTemp: Unlocking Video Generation in Any Temporal Order via Bidirectional Distillation
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models have emerged as a promising approach for long video generation, achieving strong performance in streaming settings. However, existing methods are restricted to forward temporal generation, whereas practical video creation often requires flexible generation order, e.g., conditioning on future context to extend backward, or on both past and future context for inbetween generation. We bridge this gap by training an autoregressive model that supports generation in arbitrary temporal directions. A key technical challenge arises from the Causal 3D VAE widely used in video diffusion models, which encodes latents strictly conditioned on past context. While suited for forward generation, this causal structure causes inter-block discontinuities when generation proceeds backward. To address this, we introduce blockwise anchor latents, a set of auxiliary latents that restore the missing past context at block boundaries during backward generation. Built on this design, we propose UniTemp, a bidirectional distillation framework that trains a single autoregressive student model for any-direction video generation. At inference time, UniTemp conditions on arbitrary past and/or future frames, improving controllability for both bidirectional and inbetween generation. Experiments show that UniTemp maintains competitive performance on short and long video generation compared to forward-only methods, while enabling diverse workflows such as bidirectional video extension, inbetween generation, looping video generation, scene transition, and visual story generation. Project website: https://lzhangbj.github.io/projects/unitemp/

AI.tldr: UniTemp introduces a bidirectional distillation framework that enables autoregressive video diffusion models to generate videos in arbitrary temporal directions (forward, backward, or inbetween), overcoming the limitations of Causal 3D VAEs through blockwise anchor latents.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive video diffusion models are restricted to forward-only temporal generation, while practical video creation requires flexible generation order such as backward extension or inbetween generation.
AI.method: (1) Introduces blockwise anchor latents to restore missing past context at block boundaries during backward generation, overcoming Causal 3D VAE limitations. (2) Proposes UniTemp, a bidirectional distillation framework that trains a single autoregressive student model for any-direction video generation.
AI.result: Maintains competitive performance on short and long video generation compared to forward-only methods, while enabling diverse workflows including bidirectional video extension, inbetween generation, looping video generation, scene transition, and visual story generation.
AI.conclusion: UniTemp effectively bridges the gap between forward-only autoregressive video generation and practical needs for flexible temporal direction control, enabling multiple new video creation workflows.

title: A Cross-Model VLM-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Mesh Quality (and Why Cheap Proxies Fall Short)
summary: Single-image-to-3D generators are improving quickly, but there is no agreed, human-free way to tell whether one generated mesh is better than another. Practitioners commonly rely on cheap automatic proxies (render-space CLIP similarity and mesh geometry-validity statistics), yet how well these track perceived quality is unestablished. We make two contributions. First, we propose and validate a reproducible VLM-judge evaluation protocol: a fixed 24-view headless render rig, two independent vision-language judge families, and a mandatory position-bias correction that queries both presentation orders and keeps only order-consistent verdicts. The two judge families agree substantially with each other (Cohen's kappa = 0.66), well above the chance-agreement floor. Second, using this protocol as the reference, we show the cheap proxies do not substitute for it. Geometry validity is only a weak signal on average (because, as we show, it is bimodal) and stays below our pre-registered target, while render-CLIP is at chance. A learned Bradley-Terry head collapses onto a single manifoldness statistic (giving render-CLIP a negative weight) and matches geometry-only exactly, so learning the feature weights buys nothing. The proxy is also bimodal: it is significantly above chance on contrasts with visible geometric defects but at chance on ambiguous contrasts, consistent with geometry validity tracking the judge only when the defect is visually salient. We therefore recommend the VLM-judge protocol as a reliable, reproducible evaluator under the conditions tested (two feed-forward generators on Google Scanned Objects, with a face-drop degradation regime) and advise against geometry/CLIP proxies as optimization targets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a VLM-judge evaluation protocol for single-image-to-3D generation that uses two independent vision-language models with position-bias correction, showing it is reliable (kappa=0.66 agreement) while demonstrating that cheap proxies like render-CLIP and geometry validity statistics fail to substitute for this protocol.
AI.motivation: There is no agreed, human-free way to evaluate whether one generated 3D mesh is better than another, as cheap automatic proxies (CLIP similarity and geometry validity) have unestablished correlation with perceived quality.
AI.method: Proposed a VLM-judge protocol with: (1) fixed 24-view headless render rig, (2) two independent vision-language judge families, (3) mandatory position-bias correction. Used this as reference to evaluate cheap proxies.
AI.result: Two VLM judge families agree substantially (Cohen's kappa=0.66). Geometry validity is weak/bimodal signal below target; render-CLIP performs at chance. Learned Bradley-Terry head collapses to geometry-only performance.
AI.conclusion: Recommend VLM-judge protocol as reliable evaluator under tested conditions; advise against using geometry/CLIP proxies as optimization targets.

title: Sensor Configuration Matters: A Systematic Evaluation of Multimodal SLAM on Quadruped Robots
summary: Autonomous navigation of quadrupedal robots in diverse environments fundamentally relies on resilient Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). While visual-inertial SLAM has matured across wheeled, handheld, and aerial platforms, a critical evaluation gap remains regarding how hardware-level sensor configurations affect performance under the aggressive dynamics of legged locomotion. Quadrupeds introduce distinct embodiment-induced sensory challenges, including foot-impact shocks, high-frequency mechanical vibrations, and rapid angular rotations, which degrade standard perception pipelines. To address this gap, we present a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art visual, visual-inertial, and LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM methods using the GrandTour dataset recorded on an ANYmal D quadruped. We isolate and quantify the impacts of camera modalities, shutter techniques, and inertial sensor tiers, analyzing their trade-offs across localization accuracy, algorithmic robustness, and computational resource utilization. Our empirical findings demonstrate that hardware selection has substantial influence on system resilience: stereo configurations consistently outperform monocular and RGB-D modalities, global shutter cameras significantly mitigate motion-induced tracking failures compared to rolling shutter cameras, and, crucially, standard inertial integration can degrade the performance of primarily vision-based frameworks under harsh legged locomotion. These insights additionally offer concrete design guidelines for tailoring custom sensor payloads to achieve dependable perception on agile legged systems.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates visual, visual-inertial, and LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM methods on quadrupedal robots using the GrandTour dataset, finding that stereo cameras, global shutters, and careful inertial integration significantly improve robustness under legged locomotion dynamics.
AI.motivation: Quadrupedal robots face unique sensory challenges (foot-impact shocks, high-frequency vibrations, rapid rotations) that degrade standard SLAM pipelines, yet there is a critical gap in understanding how hardware-level sensor configurations affect SLAM performance under such aggressive dynamics.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art visual, visual-inertial, and LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM methods using the GrandTour dataset recorded on an ANYmal D quadruped, isolating and quantifying the impacts of camera modalities, shutter techniques, and inertial sensor tiers.
AI.result: Stereo configurations outperform monocular and RGB-D modalities; global shutter cameras significantly reduce motion-induced tracking failures compared to rolling shutter cameras; standard inertial integration can degrade vision-based frameworks under harsh legged locomotion.
AI.conclusion: Hardware selection substantially influences SLAM system resilience on legged robots, and the findings provide concrete design guidelines for tailoring sensor payloads to achieve dependable perception on agile quadrupedal systems.

title: PEFT-MedSAM: Efficient Fine-Tuning of Medical Foundation Models for Explainable Skin Lesion Segmentation
summary: Automated segmentation of skin lesions using deep learning models for dermoscopic images can be very helpful in finding melanomas earlier than they would normally be detected. However, most deep learning methods available do not perform well. The aim of this paper is to present a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method called PEFT-MedSAM for adapting the Medical Segment Anything Model (MedSAM) to automatically segment dermoscopic skin lesions. The PEFT-MedSAM method uses only the lightweight mask decoder for training the model while keeping the pre-trained image encoder and prompt encoder frozen. The experiments performed on the ISIC 2018 benchmark dataset shows that PEFT-MedSAM obtains a dice coefficient of .9411 and an intersection over union value of .8918 when compared to both a fully trained U-Net baseline (.8715 dice coefficient) and zero-shot MedSAM inference (.8997 dice coefficient). The external validation of the model using PH2 dataset shows .9467 dice coefficient with +/- .0310 standard deviation. Supportive evidence for these claims include a p-value less than .0001 for Wilcoxon signed rank tests comparing the two datasets and bootstrap-estimated 95% confidence intervals of [.9364,.9447] that represent the estimated range of possible values for the average dice coefficient obtained by repeating the test. To increase clinical trustworthiness, we used Grad-CAM explainability along with a pointing game based evaluation methodology to evaluate the CNN baseline model on the validation set. The results showed that we had an accuracy rate of 98.27% on the validation set of 519 images and confirmed that the model classified regions containing skin lesions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes PEFT-MedSAM, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for adapting MedSAM to skin lesion segmentation, achieving superior performance (Dice .9411, IoU .8918) on ISIC 2018 and external validation on PH2 (Dice .9467), with statistical significance and Grad-CAM explainability for clinical trustworthiness.
AI.motivation: Automated segmentation of skin lesions using deep learning can help detect melanomas earlier, but existing deep learning methods do not perform well.
AI.method: PEFT-MedSAM adapts MedSAM by fine-tuning only the lightweight mask decoder while keeping the pre-trained image encoder and prompt encoder frozen, and uses Grad-CAM with a pointing game for explainability.
AI.result: On ISIC 2018: Dice .9411, IoU .8918 (vs U-Net .8715 Dice and zero-shot MedSAM .8997 Dice). External validation on PH2: Dice .9467 ± .0310. Statistical significance p<.0001, 95% CI [.9364,.9447]. Grad-CAM pointing game accuracy 98.27%.
AI.conclusion: PEFT-MedSAM is an effective and parameter-efficient approach for dermoscopic skin lesion segmentation with strong generalization and clinically trustworthy explainability.

title: Veriphi: Attack-Guided Neural Network Verification with Dataset-Dependent Training Methods
summary: We present Veriphi, a GPU-accelerated neural network verification system that combines fast adversarial attacks with formal bound certification using alpha,beta-CROWN methods. Through systematic experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 using three training methodologies (standard, adversarial, certified), we demonstrate that training method effectiveness is fundamentally dataset-dependent. Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) achieves 78% certified accuracy on simple MNIST (784 dimensions) but provides negligible certification performance on the more complex CIFAR-10 dataset, where PGD adversarial training dominates with 94% certification at small perturbations. We achieve 5x verification speedup through attack-guided falsification and scale our approach to production-size models (105.8M parameters) for real-world aerospace logistics optimization. Our results challenge the assumption that certified training universally outperforms adversarial training, showing context matters critically for verification strategy selection.

AI.tldr: Veriphi is a GPU-accelerated neural network verification system combining adversarial attacks with formal certification (alpha,beta-CROWN). It shows that training method effectiveness is dataset-dependent: IBP excels on simple MNIST (78% certified accuracy) while PGD adversarial training dominates on CIFAR-10 (94% certification). The system achieves 5x speedup and scales to production models (105.8M parameters) for aerospace logistics.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that certified training universally outperforms adversarial training, and demonstrate that verification strategy selection is context-dependent rather than one-size-fits-all.
AI.method: Combines fast adversarial attacks with formal bound certification using alpha,beta-CROWN methods, with attack-guided falsification for 5x speedup. Evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across three training paradigms: standard, adversarial, and certified (IBP).
AI.result: IBP achieves 78% certified accuracy on MNIST but negligible performance on CIFAR-10; PGD adversarial training achieves 94% certification on CIFAR-10 at small perturbations; 5x verification speedup achieved; system scales to 105.8M parameter models for aerospace logistics.
AI.conclusion: Training method effectiveness is fundamentally dataset-dependent; certified training does not universally outperform adversarial training; context critically determines optimal verification strategy.

title: ReSiReg: Towards Spatially Consistent Semantics in Language-Conditioned Robotic Tasks
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) enable robots to follow open-language instructions. However, dense VLM embeddings have shown to be noisy and lack spatial consistency. This is problematic for robotic applications, which require simultaneous reasoning over semantics and 3D space. We examine spatial structure across recent VLMs and propose ReSiReg, a feature reconstruction method that uses spatially consistent VLM intermediates to improve dense language-grounded retrieval. ReSiReg clusters intermediates into visual prototypes, derives their language descriptors, and reconstructs each patch as a soft mixture of prototype-level language embeddings. We evaluate quantitatively on OVSS and 3D mapping across backbones, and qualitatively in real-world manipulation scenes. Quantitative results show improved dense retrieval; manipulation scenes show more spatially consistent target activations. We further provide a compact 25M dense VLM for robotic applications, substantially smaller than and competitive with ViT-B baselines. Available at https://resireg.github.io

AI.tldr: ReSiReg improves dense language-grounded retrieval for robotics by reconstructing VLM features using spatially consistent intermediates, achieving better spatial consistency with a compact 25M model.
AI.motivation: VLMs for robotics suffer from noisy, spatially inconsistent dense embeddings, making it difficult to simultaneously reason over semantics and 3D space for open-language instruction following.
AI.method: ReSiReg clusters spatially consistent VLM intermediates into visual prototypes, derives language descriptors for them, and reconstructs each patch as a soft mixture of prototype-level language embeddings.
AI.result: Improved dense retrieval on OVSS and 3D mapping tasks; more spatially consistent target activations in real-world manipulation; a compact 25M model competitive with ViT-B baselines.
AI.conclusion: Feature reconstruction via spatially consistent prototypes effectively reduces noise and improves spatial grounding for VLM-based robotic manipulation.

title: Rethinking the Pointer Loss in Table Structure Recognition: Geometry-Aware Pointer Loss for Spatial Locality
summary: Table Structure Recognition (TSR) using a pointer network achieves impressive results by predicting HTML sequences while aligning tags to detected text (or cell) regions. However, our analysis reveals that when pointer networks fail, 79.6% of errors occur between spatially adjacent cells (Manhattan distance <= 2). Despite this, standard cross-entropy loss weights all negative candidates equally. In this work, we propose Geometry-Aware Pointer (GAP) Loss, which reweights the cross-entropy objective based on spatial proximity to ground truth. By applying inverse distance weighting, GAP focuses gradient flow where the model struggles most: immediate neighbors receive stronger gradients than distant cells. Our approach requires only a straightforward modification to the loss computation, maintaining the same model architecture with zero additional inference cost. Extensive experiments on PubTabNet and SynthTabNet demonstrate that GAP consistently reduces adjacent-cell errors, achieving new state-of-the-art performance. Our findings suggest that incorporating geometric inductive biases at the loss level provides a simple yet effective approach to robust TSR. Our code is available at https://github.com/teamreboott/GAP

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Geometry-Aware Pointer (GAP) Loss, a modified loss function that reweights cross-entropy based on spatial proximity to reduce adjacent-cell errors in Table Structure Recognition using pointer networks, achieving SOTA without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Analysis reveals 79.6% of pointer network errors occur between spatially adjacent cells (Manhattan distance ≤ 2), yet standard cross-entropy weights all negative candidates equally, failing to address this spatial error pattern.
AI.method: GAP Loss applies inverse distance weighting to the cross-entropy objective, giving stronger gradients to immediate neighbors of ground truth cells while reducing gradients for distant cells.
AI.result: Consistently reduces adjacent-cell errors on PubTabNet and SynthTabNet, achieving new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating geometric inductive biases at the loss level is a simple yet effective approach for robust TSR, requiring only a loss modification with zero additional inference cost.

title: Task-Restricted Symmetries in Recurrent Weight Space
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Weight-Space Symmetries
summary:
Recurrent networks can contain substantial functional redundancy in
  weight space: changing a recurrent matrix may leave the input-output
  rollout nearly unchanged on a task distribution, while similar-scale
  changes can destroy the same behavior. We study this redundancy in
  one-layer tanh RNNs using ordered real Schur coordinates. The Schur
  form separates spectral blocks from directed nonnormal couplings,
  giving a diagnostic basis for structured ablations that keep the input
  and readout maps fixed. In a fixed-length copy task, selected
  nonnormal Schur couplings can be removed with little loss in some
  trained solutions, whereas other couplings are necessary for accurate
  autonomous replay. Across flip-flop, sine generation, and
  context-dependent integration, the loss-preserving ablation profile
  varies across tasks and trained solutions. These results identify
  candidate approximate functional invariances, not universal symmetries
  of recurrent weight space. Schur-coordinate ablations provide a
  practical diagnostic for which structured perturbations preserve a
  trained recurrent solution and which ones disrupt its computation.

AI.tldr: Using ordered real Schur coordinates, the paper reveals substantial functional redundancy in one-layer tanh RNNs: certain nonnormal couplings can be ablated with minimal performance loss in trained solutions, while others are critical; the ablation profile varies across tasks and solutions, identifying approximate functional invariances rather than universal symmetries.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify the functional redundancy in recurrent network weight space—where some weight changes preserve behavior while similar-scale changes destroy it—by providing a diagnostic framework.
AI.method: Applied ordered real Schur decomposition to one-layer tanh RNNs, separating spectral blocks from directed nonnormal couplings, then performed structured ablations (removing specific Schur couplings) while keeping input/readout maps fixed across multiple tasks.
AI.result: In a copy task, some nonnormal Schur couplings could be removed with little loss, while others were necessary; across flip-flop, sine generation, and context-dependent integration tasks, the ablation profile varied, showing redundancy is task- and solution-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Schur-coordinate ablations are a practical diagnostic tool for identifying which structured weight perturbations preserve or disrupt a trained recurrent computation, revealing approximate functional invariances rather than universal symmetries.

title: ART-VS: Adaptive Resolution Tiling for Vision Transformer Visual Servoing
summary: Visual servoing with self-supervised Vision Transformer (ViT) features enables training-free robotic positioning with strong generalization, but faces a fundamental trade-off between robustness and precision. Coarse patch-level descriptors provide stable correspondences yet limit positioning accuracy. Increasing image resolution improves precision but yields only marginal robustness gains - under perturbation, high-resolution processing improves convergence success rate from 76.6% to just 81.0% despite 12x more ViT patches. Therefore, we propose Adaptive Resolution Tiling Visual Servoing (ART-VS), a two-phase method that adapts feature granularity to servoing progress: a coarse phase at native ViT resolution for stable alignment, then a tiled high-resolution phase that restricts matching to local neighborhoods improving positioning accuracy. Without any task-specific training, ART-VS achieves 95.4% convergence under perturbation, outperforming standard and full-resolution ViT-based servoing by 18.8 and 14.4 percentage points. Over the former it reduces positioning error by 53%, while running at over 10x higher speed and 27% lower VRAM than the latter. We validate ART-VS across three ViT backbones and demonstrate real-world category-level grasping of unseen object instances, achieving 95/100 on transparent bottles and 98/100 on shoes. Code available under https://art-vs.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ART-VS proposes Adaptive Resolution Tiling to solve the robustness-precision trade-off in ViT-based visual servoing, achieving 95.4% convergence under perturbation with 53% lower positioning error and 10x faster speed than full-resolution methods, without any training.
AI.motivation: Existing ViT-based visual servoing faces a fundamental trade-off between robustness (coarse patch descriptors provide stable correspondences) and precision (limited by coarse resolution). Increasing resolution only marginally improves robustness (76.6%→81.0%) despite 12x more patches, motivating a more efficient adaptive approach.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: (1) Coarse phase using native ViT resolution for stable alignment, (2) Tiled high-resolution phase restricting matching to local neighborhoods for improved positioning accuracy, without task-specific training.
AI.result: 95.4% convergence under perturbation (vs 76.6% standard, 81.0% full-resolution), 53% lower positioning error, 10x higher speed and 27% lower VRAM than full-resolution. Validated on three ViT backbones and real-world grasping (95/100 transparent bottles, 98/100 shoes).
AI.conclusion: ART-VS effectively resolves the robustness-precision trade-off in visual servoing by adaptively adjusting feature granularity during servoing progress, achieving superior performance without any task-specific training.

title: Clinically Aligned Geometry Constraints for Robust IVUS Vessel Boundary Segmentation
summary: Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) lumen and external elastic membrane (EEM) segmentation is important for quantitative coronary plaque burden assessment. Errors in lumen or EEM delineation directly propagate to plaque area, plaque burden and geometric measurements. However, standard methods prioritising overlap scores often suffer from boundary drift and topology errors, leading to inaccurate clinical measurements. We present GeoCat, a geometry-consistent network that processes 5-frame IVUS clips using dual Cartesian-polar encoders with cross-domain attention and temporal fusion. A differentiable geometry consistency loss directly supervises clinically relevant descriptors including diameters, orientations, and cross-sectional areas. The model is trained on 12,242 annotated frames from 146 patients acquired with two commercial IVUS systems. We evaluate performance using both segmentation accuracy and plaque-relevant clinical metrics, including Dice/IoU, boundary measures(95HD (mm), ASSD), topology violation rate, and clinical geometry errors (dmax/dmin, angles, and areas). On our dataset, GeoCat achieves a Dice of 0.93, reduces 95HD to 0.14 mm, and lowers topology violations to 1.0%. Importantly, it significantly improves geometric fidelity, yielding diameter errors of 0.13-0.16 mm and angular errors of ~8 degrees, supporting reliable plaque burden quantification.

AI.tldr: GeoCat is a geometry-consistent network for IVUS lumen and EEM segmentation that uses dual Cartesian-polar encoders, cross-domain attention, and temporal fusion to improve geometric fidelity and reduce boundary drift, achieving 0.93 Dice, 0.14mm 95HD, and 1.0% topology violations on a large multi-system dataset.
AI.motivation: Standard IVUS segmentation methods prioritize overlap scores (Dice/IoU) but suffer from boundary drift and topology errors, which directly propagate into inaccurate clinical measurements of plaque burden, diameters, and angles.
AI.method: GeoCat processes 5-frame IVUS clips using dual Cartesian-polar encoders with cross-domain attention and temporal fusion, supervised by a differentiable geometry consistency loss that directly targets clinically relevant descriptors (diameters, orientations, areas).
AI.result: On 12,242 annotated frames from 146 patients (two commercial IVUS systems), GeoCat achieves 0.93 Dice, 0.14mm 95HD, 1.0% topology violations, diameter errors of 0.13-0.16mm, and angular errors of ~8 degrees.
AI.conclusion: GeoCat significantly improves geometric fidelity over standard overlap-based methods, enabling reliable plaque burden quantification for clinical use.

title: What Does the Weight Norm Control in Grokking? Logit-Scale Mediation under Cross-Entropy
comment: 16 papges, 10 tables and 4 figures. Code and data to reproduce all numbers, tables, and figures: https://github.com/ClevixLab/grokking-logit-scale
summary: Grokking, the delayed jump from memorization to generalization, is usually tied to the weight norm: a smaller norm generalizes sooner. We ask what the norm actually controls. Holding the weight norm fixed by clamping and varying only an output temperature, we slide the grokking delay across its entire norm-induced range under cross-entropy; matching the effective logit scale back to baseline recovers about 85% of the delay at two moduli. Across a grid of norms and temperatures the delay collapses onto the logit scale alone (R2 = 0.97), with the norm adding 1-2% beyond it. The effect is loss-dependent: under mean-squared error the logit scale is pinned and the norm acts through a different route. A memorization control, a float64 softmax-collapse audit, and a no-LayerNorm transformer point to the same channel. Forking arms from one identical state, the delay follows the held norm value and not the clamp operation, which closes a rescaling-artifact concern. The proximal variable is the logit scale and the softmax saturation it drives; the weight norm is only an upstream handle. All numbers, tables, and figures reproduce from released code and data.

AI.tldr: Weight norm's effect on grokking delay is mediated entirely by logit scale and softmax saturation, not by norm itself; under cross-entropy, controlling logit scale recovers ~85% of delay variation.
AI.motivation: To understand whether weight norm directly controls grokking (delayed generalization) or if it is a proxy for some other variable, by disentangling norm from logit scale.
AI.method: Clamped weight norm while varying output temperature (logit scale) across cross-entropy and MSE losses; measured grokking delay across a grid of norms/temperatures; used control experiments (float64 softmax-collapse audit, no-LayerNorm transformer, fork test).
AI.result: Logit scale alone explains 97% of delay variation (R²=0.97); weight norm adds only 1-2%. Under MSE, effect differs because logit scale is pinned. Fork test confirms norm value not clamp operation drives delay.
AI.conclusion: The proximal cause of grokking delay is logit scale (controlling softmax saturation); weight norm is an upstream manipulator of that scale.

title: GCNGrasp-VP: Affordance-Guided View Planning for Efficient Task-Oriented Grasping
summary: Task-oriented grasping performance degrades significantly when object views suffer from occlusions. Existing task-oriented grasping methods typically assume task-relevant regions are visible in the initial frame, while view planning approaches enable active perception but often ignore task semantics and rely on time-consuming scene reconstruction. To address these limitations, we present GCNGrasp-VP, an efficient framework integrating affordance field prediction with active view planning. Central to this framework is GCNGrasp-v2, a task-oriented grasp model that simultaneously supports grasp evaluation and affordance field prediction, achieving constant-time inference complexity. Leveraging this capability, our Affordance-guided View Planner (Affordance-VP) utilizes the affordance field as an information gain metric to guide camera observation of task-relevant regions without requiring scene reconstruction. View planning results show that our method significantly outperforms scene-uncertainty-driven baselines with only one view adjustment. Real-world validation further confirms substantial improvements in grasp success rates for single-object scenarios while maintaining millisecond-level computational latency. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Instinct323/GCNGrasp-VP.

AI.tldr: GCNGrasp-VP integrates task-oriented grasping with active view planning using affordance field prediction, achieving efficient grasp evaluation and view adjustment without scene reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Task-oriented grasping degrades under occlusions; existing methods assume visible task-relevant regions or rely on time-consuming scene reconstruction and ignore task semantics.
AI.method: GCNGrasp-v2 model supports both grasp evaluation and affordance field prediction with constant-time inference; Affordance-VP uses the affordance field as an information gain metric to guide camera views without reconstruction.
AI.result: Outperforms scene-uncertainty baselines with only one view adjustment; real-world validation shows improved grasp success rates in single-object scenarios with millisecond-level latency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed efficient framework effectively addresses occlusion in task-oriented grasping via affordance-guided active perception, enabling fast and accurate view planning.

title: Toward Training-Free Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection in 3D Medical Images: A Batch-Based Approach Using 2D Foundation Models
summary: Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) is attractive for medical imaging because clinical systems must handle heterogeneous acquisition protocols, changing patient populations, and pathologies for which annotated training data may be unavailable. Most existing zero-shot anomaly detection methods are designed for 2D images, and their direct extension to 3D medical volumes is limited by the scarcity of large-scale volumetric foundation models or by the difficulty of utilizing volumetric context. We propose CS3F, a training-free batch-based framework for ZSAD in 3D medical images using 2D foundation models. Each volume is decomposed along multiple anatomical axes and encoded slice-wise by a 2D vision transformer. These are then converted into localized volumetric tokens by pooling neighboring slice features. Anomaly scores are obtained from cross-subject mutual similarity: tokens that lack close analogues in other subjects are assigned higher anomaly scores. To reduce the attenuation of focal lesion signals caused by depth pooling, we introduce a coarse-to-fine tokenization strategy that enables fine-resolution volumetric scoring without exhaustive matching. CS3F is evaluated on brain MRI across metastases, glioma, and stroke, as well as validated on lung CT to test generalizability beyond atlas-aligned brain MRI. The results show that frozen 2D foundation models can support anomaly localization in 3D medical images, and that the benefit of fine tokenization depends strongly on lesion contrast and imaging modality.

AI.tldr: CS3F is a training-free, batch-based framework for zero-shot anomaly detection in 3D medical images using 2D foundation models, achieving strong results on brain MRI and lung CT by decomposing volumes along anatomical axes and using cross-subject similarity scoring with coarse-to-fine tokenization.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) is needed for medical imaging due to heterogeneous acquisition protocols, changing patient populations, and unavailability of annotated training data. Existing ZSAD methods are mostly for 2D images, and extending them to 3D is limited by lack of large-scale volumetric foundation models and difficulty in utilizing volumetric context.
AI.method: CS3F decomposes 3D volumes along multiple anatomical axes, encodes slices with a 2D ViT, converts them into localized volumetric tokens via pooling neighboring slice features, and assigns anomaly scores based on cross-subject mutual similarity (tokens lacking close analogues in other subjects get higher scores). A coarse-to-fine tokenization strategy reduces focal lesion signal attenuation.
AI.result: Evaluated on brain MRI (metastases, glioma, stroke) and lung CT. Frozen 2D foundation models effectively support anomaly localization in 3D. The benefit of fine tokenization depends on lesion contrast and imaging modality.
AI.conclusion: CS3F demonstrates that 2D foundation models can be effectively leveraged for 3D zero-shot anomaly detection without training, and the coarse-to-fine strategy improves detection sensitivity, though effectiveness varies with lesion characteristics.

title: Structured Representation Learning with Locally Linear Embeddings and Adaptive Feature Fusion
comment: Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (04/2026)
summary: Neuroscientific research has revealed that the brain encodes complex behaviors by leveraging structured, low-dimensional manifolds and dynamically fusing multiple sources of information through adaptive gating mechanisms. Inspired by these principles, we propose a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework that encourages the disentanglement of dynamics-specific and reward-specific features, drawing direct parallels to how neural circuits separate and integrate information for efficient decision-making. Our approach leverages locally linear embeddings (LLEs) to capture the intrinsic, locally linear structure inherent in many environments, mirroring the local smoothness observed in neural population activity, while concurrently deriving reward-specific features through the standard RL objective. An attention mechanism, analogous to cortical gating, adaptively fuses these complementary representations on a per-state basis. Experimental results on benchmark tasks demonstrate that our method, grounded in neuroscientific principles, improves learning efficiency and overall performance compared to conventional RL approaches, highlighting the benefits of explicitly modeling local state structures and adaptive feature selection as observed in biological systems.

AI.tldr: A neuroscience-inspired RL framework that disentangles dynamics and reward features using LLE and attention mechanisms, improving learning efficiency on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: To bridge neuroscience principles (low-dimensional manifolds, adaptive gating) with RL by explicitly modeling local state structures and adaptive feature fusion, which conventional RL approaches lack.
AI.method: 1) Uses Locally Linear Embeddings (LLEs) to capture intrinsic local structure of environments. 2) Derives reward-specific features via standard RL objective. 3) Employs an attention mechanism (analogous to cortical gating) to adaptively fuse these representations per state.
AI.result: On benchmark tasks, the proposed method outperforms conventional RL approaches in learning efficiency and overall performance.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling local state structures and adaptive feature selection—inspired by biological neural processing—improves RL performance.

title: Monocular 3D Occupancy Perception for Robots on Sidewalks via Hybrid 2D-3D Learning
summary: Sidewalks in the real world are crowded, cluttered, and less structured than roads, making 3D occupancy prediction a key ingredient for the safe navigation of mobile robots such as delivery bots and electric wheelchairs. Existing occupancy learning pipelines are largely designed for on-road autonomous driving and often train on large-scale paired LiDAR-RGB datasets with dense 3D supervision and multiple camera inputs, which are costly to collect and do not adequately capture sidewalk-specific characteristics. We propose WalkOCC, a hybrid Ray-marching monocular 3D occupancy perception framework for robots operating on sidewalks. WalkOCC explicitly couples geometric grounding from LiDAR-RGB paired data with scalable learning from large-scale unpaired monocular images. It bootstraps pseudo occupancy supervision from paired sequences and jointly learns image-level representations on additional 2D-only data. It yields stable optimization and improved generalization without requiring costly 3D occupancy annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent gains in prediction accuracy, fine-grained segmentation of subtle urban structures such as curbs and gutters, and robustness to environmental and cross-embodiment shifts compared with self-supervised image-based baselines. To facilitate evaluation and benchmarking, we also introduce Sidewalk3D, a large-scale sidewalk perception dataset with LiDAR-camera paired sequences collected across multiple locations and time periods, along with 3D semantic occupancy annotations for evaluation. Code and data will be made available.

AI.tldr: WalkOCC is a monocular 3D occupancy prediction framework for sidewalk robots that combines LiDAR-RGB data with unpaired monocular images to achieve robust perception without costly 3D annotations, validated on a new Sidewalk3D dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D occupancy prediction methods are designed for on-road autonomous driving, requiring expensive paired LiDAR-RGB data with dense 3D supervision and multiple cameras, and fail to capture sidewalk-specific characteristics like curbs and gutters, which are critical for safe robot navigation on sidewalks.
AI.method: WalkOCC uses a hybrid Ray-marching monocular 3D occupancy framework that couples geometric grounding from LiDAR-RGB paired data with scalable learning from large-scale unpaired monocular images. It bootstraps pseudo occupancy supervision from paired sequences and jointly learns image-level representations on additional 2D-only data.
AI.result: Consistent gains in prediction accuracy, fine-grained segmentation of subtle urban structures (curbs, gutters), and robustness to environmental and cross-embodiment shifts compared to self-supervised image-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach yields stable optimization and improved generalization without requiring costly 3D occupancy annotations, and the new Sidewalk3D dataset facilitates future benchmarking for sidewalk robot perception.

title: SMART: A Flexible, Interpretable, and Scalable Spatio-temporal Brain Atlas from High-Resolution Imaging Data
summary: We introduce SMART, a framework for learning a flexible, interpretable, and scalable spatio-temporal brain atlas from longitudinal high-resolution 3D medical images. Existing approaches to spatio-temporal atlas construction rely on black-box generative models that lack flexibility, limit interpretability, and struggle to scale to high-dimensional data. SMART addresses these challenges by learning a continuous disease-time atlas that decouples global group-wise disease dynamics from their patient-specific anatomical manifestation. Guided by anatomically inspired priors, SMART models interpretable global trajectories of regional progression along a shared disease timeline through region-specific differential equations. Global trajectories are further personalized to individual anatomies via dense diffeomorphic displacements parameterized by a flexible and scalable multi-scale Neural Cellular Automata. Evaluated on five longitudinal MRI datasets in Alzheimer's disease (ADNI-1/GO/2, OASIS-3, AIBL; > 1,300 subjects), SMART produces anatomically meaningful predictions of disease progression and achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy and improved temporal consistency over adversarial and diffusion baselines. Our approach establishes a new paradigm for flexible, interpretable, and scalable modeling of spatio-temporal change in high-dimensional medical image time-series.

AI.tldr: SMART is a framework for learning flexible, interpretable, and scalable spatio-temporal brain atlases from longitudinal 3D MRI data, combining continuous disease-time modeling, region-specific differential equations, and Neural Cellular Automata to achieve state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy for Alzheimer's disease progression.
AI.motivation: Existing spatio-temporal atlas construction methods rely on black-box generative models that lack flexibility, limit interpretability, and cannot scale to high-dimensional data.
AI.method: SMART learns a continuous disease-time atlas that decouples global group-wise disease dynamics from patient-specific anatomy using anatomically inspired priors, region-specific differential equations for global trajectories, and dense diffeomorphic displacements via multi-scale Neural Cellular Automata for personalization.
AI.result: Evaluated on five longitudinal MRI datasets (ADNI-1/GO/2, OASIS-3, AIBL; >1,300 subjects), SMART produces anatomically meaningful predictions, achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy, and improved temporal consistency over adversarial and diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: SMART establishes a new paradigm for flexible, interpretable, and scalable modeling of spatio-temporal change in high-dimensional medical image time-series.

title: The Illusion of Improvement: Reject Inference Strategies in Credit Scoring
summary: Reject inference methods are widely used to mitigate survival bias in credit scoring, yet their effectiveness remains poorly understood. We systematically evaluate several such methods and uncover a structural failure mode: in a natural retraining cycle, models whose accuracy improves while recall collapses create an illusion of improvement that leads practitioners to believe the system is getting better when, in fact, its rejection quality -- the ability to correctly screen out defaulters -- is deteriorating. We then propose a controlled exploration strategy that breaks the feedback loop without statistical assumptions: the lender deliberately approves a fraction of rejected applicants and observes their true outcomes. We show that accuracy and rejection quality give opposite recommendations on whether to explore: accuracy favors no exploration, while rejection quality improves with it, confirming that standard evaluation metrics are misleading under selection bias. Even minimal exploration rates (2--5\%) prove sufficient in our experiments to diagnose the severity of the feedback loop at near-zero cost. Our findings are consistent across two machine learning methods and three real-world datasets, and suggest that standard evaluation protocols are inadequate for assessing models trained under survival bias.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that reject inference methods in credit scoring create a deceptive feedback loop where accuracy improves but recall collapses, making models seem better while actually worsening rejection quality. The authors propose a simple exploration strategy (approving 2-5% of rejected applicants) to break this loop and diagnose the problem without statistical assumptions.
AI.motivation: Standard reject inference methods for credit scoring suffer from survival bias, and their effectiveness is poorly understood. Practitioners may believe their models are improving when they are actually deteriorating due to a structural failure mode in the retraining cycle.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of reject inference methods revealing a failure mode in retraining cycles. Proposal of a controlled exploration strategy where a fraction (2-5%) of rejected applicants are deliberately approved to observe true outcomes, breaking the feedback loop without statistical assumptions.
AI.result: Accuracy and rejection quality give opposing recommendations on exploration. Even minimal exploration (2-5%) suffices to diagnose feedback loop severity at near-zero cost. Findings are consistent across two ML methods and three real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Standard evaluation protocols are inadequate for assessing models trained under survival bias. The proposed exploration strategy effectively breaks the harmful feedback loop.

title: Viking Hill Dataset: A Lidar-Radar-Camera Dataset for Detection and Segmentation in Forest Scenes
summary: Autonomous robots operating under forest canopies need robust perception of trees and surrounding vegetation across varying seasonal conditions. Existing forestry datasets provide lidar or camera data with per-tree annotations, but none include co-registered 4D imaging radar -- a modality of growing interest for its resilience to visual degradation, surface contamination, and vegetation occlusion. We introduce a multi-sensor forest dataset collected by a mobile robot equipped with a high-resolution FMCW imaging radar, lidar, RGB camera, IMU, and RTK-GNSS. The site was recorded in two sessions under contrasting vegetation states, and 3D cuboid annotations -- including per-tree diameter estimates -- provide shared semantic labels across all three perception modalities. Furthermore, we provide baseline results for semantic segmentation of the radar and lidar point clouds using MinkowskiUNet. Radar achieves IoU scores competitive with lidar for dominant classes (ground 91%, canopy 86%) while lagging on geometrically fine structures such as tree trunks (56% vs. 74%). A cross-modality analysis further compares lidar and radar trunk segmentation against an RGB detection model, and a diameter-stratified evaluation reveals how trunk segmentation quality varies with tree size. Beyond segmentation, the co-registered multi-modal data and RTK-GNSS-aided reference positioning support research in mapping, localization, and sensor fusion under canopy. The dataset and annotation tools are publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multi-sensor forest dataset collected by a robot under two seasonal conditions, featuring co-registered 4D imaging radar, lidar, RGB camera, IMU, and RTK-GNSS with 3D cuboid tree annotations. Baseline semantic segmentation results show radar performs competitively with lidar on ground and canopy but worse on tree trunks.
AI.motivation: Existing forestry datasets lack co-registered 4D imaging radar data, despite radar's growing importance for robot perception under visual degradation, surface contamination, and vegetation occlusion in forest environments.
AI.method: A mobile robot collected multi-sensor data (FMCW imaging radar, lidar, RGB camera, IMU, RTK-GNSS) in two sessions under different vegetation states. 3D cuboid annotations with per-tree diameter estimates were provided across all modalities. Baseline semantic segmentation used MinkowskiUNet.
AI.result: Radar achieved competitive IoU with lidar for ground (91%) and canopy (86%), but lagged on tree trunks (56% vs 74%). Cross-modality and diameter-stratified analyses revealed segmentation quality variation by tree size.
AI.conclusion: The co-registered multi-modal dataset supports research in mapping, localization, sensor fusion, and semantic segmentation under forest canopy, with public availability of data and annotation tools.

title: SpectralDiT: Timestep-Conditioned Spectral Residual Correction for Flow-Matching DiTs
summary: We propose SpectralDiT, a lightweight modification to flow-matching Diffusion Transformers that adds timestep-conditioned spectral correction to the MLP residual branch. The module decomposes each residual update into low- and high-frequency components on the patch-token grid, then learns a zero-initialized additive gate so the model initially matches the baseline DiT. On CIFAR-10 pixel-space generation, SpectralDiT improves FID from 20.78 to 19.71 at patch size 1 and reduces the radial Fourier spectrum gap. Furthermore, we scale our method to latent diffusion on ImageNet-100. With 0.6% additional theoretical FLOPs and 1.36% additional parameters, SpectralDiT improves latent flow-matching, achieving an 8.7% relative FID reduction under classifier-free guidance (CFG 2.0). All reported results are averaged over five seeds. Ablations and gate visualizations on CIFAR-10 reveal stable block-specific spectral correction patterns.

AI.tldr: SpectralDiT adds a lightweight spectral correction module to Diffusion Transformers that decomposes residual updates into low/high-frequency components, improving FID scores on CIFAR-10 (20.78→19.71) and ImageNet-100 (8.7% relative FID reduction) with negligible computational overhead.
AI.motivation: To improve flow-matching Diffusion Transformers by introducing spectral (frequency-domain) awareness into the residual updates, which can reduce the radial Fourier spectrum gap and enhance generation quality.
AI.method: A zero-initialized, timestep-conditioned additive gate is added to the MLP residual branch that decomposes updates into low- and high-frequency components on the patch-token grid, then learns to correct them spectrally.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10: FID improved from 20.78 to 19.71 at patch size 1. On ImageNet-100 latent diffusion: 8.7% relative FID reduction (CFG 2.0) with only 0.6% extra FLOPs and 1.36% extra parameters. Results averaged over five seeds.
AI.conclusion: SpectralDiT offers an efficient and effective spectral correction mechanism for DiTs, achieving better generation quality with minimal overhead, revealing stable block-specific spectral correction patterns.

title: SFT Overtraining Predicts Rank Inversion via Entropy Collapse Under RLVR
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures. Accepted at the Deep Learning for Code (DL4C) Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: The standard heuristic of selecting the SFT checkpoint with the highest pass@1 for GRPO can fail when SFT compresses the rollout distribution. For binary rewards, the expected within group advantage variance is $p(1{-}p)(g{-}1)/g$; when early GRPO drives $p$ below $p^*(g)$, most groups have identical rewards and provide no group relative signal. We study SFT depth ladders for Qwen2.5-Coder-3B and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B. We test Qwen2.5-Coder-3B across five depths and three seeds, and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B across four matched depths and three seeds. On Qwen, pre RL pass@1 rises with SFT depth, but peak GRPO pass@10 falls from $0.806$ to $0.481$ (3 seed mean, $n{=}20$); pre RL entropy is positively associated with the GRPO outcome ($ρ{=}{+}0.69$). On DeepSeek, pass@1 remains far above $p^*(8){=}0.083$, and GRPO outcomes compress rather than invert. A two stage diagnostic, combining pre RL entropy triage with an early GRPO entropy monitor, flags high risk checkpoints and can stop failing runs early. Simple KL to reference regularisation and label smoothing variants do not rescue the collapsed Qwen checkpoint in our setting, suggesting the failure is not a trivial GRPO hyperparameter artefact.

AI.tldr: Selecting the SFT checkpoint with highest pass@1 for GRPO can fail when SFT compresses rollout distribution. The paper studies this phenomenon, finding that pre-RL entropy positively correlates with GRPO success, and proposes a two-stage diagnostic to identify and stop failing runs early.
AI.motivation: The standard heuristic of selecting the SFT checkpoint with the highest pass@1 for GRPO can fail when SFT compresses the rollout distribution, leading to poor GRPO outcomes. The authors aim to understand this failure mode and develop diagnostics to detect it early.
AI.method: The authors study SFT depth ladders for Qwen2.5-Coder-3B (5 depths, 3 seeds) and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B (4 depths, 3 seeds). They analyze pre-RL entropy, pass@1, pass@10, and propose a two-stage diagnostic combining pre-RL entropy triage with early GRPO entropy monitoring.
AI.result: On Qwen, pre-RL pass@1 rises with SFT depth, but peak GRPO pass@10 falls from 0.806 to 0.481 (3 seed mean, n=20); pre-RL entropy is positively associated with GRPO outcome (ρ=+0.69). On DeepSeek, pass@1 remains far above p*(8)=0.083 and GRPO outcomes compress rather than invert. Simple KL regularization and label smoothing do not rescue the collapsed Qwen checkpoint.
AI.conclusion: The SFT checkpoint with highest pass@1 is not always optimal for GRPO. Pre-RL entropy is a useful predictor of GRPO success. The two-stage diagnostic can flag high-risk checkpoints and stop failing runs early. The failure is not a trivial GRPO hyperparameter artifact.

title: HT-Bench: Benchmarking and Learning Dexterous Full-Hand Tactile Representations with Egocentric Vision
summary: Establishing a universal benchmark for tactile representation learning in robotic manipulation remains challenging due to the diversity of tactile sensor designs, data formats, and robot embodiments. Rather than seeking to establish such, we explore a scalable and promising direction for future development: egocentric vision paired with full-hand tactile data. To this end, we introduce \textbf{HT-Bench}, a large-scale multi-task benchmark for dexterous full-hand tactile sensing, comprising 10M RGB frames and 7.8M tactile frames collected across 226 tasks. HT-Bench evaluates tactile representations from three key perspectives: whether they encode meaningful contact geometry, whether they can align tactile observations with visual information, and whether they generalize to unseen tasks. To assess these capabilities, HT-Bench includes four tasks: fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval, masked tactile inpainting, vision-to-tactile synthesis, and multimodal tactile frame prediction. We further propose \textbf{HandTouch}, a vector-quantized vision--tactile encoder that learns tactile representations through progressive spatial, cross-modal, and temporal training. Across HT-Bench, HandTouch consistently outperforms representative tactile encoder baselines, improving Recall@5 on fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval from 74.65\% to 85.23\%, reducing RMSE on masked tactile inpainting from 0.022 to 0.010, and increasing OOD cIoU on vision-to-tactile synthesis from 0.628 to 0.705. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of HandTouch and suggest that large-scale egocentric full-hand tactile data provides a scalable basis for evaluating and advancing tactile representation learning in dexterous manipulation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces HT-Bench, a large-scale benchmark with 10M RGB and 7.8M tactile frames across 226 dexterous manipulation tasks, and proposes HandTouch, a vector-quantized vision-tactile encoder that learns tactile representations through progressive spatial, cross-modal, and temporal training, consistently outperforming baselines across four evaluation tasks.
AI.motivation: The authors argue that establishing a universal benchmark for tactile representation learning is difficult due to diversity in sensor designs, data formats, and robot embodiments. Instead of pursuing such a universal standard, they explore a scalable alternative: egocentric vision paired with full-hand tactile data for dexterous manipulation.
AI.method: Two main contributions: (1) HT-Bench, a large-scale multi-task benchmark comprising 10M RGB frames and 7.8M tactile frames across 226 tasks, evaluating tactile representations on four tasks (fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval, masked tactile inpainting, vision-to-tactile synthesis, multimodal tactile frame prediction). (2) HandTouch, a vector-quantized vision-tactile encoder that learns representations through progressive spatial, cross-modal, and temporal training stages.
AI.result: HandTouch outperforms baselines: Recall@5 on tactile similarity retrieval from 74.65% to 85.23%, RMSE on masked tactile inpainting from 0.022 to 0.010, OOD cIoU on vision-to-tactile synthesis from 0.628 to 0.705.
AI.conclusion: HandTouch demonstrates effectiveness, and large-scale egocentric full-hand tactile data provides a scalable basis for evaluating and advancing tactile representation learning in dexterous manipulation.

title: SAMA: Semantic Anchor-aligned Augmentation for Unified Low-Resource Multimodal Information Extraction
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
summary: Multimodal Information Extraction (MIE)-covering tasks such as Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER), Relation Extraction (MRE), and Event Extraction (MEE)-is essential for understanding multimedia content but remains constrained by severe data scarcity. Although data augmentation is a promising remedy, existing approaches are impeded by coarse cross-modal alignment and fragmented, task-specific designs that fail to exploit shared semantic knowledge. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Semantic Anchor-aligned Multimodal Augmentation (SAMA), a unified framework for generating high-fidelity, task-aware synthetic data. SAMA constructs structured semantic anchors from ground-truth labels to guide a Collaborative Multi-Experts Multimodal Large Language Model (CME-MLLM), which integrates a Universal Adapter for shared semantics with Task-Specific Adapters to produce diverse yet constraint-compliant textual samples. For image synthesis, SAMA employs an Anchor-Preserving Diffusion mechanism that uses anchor-weighted prompts and latent conditioning to maintain critical semantic anchors while diversifying visual contexts. To eliminate the need for manual verification, SAMA further introduces a Dual-Constraint Filtering module that selects synthetic samples based on both cross-modal consistency and anchor fidelity. Extensive experiments across benchmark datasets for MNER, MRE, and MEE demonstrate that SAMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art augmentation baselines under both fully supervised and low-resource settings, underscoring its versatility, robustness, and effectiveness.

AI.tldr: SAMA is a unified data augmentation framework for Multimodal Information Extraction that uses semantic anchors to guide a collaborative multi-expert MLLM and anchor-preserving diffusion to generate high-fidelity, task-aware synthetic data, consistently outperforming baselines across MNER, MRE, and MEE tasks.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Information Extraction suffers from severe data scarcity, and existing augmentation methods are limited by coarse cross-modal alignment and fragmented, task-specific designs that fail to exploit shared semantic knowledge.
AI.method: SAMA constructs structured semantic anchors from ground-truth labels, uses a Collaborative Multi-Experts MLLM (CME-MLLM) with Universal and Task-Specific Adapters for text generation, employs Anchor-Preserving Diffusion for image synthesis, and applies Dual-Constraint Filtering for quality assurance.
AI.result: SAMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art augmentation baselines on MNER, MRE, and MEE benchmarks under both fully supervised and low-resource settings, demonstrating versatility, robustness, and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: SAMA provides a unified, scalable solution to data scarcity in multimodal IE by leveraging semantic anchors and multi-expert collaboration, eliminating manual verification needs while generating high-quality synthetic data.

title: Quantum Annealing Enhanced Reinforcement Learning for Accurate Remaining Useful Lifetime Prediction
summary: Remaining useful life (RUL) estimation is central to predictive maintenance, where an unplanned failure can cost far more than the asset itself. Statistical degradation models miss the strong nonlinearity of real systems, and data-driven models often converge to suboptimal solutions in high-dimensional, non-convex search spaces.
