We validate the approach on a tracking-detector toy model with controlled layer shifts and demonstrate its application on high-granularity Geant4-simulated calorimeter data with cell-wise aging effects. The method recovers aging coefficients for individual cells with correlation to ground truth and improves agreement between calibrated and reference energy-sum distributions, while exhibiting the expected degradation at increasing channel-to-channel noise levels. These results indicate that adversarial distribution matching can serve as a data-driven component of calibration strategies in settings where direct labels for degradation parameters are unavailable.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Wasserstein-GAN-inspired method for unsupervised calibration of sensor systems, learning transformation parameters that map degraded detector response back to a nominal reference distribution without requiring direct labels.
AI.motivation: Sensor motion and aging degrade data quality and stability of downstream methods, but direct labels for degradation parameters are often unavailable, motivating an unsupervised calibration approach.
AI.method: A Wasserstein-GAN framework where the generator acts as a learnable calibration transformation with interpretable weights (representing sought parameters), and the critic provides distributional distance via Wasserstein objective.
AI.result: Validated on a tracking-detector toy model with layer shifts and Geant4-simulated calorimeter data with aging effects. The method recovers aging coefficients with correlation to ground truth, improves energy-sum distribution agreement, and shows expected degradation with increased noise.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial distribution matching can serve as a data-driven calibration strategy in settings where direct labels for degradation parameters are unavailable.

title: Modeling Branches for Active Manipulation using Iterative Parameter Estimation
summary: This study presents a method for modeling diverse plant branches by iteratively estimating material parameters to support delicate branch manipulation. Branch manipulation is necessary in agricultural robotics for plant repositioning, stabilizing, and clearing visual obstructions in dense foliage. The proposed method builds a tetrahedral branch model from point-cloud data and simulates its behavior using the finite element method. Using real observed deformation data, it iteratively estimates branch parameters and then computes an optimal path with a deformation-aware motion planner to move and stabilize branches within another robot's field of view. Across 30 trials on branches with varying geometries and material properties, the proposed method reduced the deformation energy by 35.69% while increasing the path length by 8.10% on average.

AI.tldr: A method for modeling and manipulating plant branches using iterative material parameter estimation, FEM simulation, and deformation-aware motion planning, achieving 35.69% reduction in deformation energy with 8.10% longer paths.
AI.motivation: Branch manipulation is needed in agricultural robotics for repositioning, stabilizing, and clearing visual obstructions in dense foliage, but delicate handling requires accurate material modeling.
AI.method: Builds tetrahedral branch models from point-cloud data, uses FEM for simulation, iteratively estimates material parameters from observed deformation, then computes optimal paths with a deformation-aware motion planner.
AI.result: Across 30 trials on branches with varying geometries and material properties, the method reduced deformation energy by 35.69% while increasing path length by 8.10% on average.
AI.conclusion: The iterative parameter estimation combined with deformation-aware planning enables more delicate branch manipulation, trading slightly longer paths for substantially reduced deformation.

title: Learning to Distort: Weakly-Supervised Image Quality Transfer for Prostate DWI Correction
summary: Single-shot echo-planar prostate diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is frequently complicated by geometric distortions, which impact the ability to derive reliable diagnoses from such images. Developing automated correction methods is challenged by the absence of paired distorted and undistorted clinical scans. In this paper, we first propose a novel weakly-supervised image quality transfer (IQT) framework from undistorted to distorted images that utilizes image quality assessment (IQA) signals to supervise the transfer process. Unlike traditional methods that require expensive, voxel-wise paired data or resort to developing unpaired algorithms, our approach utilizes image-level quality labels (here, distorted vs. undistorted) to establish latent quality prototypes within a pre-trained feature space. Recognizing that simulating realistic distortions is more reliable than direct unpaired correction, we describe a weakly-supervised prototype flow matching algorithm to explicitly regularize generative trajectories towards distorted prototypes, producing realistic susceptibility artifacts that mimic clinical degradations. By synthesizing these realistic pairs, we enable a second IQT model to be trained in the forward direction for distortion correction. Experimental results demonstrate that our generated images successfully mimic the diagnostic interference of real-world artifacts, which leads to more capable distortion correction IQT models. In addition to qualitative comparisons, we also conduct exhaustive quantitative evaluations that compare our approach with existing unpaired approaches (e.g., CycleGAN, UNIT-DDPM, and OT-FM) - as either forward or reverse alternatives - by assessing clinical downstream task performance in PI-RADS and Gleason score classification, using both in-distribution and external data sets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a weakly-supervised Image Quality Transfer (IQT) framework for correcting geometric distortions in prostate DWI, using image-level quality labels and prototype flow matching to generate realistic distorted images, then training a correction model on these synthetic pairs.
AI.motivation: Geometric distortions in single-shot echo-planar prostate DWI images hinder reliable diagnosis. Existing correction methods are limited because there are no paired distorted and undistorted clinical images available for supervised training.
AI.method: (1) A weakly-supervised IQT framework using image-level quality labels (distorted vs. undistorted) to establish latent quality prototypes. (2) A prototype flow matching algorithm that regularizes generative trajectories toward distorted prototypes to simulate realistic susceptibility artifacts. (3) A second IQT model trained on these synthetic pairs for forward distortion correction.
AI.result: Generated images successfully mimic diagnostic interference of real-world artifacts. The approach outperforms existing unpaired methods (CycleGAN, UNIT-DDPM, OT-FM) in both forward and reverse settings, validated through PI-RADS and Gleason score classification on in-distribution and external datasets.
AI.conclusion: Simulating realistic distortions weakly-supervised is more reliable than direct unpaired correction, leading to more capable distortion correction IQT models for clinical use.

title: Fair Cognitive Impairment Detection Through Unlearning
summary: Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) is a medical condition characterized by a noticeable decline in memory, language, or thinking abilities. MCI detection from spontaneous speech is promising for scalable screening. However, learned models often exploit demographic cues correlated with labels, resulting in a large performance gap across subgroups. We present a multimodal framework that combines (i) cross-model fusion between modalities (speech, text, and image), and (ii) unlearning using gradient reversal that discourages the shared embedding from encoding task-irrelevant demographic attributes. Evaluated on the multilingual benchmarks TAUKADIAL and PREPARE, our method outperforms the state-of-the-art multilingual and multimodal baseline in MCI classification while substantially reducing the performance gap across patient subgroups (sex and language). We further analyze transfer across datasets, showing that demographic unlearning helps learn more robust representations for MCI detection.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multimodal framework combining cross-model fusion (speech, text, image) with demographic unlearning via gradient reversal to detect Mild Cognitive Impairment from spontaneous speech, achieving state-of-the-art results on multilingual benchmarks while reducing performance disparities across sex and language subgroups.
AI.motivation: MCI detection from speech is promising for scalable screening, but learned models often exploit demographic cues correlated with labels, causing large performance gaps across subgroups (e.g., sex, language).
AI.method: A multimodal framework combining (i) cross-model fusion between speech, text, and image modalities, and (ii) demographic unlearning using gradient reversal to discourage shared embeddings from encoding task-irrelevant demographic attributes.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art multilingual and multimodal baselines on TAUKADIAL and PREPARE benchmarks in MCI classification, while substantially reducing the performance gap across sex and language subgroups.
AI.conclusion: Demographic unlearning helps learn more robust representations for MCI detection, and the approach shows effective transfer across datasets.

title: Do as I Do: Dexterous Manipulation Data from Everyday Human Videos
summary: How can we scalably generate data for robotic manipulation, especially on human-like platforms such as dexterous multi-fingered hands? Learning from human videos has recently emerged as a likely answer to this question. However, difficulties in estimating hand-object interaction and crossing the human-to-robot embodiment gap have hindered the adoption of abundant monocular RGB-only human videos as the primary source of robot manipulation data. In this work, we present DO AS I DO, an algorithm to reconstruct and retarget monocular RGB human videos to multi-fingered dexterous robotic hands. DO AS I DO reconstructs hand-object interactions from various egocentric and exocentric in-the-wild video sources. The algorithm then retargets these hand-object interaction estimates into a sequence of actions executable in the real world, yielding robot-complete manipulation data from disparate human videos. Overall, DO AS I DO outperforms previous state of the art in estimating hand-object interactions and extracting dexterous manipulation trajectories from RGB videos, as we show in experiments on datasets with ground truths and on a dataset of video clips collected online. Our experiments enable us to propose an efficacy playbook for practitioners collecting human data for manipulation.

AI.tldr: DO AS I DO is an algorithm that reconstructs and retargets monocular RGB human videos into executable dexterous robotic hand manipulation data, overcoming the human-to-robot embodiment gap and outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: To scalably generate robotic manipulation data for human-like platforms (e.g., dexterous multi-fingered hands) by leveraging abundant monocular RGB human videos, despite challenges in hand-object interaction estimation and embodiment gap crossing.
AI.method: The algorithm reconstructs hand-object interactions from egocentric and exocentric in-the-wild RGB videos, then retargets these estimates into real-world executable action sequences for multi-fingered dexterous robotic hands.
AI.result: Outperforms previous state-of-the-art in hand-object interaction estimation and dexterous manipulation trajectory extraction from RGB videos, validated on ground-truth datasets and online video clip collections.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an efficacy playbook for practitioners collecting human manipulation data, demonstrating that monocular RGB human videos can serve as a viable primary source for robot manipulation data.

title: Bridging Single Distortion Artifacts and Mmultifactorial Clinical Quality: Few-shot Biparametric MRI Quality Assessment via Distortion-trained Prototypical Networks
summary: Clinical prostate multi-parametric MRI relies heavily on high-quality diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), yet reading DWI is frequently compromised by geometric distortion, often caused by rectal air. Assessing quality via the PI-QUAL scoring system is an emerging clinical standard, but it is subjective, time-consuming and suffers from a class imbalance where low-quality cases are diverse and relatively scarce. Using the PRIME clinical trial as an example, there are $6\%$ images with PI-QUAL scores lower than 4, $87\%$ of DWI issues are due to distortion. Many of the other clinical quality issues are under-represented. To address this common dual-scarcity of annotated clinical data, we propose a few-shot biparametric prototypical network for automated image quality assessment (IQA). Our framework utilizes a dual-branch 3D ResNet to fuse T2-weighted and DWI features, providing anatomical context to distinguish true morphology from distortion. To handle real-world heterogeneity, we introduce feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) and a gradient reversal layer (GRL) to align feature distributions conditioned on varying b-values while suppressing acquisition-related biases. We demonstrate that a model meta-trained solely on comparatively objective, readily obtainable distortion labels can effectively adapt to predicting complex, multi-factorial clinical quality scores such as PI-QUAL using only five representative samples. Experimental results on two datasets show that our method significantly outperforms few-shot learning baselines for this challenging IQA task, offering a practically feasible and data-efficient solution for standardizing prostate MRI quality control in clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: A few-shot biparametric prototypical network is proposed for automated prostate MRI quality assessment, using distortion labels for meta-training and adapting to predict PI-QUAL scores with only 5 samples.
AI.motivation: Manual PI-QUAL scoring is subjective, time-consuming, and suffers from class imbalance—low-quality cases are rare and diverse; 87% of DWI issues are distortion, but other quality issues are under-represented.
AI.method: A dual-branch 3D ResNet fuses T2-weighted and DWI features; Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) and Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL) align distributions across b-values and suppress acquisition biases; meta-learned on distortion labels then adapted with 5 PI-QUAL samples.
AI.result: Outperforms few-shot learning baselines on two datasets; effectively adapts from distortion labels to multi-factorial PI-QUAL scoring with minimal annotated samples.
AI.conclusion: Offers a data-efficient, practically feasible solution for standardizing prostate MRI quality control in clinical workflows.

title: Towards Anomaly Detection on Relational Data
summary: Relational databases are widely used for managing structured data in real-world systems. Detecting anomalies from such relational data is crucial for identifying fraud, risks, and abnormal behaviors, yet remains under-explored. The key challenges lie in the intrinsic complexity of relational data: multi-table attributes are high-dimensional and heterogeneous, making sparse abnormal clues easy to overwhelm by normal or irrelevant information; and anomalies may further manifest as abnormal connection patterns across different foreign-key relations, which existing tabular and graph anomaly detection methods are ill-suited to capture. To address them, we propose RelAD, a reconstruction-based framework that captures anomalies from both attribute and relational edge reconstruction. RelAD contains two core modules: conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction, which suppresses redundant multi-table attributes and emphasizes abnormal semantic blocks, and dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction, which detects relation-specific abnormal connections from both intrinsic and behavioral entity profiles. The resulting attribute and relational signals are integrated through a lightweight fusion module to produce the final anomaly score. We further construct 6 benchmark datasets with systematic anomalies, on which extensive experiments show that RelAD consistently outperforms other baselines while achieving competitive efficiency.

AI.tldr: RelAD is a reconstruction-based framework for anomaly detection in relational databases, using conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction and dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction to capture anomalies from both attributes and connection patterns, outperforming baselines on 6 benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Relational databases are widely used but anomaly detection in such data remains under-explored. Existing tabular and graph methods fail to handle high-dimensional heterogeneous multi-table attributes and abnormal foreign-key connection patterns.
AI.method: RelAD is a reconstruction-based framework with two core modules: (1) conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction to suppress redundant multi-table attributes and emphasize abnormal semantic blocks; (2) dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction to detect relation-specific abnormal connections from intrinsic and behavioral entity profiles. A lightweight fusion module integrates attribute and relational signals for final anomaly scoring.
AI.result: Constructed 6 benchmark datasets with systematic anomalies. Extensive experiments show RelAD consistently outperforms other baselines while achieving competitive efficiency.
AI.conclusion: RelAD effectively addresses the unique challenges of anomaly detection in relational databases by jointly modeling attribute and relational edge anomalies, outperforming existing methods.

title: Zero-Shot Long-Horizon Dexterous Manipulation via Multi-View 3D-Grounded VLM Reasoning
summary: We present a zero-shot framework for long-horizon dexterous manipulation that grounds language instructions into executable 3D task plans from calibrated multi-view RGB images. Rather than training an end-to-end policy, our system uses a vision-language model (VLM) to produce reference-frame task grounding and primitive-level 2D keypoints, then lifts them into 3D via multi-view fusion. This lifting combines triangulation of view-wise VLM groundings with reference-view ray voting, which searches along a semantic camera ray for geometrically consistent candidates across neighboring views. The resulting 3D keypoints support both pick-and-place and tool-use: for tool-use, we retrieve an object-centric atomic action corresponding to the inferred skill category and align its stored 6D tool trajectory to the scene; for dexterous execution, we expand the lifted grasp keypoint into a task-conditioned grasp affordance region and generate feasible grasp-motion pairs with an arm-hand motion generator. Real-world experiments show improved 3D grounding accuracy and execution reliability over single-view RGB-D grounding and fine-tuned VLA baselines. We further demonstrate long-horizon manipulation through closed-loop status verification and replan, enabling zero-shot execution on unseen objects and tool-use tasks in novel scenes.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot framework for long-horizon dexterous manipulation that uses a VLM to ground language instructions into 3D task plans from multi-view RGB images, achieving improved 3D grounding and execution reliability over baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation without end-to-end training, by grounding language instructions into executable 3D task plans from multi-view RGB images.
AI.method: Uses VLM for reference-frame task grounding and primitive-level 2D keypoints, lifts them to 3D via multi-view fusion combining triangulation and reference-view ray voting. For tool-use, retrieves atomic actions and aligns 6D tool trajectories; for dexterous execution, expands grasp keypoints into affordance regions and generates grasp-motion pairs with an arm-hand motion generator.
AI.result: Improved 3D grounding accuracy and execution reliability over single-view RGB-D grounding and fine-tuned VLA baselines in real-world experiments. Demonstrates zero-shot execution on unseen objects and tool-use tasks in novel scenes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed zero-shot framework enables effective long-horizon dexterous manipulation through multi-view fusion and VLM-based grounding, outperforming existing single-view and fine-tuned approaches.

title: Test-Time Adaptation in Optical Coherence Tomography Using Trajectory-Aligned Time-Independent Flow
summary: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is essential in ophthalmology, but inconsistent image quality especially in low-cost devices hinders automated analysis. To address this, we introduce a flow-matching-based test-time adaptation method that generates high-quality surrogate images from noisy inputs. Typically, domain gaps between test and training data cause pixel distribution mismatches during the denoising process. We overcome this by matching the test image's histogram to synthetic reference trajectories, successfully aligning the input with expected distributions. Additionally, we remove the network's time conditioning to account for slight deviations in real-world noise distributions. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in segmenting critical biomarkers for two stages of Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD). Code is available: https://github.com/Veit21/tta-flow.

AI.tldr: A flow-matching-based test-time adaptation method generates high-quality surrogate images from noisy OCT inputs, achieving state-of-the-art AMD biomarker segmentation.
AI.motivation: OCT image quality is inconsistent, especially in low-cost devices, which hinders automated analysis of critical biomarkers.
AI.method: Uses flow-matching-based test-time adaptation with histogram matching to synthetic reference trajectories and removes network time conditioning to handle real-world noise distribution shifts.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in segmenting critical biomarkers for two stages of Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD).
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively bridges domain gaps in OCT imaging, enabling robust automated analysis despite noisy inputs.

title: PACT: Preserving Anchored Cores in Task-vectors for Model Merging
summary: Model merging has emerged as a training-free alternative to multi-task learning, aiming to combine multiple task-specific fine-tuned models into a single multi-task model. Most existing model merging approaches follow the Task Arithmetic paradigm, which decomposes fine-tuned weights into pre-trained parameters and task vectors, and performs merging exclusively in the task-vector space. The effectiveness of this paradigm implicitly relies on the assumption that task-specific knowledge is encoded solely within task vectors. We argue that this assumption generally does not hold due to the intrinsic task preferences of pre-trained models. Specifically, we identify \textbf{Load-Bearing Wall (LBW) dimensions}, namely some task-critical knowledge that remains embedded in the pre-trained weights rather than being fully transferred into task vectors. We characterize LBW dimensions from both scalar-weight and subspace perspectives, thereby covering the major paradigms of existing model merging methods. Our analysis reveals that, by ignoring LBW dimensions, task-vector-based approaches fail to fully resolve task conflicts and may inadvertently damage task-specific knowledge encoded in the pre-trained model, leading to degradation. To address this issue, we propose PACT, which preserves the anchored task-specific cores (i.e., LBW dimensions) within task vectors by aligning their orthogonal complements with the subspace of the pre-trained weights. These aligned subspace components are then removed from the task vectors before applying existing model merging algorithms. Furthermore, we develop an efficient variant based on randomized SVD to improve scalability. PACT can be seamlessly integrated with existing methods. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that PACT consistently enhances mainstream model merging approaches and establishes new state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies "Load-Bearing Wall (LBW) dimensions" in pre-trained models that retain task-critical knowledge not captured in task vectors. It proposes PACT, a method that preserves these dimensions by aligning orthogonal complements in task vectors, improving model merging performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing model merging methods assume task-specific knowledge is fully encoded in task vectors, but this assumption is flawed because pre-trained models retain intrinsic task preferences (LBW dimensions) not transferred to task vectors, causing unresolved conflicts and performance degradation.
AI.method: PACT preserves anchored task-specific cores (LBW dimensions) by aligning orthogonal complements of task vectors with the pre-trained weight subspace, then removing these aligned components before applying existing merging algorithms. An efficient variant using randomized SVD is also developed.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show PACT consistently enhances mainstream model merging approaches and establishes new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: Acknowledging and preserving LBW dimensions is crucial for effective model merging, and PACT provides a seamless, scalable solution that integrates with existing methods.

title: Performance Gap Analysis between Latin and Arabic Scripts HTR
comment: this paper accepted at TIPS workshop ICPR 2026
summary: Recent studies have shown that handwritten text recognition (HTR) systems perform worse on Arabic-script datasets than on Latin-script data. However, the reasons for this gap are still not well understood due to the lack of controlled comparisons. In this work, we present a comprehensive study of Arabic and Latin scripts HTR using a unified CRNN model for line-level HTR across nine datasets (including KHATT (Arabic), Muharaf (Arabic), NUST-UHWR (Urdu), PHTD (Persian), IAM (English), READ-2016 (German), and others) and di ferent training sizes (K in {100, 500, 1000, 2000, ..., Kfull}). Our results show the performance gap remains: it is large in low-resource settings, decreases with more data, but remains even at full scale, with a consistent difference of 5-7 CER points. We show that annotation quality matters, as many datasets contain labeling errors. Cleaning reduces error rates and narrows the gap, but does not eliminate it. In addition, we find that a fixed number of training samples provides less effective coverage in Arabic due to higher visual variability, requiring more data to learn similar representations. We compare recognition across datasets in terms of the number of text lines and the number of characters, showing an equivalence trade-off. We compare character frequency distributions across scripts and show that Arabic is significantly more heavy-tailed than Latin. Our error analysis reveals that around 30 percent of substitution errors in Arabic datasets (e.g., KHATT) are caused by confusion between visually similar characters, compared to about 15 percent in Latin-script datasets such as IAM.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares Arabic-script and Latin-script handwritten text recognition (HTR) using a unified CRNN model across nine datasets, finding a persistent 5-7 CER gap driven by annotation errors, higher visual variability in Arabic scripts, heavy-tailed character distributions, and greater visual character confusion (~30% vs ~15% substitution errors).
AI.motivation: To understand why Arabic-script HTR systems consistently underperform compared to Latin-script systems, as previous work lacked controlled, systematic comparisons across scripts and datasets.
AI.method: Used a unified CRNN model for line-level HTR across nine datasets (Arabic, Urdu, Persian, English, German, etc.) with varying training sizes (K ∈ {100, 500, 1000, ...}), analyzed annotation quality, visual variability, character frequency distributions, and error types.
AI.result: Performance gap persists (5-7 CER points) even at full scale; annotation quality matters; Arabic requires more data due to higher visual variability; Arabic character distributions are more heavy-tailed; ~30% of substitution errors in Arabic vs ~15% in Latin are due to visual character confusion.
AI.conclusion: The Arabic-Latin HTR gap is multi-faceted, stemming from annotation quality, visual variability, character frequency distributions, and character confusion patterns. Addressing these factors requires script-specific data augmentation, better annotation protocols, and more targeted modeling.

title: MetaboNet-Bench: A Multi-modal Benchmark for Glucose Forecasting in Type 1 Diabetes
comment: main content in 10 pages with 5 figures; supplementary section with 11 more pages and 5 more figures
summary: Glucose forecasting algorithms are an important aspect of glycemic control management in type 1 diabetes. So far, the research community has developed numerous algorithms and models for forecasting. However, it is well-recognized that the lack of standardized model performance evaluation benchmarks makes fair comparison difficult and hinders further innovation, and thus benchmark standardization is in urgent need. Furthermore, many published glucose forecasting algorithms are limited to CGM data alone, ignoring other multimodal signals such as insulin dosing and carbohydrate intake. Here, we introduce MetaboNet-Bench, a benchmark for multimodal glucose forecasting for patients with type 1 diabetes that provides an extensible open-source evaluation framework for comparison of glucose forecasting algorithms that leverage glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate data. We then demonstrate its utility by benchmarking several recently published glucose forecasting models and a custom multimodal time-series model, representing different model architectures. The results show that the benefit of adding data modalities is conditioned on the complexity of the model and that incorporating more clinical metrics helps identify meaningful gaps to fill for future research.

AI.tldr: MetaboNet-Bench is a standardized open-source benchmark for evaluating multimodal glucose forecasting algorithms in type 1 diabetes, integrating glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate data. Benchmarking reveals that adding data modalities benefits depend on model complexity, and more clinical metrics reveal research gaps.
AI.motivation: The lack of standardized evaluation benchmarks for glucose forecasting algorithms hinders fair comparison and innovation, and most existing algorithms ignore multimodal signals beyond CGM data (e.g., insulin, carbohydrates).
AI.method: MetaboNet-Bench provides an extensible open-source evaluation framework for multimodal glucose forecasting, leveraging glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate data. It benchmarks several published models and a custom multimodal time-series model representing different architectures.
AI.result: The benefit of adding data modalities depends on model complexity; incorporating more clinical metrics helps identify meaningful gaps for future research.
AI.conclusion: Standardized multimodal benchmarks like MetaboNet-Bench are essential for fair comparison and advancing glucose forecasting, and the value of additional data modalities is contingent on model architecture complexity.

title: LARE: Low-Attention Region Encoding for Text-Image Retrieval
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Efficient Multimodal Question Answering (EMM-QA). Code: https://github.com/AbdulmalikDS/LARE ; Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/AbdulmalekDS/Dense-Set
summary: Image retrieval in crowded scenes is particularly challenging due to the salience bias of conventional visual encoders, which tend to focus on dominant objects while neglecting low-attention regions that are often crucial for fine-grained retrieval. We propose LARE (Low-Attention Region Encoding), a framework that explicitly models these overlooked regions. LARE adopts a dual-encoding strategy that encodes low-attention regions of an image and the full image in parallel, leading to more diverse and informative image embeddings. To evaluate image retrieval performance in challenging crowded scenes, we introduce Dense-Set, a challenging subset derived from COCO and Flickr30K. In this subset, images are re-captioned to provide richer descriptions of low-attention or previously overlooked regions. This dataset highlights the limitations of existing retrieval models and enables a more rigorous evaluation under densely crowded scene conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework improves retrieval performance by preserving subtle, non-dominant visual cues within the shared latent space.

AI.tldr: LARE is a dual-encoding framework that explicitly models low-attention regions in crowded scenes to improve fine-grained image retrieval, outperforming conventional methods on the new Dense-Set benchmark.
AI.motivation: Image retrieval in crowded scenes suffers from salience bias—conventional encoders focus on dominant objects and neglect low-attention regions that are important for fine-grained retrieval.
AI.method: LARE adopts a dual-encoding strategy that encodes low-attention regions and the full image in parallel, along with Dense-Set dataset (subset of COCO and Flickr30K re-captioned with richer descriptions of overlooked regions).
AI.result: LARE improves retrieval performance by preserving subtle, non-dominant visual cues in the shared latent space, and Dense-Set reveals limitations of existing retrieval models.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling low-attention regions through dual-encoding is effective for fine-grained retrieval in crowded scenes; Dense-Set provides a more rigorous evaluation benchmark.

title: BLADE: Scalable Bi-level Adaptive Data Selection for LLM Training
summary: As Large Language Model (LLM) datasets scale to trillions of tokens, data selection has emerged as a critical frontier to filter out uninformative noise and construct adaptive learning trajectories. Beyond static heuristic filtering, advanced data selection methods for LLM training largely follow two paradigms, each with fundamental limitations. Influence-based methods provide principled bi-level objectives but require intractable inverse-Hessian computations, while excess-loss methods are computationally efficient but rely on a static reference model that becomes misaligned with the evolving proxy model during training. We propose BLADE (Bi-Level Adaptive Data sElection), a Hessian-free framework for data selection. BLADE reformulates the bi-level optimization problem underlying influence-based methods as a penalized single-level objective via Lagrange multipliers, avoiding inverse-Hessian computation while revealing a principled connection to excess-loss based data selection. The resulting objective recovers an excess-loss form but replaces the static reference model with a dynamic one that stays synchronized with training. Theoretically, we prove that this penalized formulation guarantees first-order convergence. For efficient online batch selection, we instantiate BLADE as a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm. Extensive experiments show that BLADE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data selection baselines, providing a practical recipe for LLM training.

AI.tldr: BLADE is a Hessian-free, bi-level adaptive data selection framework for LLM training that reformulates influence-based methods as a penalized single-level objective, replacing static reference models with dynamic ones, and achieves consistent SOTA performance via a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM data selection methods fall into two paradigms with fundamental limitations: influence-based methods require intractable inverse-Hessian computations, while excess-loss methods rely on a static reference model that becomes misaligned with the evolving proxy model during training.
AI.method: BLADE reformulates the bi-level optimization problem as a penalized single-level objective using Lagrange multipliers (avoiding inverse-Hessian), which recovers an excess-loss form but replaces the static reference model with a dynamic one. Theoretically proven to guarantee first-order convergence, and implemented via a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm for efficient online batch selection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show BLADE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data selection baselines across LLM training tasks.
AI.conclusion: BLADE provides a principled, computationally efficient, and practical solution for adaptive data selection in LLM training, bridging the gap between influence-based and excess-loss methods.

title: DINO-Med3D: Bridging Dimension and Domain Gaps in Volumetric Segmentation via Progressive Adaptation
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. The camera-ready version and link will be made publicly available upon publication
summary: Although DINOv3 has demonstrated remarkable semantic discrimination in natural imagery, its direct application to volumetric medical segmentation is hindered by inherent dimension and domain disparities. To resolve these issues, we propose DINO-Med3D, a two-stage progressive framework that repurpose the pre-trained DINOv3 encoder for 3D medical tasks. In the first stage, we mitigate the dimension gap by introducing a multi-slice embedding module that incorporates pseudo-3D context, while simultaneously employing a segmentation proxy task to adapt representations learned from natural scenes to the medical domain. Subsequently, we further enhance volumetric understanding by adding lightweight 3D adapters into the frozen backbone to enforce global inter-slice continuity. Finally, to compensate for the spatial information loss inherent in the embedding process, we design a parallel detail recovery stream to explicitly preserve high-frequency boundary cues. Extensive experiments on five public datasets demonstrate that our approach successfully adapts DINOv3 to the medical domain and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: DINO-Med3D is a two-stage framework that adapts DINOv3 (a 2D natural image model) to 3D medical segmentation by bridging dimension and domain gaps via multi-slice embedding, 3D adapters, and a detail recovery stream, outperforming SOTA on five public datasets.
AI.motivation: DINOv3 excels at semantic discrimination in natural images but fails when directly applied to 3D medical segmentation due to (1) dimension gap (2D vs. 3D) and (2) domain gap (natural scenes vs. medical imaging).
AI.method: A two-stage progressive framework: (1) Stage 1: Multi-slice embedding module for pseudo-3D context + segmentation proxy task for domain adaptation. (2) Stage 2: Lightweight 3D adapters in frozen backbone for inter-slice continuity + parallel detail recovery stream to preserve high-frequency boundary cues.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public datasets show successful adaptation of DINOv3 to medical domain, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: DINO-Med3D effectively repurposes 2D pre-trained vision transformers for 3D volumetric medical segmentation, achieving superior performance by addressing both dimension and domain gaps.

title: scGTN: Deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network for Single-cell RNA Sequencing Clustering
comment: Accepted by Proceedings of the Thirty-Fifth International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2026)
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) serves a pivotal role in characterizing gene expression at the cellular level, enabling the identification of cell types and advancing the understanding of cellular heterogeneity. Despite the significant progress in scRNA-seq data clustering, we argue that current methods always ignore the sparsity and noise, as well as the complex intercellular structural information inherent in scRNA-seq data. Toward this end, in this paper, we propose a novel single-cell RNA-seq clustering framework via deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network (termed scGTN), which explicitly integrates gene expression profile and intercellular structural dependencies for cell clustering. In particular, we formulate scRNA-seq data as a graph and construct two augmented graph views that serve as dual views to capture complementary intercellular information. Then, a Siamese graph transformer network is employed to explicitly incorporate shortest-path information and node-wise distances for capturing richer structural relationships between cells. Finally, we employ an optimal transport strategy to guide the cell clustering in a self-supervised manner. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark scRNA-seq datasets demonstrate that our scGTN consistently outperforms existing methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/W-RMSL/scGTN.

AI.tldr: scGTN is a deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network for single-cell RNA-seq clustering that integrates gene expression profiles with intercellular structural dependencies, using augmented graph views, graph transformer with shortest-path information, and optimal transport self-supervision to outperform existing methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Current scRNA-seq clustering methods ignore sparsity, noise, and complex intercellular structural information in the data.
AI.method: (1) Formulates scRNA-seq data as a graph with two augmented views; (2) Uses a Siamese graph transformer network incorporating shortest-path and node-wise distances; (3) Employs optimal transport for self-supervised clustering.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmark scRNA-seq datasets.
AI.conclusion: scGTN effectively integrates structural and expression information for superior cell clustering.

title: Automatic ply-specific analyses of CFRP micrographs using shortest-path-based ply distinction
summary: We present an automated approach to distinguish between ply instances in semantic segmentation masks of high-resolution carbon-fiber reinforced polymer micrographs. Interpreting the segmentation mask as a graph with pixels as vertices, enables us to use a shortest-path algorithm yielding the ply-separating paths. Thereby, we bridge the gap between semantic segmentation and ply instance segmentation using global information. We successfully apply our approach on high-resolution micrographs featuring a broad range of characteristics like artificially added gaps in single or multiple plies, different stacking sequences and ply traversing cracks. Assigning each fiber pixel to a ply based on the calculated paths, allows for a comprehensive, quantitative ply analysis with respect to its microstructural properties like the local fiber volume fraction as well as locally resolved ply and interleaf layer thickness. These insights help to reveal manufacturing-induced inhomogeneities, draw conclusions on manufacturing parameters and link mechanical properties to underlying microstructural imperfections.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an automated method that converts semantic segmentation masks into ply instance segmentation for carbon-fiber reinforced polymer micrographs using graph-based shortest-path algorithms, enabling quantitative ply-level microstructural analysis.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between semantic segmentation (pixel-level classification) and instance segmentation (distinguishing individual ply instances) in high-resolution micrographs, enabling quantitative analysis of manufacturing-induced inhomogeneities and microstructural properties.
AI.method: Interpret the semantic segmentation mask as a graph with pixels as vertices, then apply a shortest-path algorithm to find ply-separating paths, effectively converting semantic labels into distinct ply instances.
AI.result: Successfully applied to high-resolution micrographs with diverse characteristics including artificial gaps, different stacking sequences, and ply-traversing cracks, enabling quantitative ply analysis of fiber volume fraction, ply thickness, and interleaf layer thickness.
AI.conclusion: The approach reveals manufacturing-induced inhomogeneities, helps draw conclusions on manufacturing parameters, and links mechanical properties to underlying microstructural imperfections.

title: InTrain: Intrinsic Trainability for Zero-Cost Neural Architecture Search
summary: Training-free neural architecture search promises efficient discovery of high-performance networks without costly training. However, existing zero-cost proxies rely on fragmented heuristics that fail to capture the fundamental question: what makes an architecture trainable? This paper introduces Intrinsic Trainability (InTrain), a unified theoretical proxy that formalizes trainability as an architectural invariant emerging from two synergistic components: geometric capacity and optimization resilience. We operationalize intrinsic trainability through analysis of neural information processing. Geometric capacity is quantified via the participation ratio of activation covariance eigenspectrum, capturing the effective dimensionality of representation manifolds. Optimization resilience is measured through cumulative gradient health, assessing the robustness of backpropagation across network depth. InTrain synthesizes these dimensions through a scale-invariant multiplicative coupling, which we hypothesize is essential for capturing their synergistic, non-additive relationship. Extensive experiments on standard NAS benchmarks and search spaces demonstrate that InTrain achieves ranking correlations on par with state-of-the-art ensemble-based proxies and outperforms other single-metric methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes InTrain, a unified theoretical proxy for training-free neural architecture search that combines geometric capacity (via activation covariance eigenspectrum) and optimization resilience (via cumulative gradient health) through a multiplicative coupling, achieving competitive ranking correlations on NAS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-cost proxies rely on fragmented heuristics that fail to capture the fundamental question of what makes an architecture trainable. The authors aim to formalize trainability as an architectural invariant with a unified theoretical framework.
AI.method: InTrain operationalizes intrinsic trainability via two synergistic components: (1) geometric capacity quantified by participation ratio of activation covariance eigenspectrum, and (2) optimization resilience measured through cumulative gradient health. These are synthesized through a scale-invariant multiplicative coupling to capture their non-additive relationship.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard NAS benchmarks and search spaces show InTrain achieves ranking correlations on par with state-of-the-art ensemble-based proxies and outperforms other single-metric methods.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic trainability can be theoretically formalized and practically measured as a multiplicative combination of geometric capacity and optimization resilience, providing a more principled foundation for training-free NAS.

title: Stealthy World Model Manipulation via Data Poisoning
comment: 41 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Model-based learning agents use learned world models to predict future states, plan actions, and adapt to new environments. However, the process of updating world models from collected experience creates a training-time attack surface: adversarially poisoned fine-tuning trajectories can manipulate the learned dynamics and thereby corrupt downstream planning. In this paper, we propose SWAAP, the first two-stage data poisoning framework for learned world models. In the first stage, SWAAP identifies a harmful target world model that induces low-return behavior under planning while remaining close to clean dynamics, using first-order bilevel optimization enabled by a transition-gradient theorem. In the second stage, SWAAP realizes this target through stealth-constrained gradient matching, modifying only a limited fraction of fine-tuning transition targets so that the induced training gradients steer the victim model toward the adversarial target, while a prediction-error regularizer encourages the poisoned targets to remain close to the world model's natural approximation error. To assess attack stealthiness, we evaluate defenses and detectability across three stages of the poisoning pipeline: pre-training detection of poisoned transitions, robust training during fine-tuning, and test-time monitoring of the resulting world model. Across diverse continuous-control tasks, SWAAP causes substantial performance degradation while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data and evading the evaluated non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses. These results reveal a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines and highlight the need for robustness methods that protect both world-model training data and learned dynamics.

AI.tldr: SWAAP is a two-stage data poisoning attack framework that corrupts learned world models by injecting stealthy poisoned transitions during fine-tuning, causing significant performance degradation in continuous-control tasks while evading existing defenses.
AI.motivation: Model-based learning agents update world models from collected experience, creating a training-time attack surface where adversarially poisoned fine-tuning trajectories can manipulate learned dynamics and corrupt downstream planning. The paper aims to reveal this vulnerability.
AI.method: First stage: Identifies a harmful target world model via first-order bilevel optimization with a transition-gradient theorem. Second stage: Realizes the target through stealth-constrained gradient matching, modifying limited fine-tuning transition targets while using a prediction-error regularizer to keep poisoned targets close to natural approximation error.
AI.result: SWAAP causes substantial performance degradation across diverse continuous-control tasks while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data. It evades non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses evaluated at three stages (pre-training detection, robust training, test-time monitoring).
AI.conclusion: The results reveal a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines and highlight the need for robustness methods that protect both world-model training data and learned dynamics.

title: BindEdit: Taming Attention Leakage for Precise Multi-Object Image Editing
summary: Real image editing enables precise manipulation of visual content, yet existing methods often fail in complex multi-object scenarios, causing semantic blending, object duplication, or incomplete edits. We attribute these failures to attention leakage, where signals across spatial regions and text tokens become entangled during the denoising process. Specifically, we identify two distinct forms of leakage: Edit-Token Leakage, where ambiguous token-region alignment leads to object blending, and Source Dominance Leakage, where tokens of unchanged source objects overwhelm the attention intended for target entities. To resolve these leakages, we propose \textbf{BindEdit}, which enforces attention-level constraints within a single diffusion trajectory. To suppress Edit-Token Leakage, BindEdit jointly regularizes cross- and self-attention so that each target token group is bound to its corresponding spatial region while maintaining instance-level separation. To suppress Source Dominance Leakage, a cross-attention re-balancing mechanism amplifies target token influence and attenuates residual source semantics within editable regions. Moreover, a region fidelity term ensures that each target concept is expressed coherently across the entire editing mask. Additionally, we propose a comprehensive multi-object benchmark encompassing diverse object counts and categories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BindEdit consistently outperforms existing methods within a single diffusion trajectory, maintaining robust performance across both single- and multi-object editing scenarios.

AI.tldr: BindEdit solves attention leakage in multi-object image editing by regularizing cross/self-attention and re-balancing attention, outperforming existing methods in a single diffusion trajectory.
AI.motivation: Existing real image editing methods fail in complex multi-object scenarios due to two forms of attention leakage: Edit-Token Leakage (ambiguous token-region alignment causing object blending) and Source Dominance Leakage (source object tokens overwhelming target entities).
AI.method: BindEdit applies attention-level constraints within a single diffusion trajectory: (1) joint cross- and self-attention regularization to bind target token groups to spatial regions with instance separation, (2) cross-attention re-balancing to amplify target tokens and suppress source semantics, and (3) a region fidelity term for coherent target expression across the editing mask.
AI.result: BindEdit consistently outperforms existing methods in single- and multi-object editing scenarios, validated on a comprehensive multi-object benchmark with diverse object counts and categories.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing attention-level constraints within a single diffusion trajectory effectively resolves semantic blending, object duplication, and incomplete edits in multi-object image editing.

title: Bounded Context Management for Tabular Foundation Models on Stream Learning
comment: Accepted as a spotlight oral (top 5%) at the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data (FMSD@ICML2026)
summary: Tabular stream learning requires predictions on sequentially arriving examples under distribution shift. While standard methods adapt by updating model states, tabular foundation models (TFMs) make predictions conditioned on a labeled context in an in-context manner, making them a natural alternative for stream learning. This shifts the challenge from how to update the model to how to manage the context. We propose a future information view that yields three practical requirements for context management: preserve recent examples, retain uncertain examples, and remove redundant examples. We instantiate these requirements as CURE (Context management via Uncertainty-aware admission and Redundancy aware Eviction), a context-managing policy with entropy-gated admission and redundancy-aware eviction. Across seven streams, CURE shows up to 27.0% relative improvement over classical stream learners, remains robust across multiple TFM backbones, and ranks first among other policy variants. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/morcellinus/CURE-ICML-FMSD.

AI.tldr: Proposes CURE, a context management policy for tabular foundation models in streaming data, achieving up to 27% relative improvement over classical methods.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) offer in-context learning for stream prediction, shifting the challenge from model updates to context management. The paper seeks to define and operationalize effective context management policies.
AI.method: Proposes CURE (Context management via Uncertainty-aware admission and Redundancy aware Eviction), which uses entropy-gated admission to retain uncertain examples and redundancy-aware eviction to remove redundant ones, based on three requirements: preserve recent, retain uncertain, remove redundant.
AI.result: CURE achieves up to 27.0% relative improvement over classical stream learners, remains robust across multiple TFM backbones, and ranks first among policy variants on seven streams.
AI.conclusion: Effective context management is key to leveraging TFMs in streaming settings, and CURE provides a principled, high-performing solution.

title: Motion-Focused Latent Action Enables Cross-Embodiment VLA Training from Human EgoVideos
summary: Training generalist Vision-Language-Action(VLA) models typically requires massive, diverse robotic datasets with high-fidelity action annotations. While egocentric human manipulation videos are abundant and capture significant environmental diversity, the absence of action labels makes them difficult to use in conventional training paradigms. To address this, we propose a latent-action-based framework designed to extract general action priors from unlabeled human videos. The architecture features a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE that decouples motion dynamics from environmental backgrounds through physical masks, enabling the construction of a cross-embodiment action codebook. By pre-training on human videos with the codebook, the VLM backbone learns deep representations of action intent. For adaptation to specific embodiments, we introduce an intent-perception decoupling strategy where the VLM predicts the action intent while a separate frozen visual encoder provides state-specific features to the action expert, thereby reducing action hallucinations. Results in simulation and real-world environments show that our method, pre-trained exclusively on unlabeled human videos, performs competitively with state-of-the-art VLA models trained on massive annotated datasets, requiring only 50 trajectories for downstream adaptation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a latent-action framework that extracts action priors from unlabeled human videos using a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE, enabling generalist VLA models to be pre-trained without action annotations and achieve competitive performance with only 50 trajectories for adaptation.
AI.motivation: Training generalist VLA models requires massive annotated robotic datasets with action labels, which are scarce. Egocentric human videos are abundant but lack action annotations, making them difficult to use in conventional training paradigms.
AI.method: A latent-action framework with three key components: (1) Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE that decouples motion dynamics from backgrounds via physical masks, (2) cross-embodiment action codebook for pre-training on human videos, and (3) intent-perception decoupling strategy where VLM predicts action intent while a frozen visual encoder provides state-specific features to reduce hallucinations.
AI.result: The method, pre-trained exclusively on unlabeled human videos, performs competitively with state-of-the-art VLA models trained on massive annotated datasets, requiring only 50 trajectories for downstream adaptation in both simulation and real-world environments.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled human videos can effectively serve as pre-training data for VLA models through latent action extraction, significantly reducing the need for expensive action-annotated robotic datasets.

title: Physics-IQ Verified
summary: Video generative models ( VGMs) have become a new frontier that can be used not just for video generation but for a multitude of downstream tasks, including world modeling. To advance these tasks, a good video model must understand the physical reality of the world. Evaluating this understanding is an emerging field and has led to the Physics-IQ benchmark, which quantifies this explicitly by comparing model-generated videos to real-world videos of physical experiments. In this work, we present a systematic audit of the Physics-IQ benchmark, expose shortcomings and propose three solutions that sharpen how we can measure physical understanding of VGMs. Specifically, we improve prompt and ground-truth quality to reduce the influence of confounding factors and further introduce a sample-level scoring system that weights each sample and metric equally. Our resulting benchmark, Physics-IQ Verified, refines 57.6\% of all samples and improves over 34.8\% of prompts. In a comparison study using six image-to-video generative models, we observe moderate but meaningful ranking changes (Kendall's $τ= 0.46$). We hope Physics-IQ Verified advances the community by providing a more reliable signal toward physically accurate VGMs. The code for the benchmark can be accessed at https://github.com/google-deepmind/physics-iq-benchmark

AI.tldr: The paper audits the Physics-IQ benchmark for measuring video generative models' physical understanding, identifies flaws, and proposes Physics-IQ Verified with improved prompts, ground truths, and sample-level scoring, resulting in refined evaluation with meaningful ranking changes.
AI.motivation: Video generative models need to understand physical reality for downstream tasks like world modeling, but existing benchmarks for evaluating this understanding have shortcomings that need to be addressed.
AI.method: The authors systematically audit the Physics-IQ benchmark, improve prompt and ground-truth quality to reduce confounding factors, and introduce a sample-level scoring system that weights each sample and metric equally.
AI.result: Physics-IQ Verified refines 57.6% of all samples and improves 34.8% of prompts. A comparison study using six image-to-video models showed moderate ranking changes (Kendall's τ = 0.46).
AI.conclusion: Physics-IQ Verified provides a more reliable signal for evaluating physical accuracy in video generative models, advancing the community toward physically accurate VGMs.

title: Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM): Structural Prevention of Objective Interference Collapse via Heterogeneous External Grounding with Inward-Only Gradient Flow
comment: Position paper. Experimental validation in progress
summary: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) are a leading approach to world model representation learning. We identify a failure mode in JEPA-based world models grounded against two qualitatively distinct external signals: physical dynamics (sparse, high-magnitude, constraint-satisfying gradient corrections) and social-behavioral dynamics (diffuse, distribution-matching corrections). We term this Objective Interference Collapse (OIC): we argue that joint learning in a shared latent space causes the dominant channel to systematically collapse the subordinate channel's representational subspace, in a manner not resolvable by loss weighting alone. We propose Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM), designed to structurally prevent OIC through a partitioned latent space (physical subspace Z_p, behavioral subspace Z_b) with inward-only gradient flow. A Physical Grounding Channel updates only Z_p via VICReg-style alignment to physical measurements; a Social-Behavioral Grounding Channel updates only Z_b via alignment to trajectories from an emergent multi-agent simulation. An Inter-Channel Interface Module couples the subspaces at the task level without cross-subspace gradients. An Asymmetric Grounding Adherence Loss penalizes rollout drift with a hard hinge for physical violations and a soft KL for behavioral divergence. A Generative Rendering Layer is architecturally isolated from the latent world model. We present three theoretical results: the partition removes the gradient-interference pathway implicated in OIC; each grounded subspace inherits anti-collapse guarantees from its alignment objective; and generative isolation is necessary under a stated assumption on the generative objective's geometry. This manuscript establishes the problem formulation and architecture; experimental validation is ongoing and will be reported in a future revision.

AI.tldr: JEPAs suffer from Objective Interference Collapse (OIC) when learning from both physical and social-behavioral signals in a shared latent space. The authors propose Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM), which uses partitioned latent subspaces with inward-only gradient flow to structurally prevent OIC. Theoretical results show the partition removes gradient interference and provides anti-collapse guarantees, though experimental validation is pending.
AI.motivation: Current JEPA-based world models fail when jointly learning from two qualitatively different external signals—physical dynamics (sparse, high-magnitude corrections) and social-behavioral dynamics (diffuse, distribution-matching corrections)—because the dominant channel collapses the subordinate channel's representational subspace, a problem not solvable by loss weighting alone.
AI.method: DCGWM uses a partitioned latent space (physical Z_p, behavioral Z_b) with inward-only gradient flow. The Physical Grounding Channel updates Z_p via VICReg-style alignment to physical measurements; the Social-Behavioral Grounding Channel updates Z_b via alignment to multi-agent simulation trajectories. An Inter-Channel Interface Module couples subspaces at the task level without cross-subspace gradients. An Asymmetric Grounding Adherence Loss penalizes rollout drift (hard hinge for physical, soft KL for behavioral). A Generative Rendering Layer is architecturally isolated from the latent world model.
AI.result: Three theoretical results: (1) the partition removes the gradient-interference pathway causing OIC; (2) each grounded subspace inherits anti-collapse guarantees from its alignment objective; (3) generative isolation is necessary under a stated assumption on the generative objective's geometry. Experimental validation is ongoing and not yet reported.
AI.conclusion: The problem formulation and architecture for DCGWM are established; experimental validation will be reported in a future revision.

title: Mem-World: Memory-Augmented Action-Conditioned World Models for Persistent Robot Manipulation
summary: Action-conditioned world models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, offering a scalable alternative to costly real-world experimentation by generating action-consistent video rollouts. However, persistent world modeling remains challenging in manipulation: frequent end-effector occlusions and rapid wrist-camera motion make the current observation insufficient for predicting future views, causing models to forget or hallucinate scene details seen in earlier frames. Existing memory retrieval strategies often fail to identify informative history in dynamic manipulation scenarios. To address this limitation, we propose Mem-World, a memory-augmented multi-view action-conditioned world model. At its core, we present W-VMem, a 4D wrist-view-centered surfel-indexed memory that anchors historical observations to temporally evolving surface elements. By explicitly modeling when and where scene elements are observed, W-VMem enables geometry-aware retrieval of relevant history frames conditioned on future actions. During generation, relevant history frames are selected via surfel-based rendering and scoring, providing informative and non-redundant context for prediction. Extensive experiments show that Mem-World generates persistent rollouts in complex manipulation scenarios, enables more reliable policy evaluation than Ctrl-World, improving the Pearson correlation with real-world performance by 14.5\%, and supports effective policy improvement through synthetic data generation, increasing success rates from 58\% to 72\% on long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: Mem-World introduces a memory-augmented world model (W-VMem) using 4D surfel-indexed memory to enable persistent video prediction in robot manipulation, improving policy evaluation and synthetic data generation.
AI.motivation: Action-conditioned world models struggle in manipulation due to frequent end-effector occlusions and rapid wrist-camera motion, causing models to forget or hallucinate scene details. Existing memory retrieval fails to identify informative history in dynamic scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes Mem-World with W-VMem, a 4D wrist-view-centered surfel-indexed memory that anchors historical observations to temporally evolving surface elements, enabling geometry-aware retrieval of relevant history frames conditioned on future actions via surfel-based rendering and scoring.
AI.result: Mem-World generates persistent rollouts in complex manipulation, improves Pearson correlation with real-world performance by 14.5% over Ctrl-World, and increases policy success rates from 58% to 72% on long-horizon tasks via synthetic data generation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed memory-augmented world model effectively addresses the persistence problem in manipulation world models, enabling more reliable policy evaluation and improvement.

title: SP-TransientBench: A Real-Captured Single Photon Perception Benchmark
summary: Single-photon LiDAR (SPL) based on single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) sensing enables time-resolved photon measurements with extreme sensitivity, offering unique potential for active 3D perception in photon-starved scenarios.However, real-world single photon perception remains fundamentally challenging due to unique measurement noise and complex multi-return transient phenomena, which jointly complicate geometric reconstruction and semantic scene understanding. Despite growing interest in SPAD-based sensing, existing studies are largely limited to simulated data or small-scale controlled captures. As a result, systematic evaluation of real-world single photon perception across depth estimation, multi-view reconstruction, and 3D semantic understanding remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce SP-TransientBench (STB), a real-captured multi-task benchmark for single photon perception. SP-TransientBenc comprises 10 diverse scenes and 10,297 views captured using a solid-state single-photon LiDAR at $256\times192$ resolution. Each view provides full time-of-flight histograms with multi-return behavior,standardized metadata, and calibrated camera poses for multi-view evaluation. We further provide 13-class 3D semantic annotations for selected scenes. By providing dedicated data splits and evaluation protocols for each task, STB enables consistent and reproducible benchmarking of real-world single photon perception across multiple 3D vision problems. The dataset and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SP-TransientBench (STB), the first real-captured multi-task benchmark for single-photon LiDAR perception, comprising 10 scenes, 10,297 views with full time-of-flight histograms, and standardized evaluation protocols for depth estimation, multi-view reconstruction, and 3D semantic understanding.
AI.motivation: Real-world single-photon perception remains fundamentally challenging due to unique measurement noise and complex multi-return transient phenomena. Existing studies are limited to simulated data or small-scale controlled captures, and systematic evaluation across depth estimation, multi-view reconstruction, and 3D semantic understanding is lacking.
AI.method: The authors built SP-TransientBench, a real-captured multi-task benchmark using a solid-state single-photon LiDAR at 256×192 resolution. It provides 10 diverse scenes, 10,297 views with full time-of-flight histograms and multi-return behavior, standardized metadata, calibrated camera poses, and 13-class 3D semantic annotations. Dedicated data splits and evaluation protocols are provided for each task.
AI.result: A comprehensive real-world benchmark capturing 10 diverse scenes with 10,297 views, including full time-of-flight histograms, multi-return transient data, calibrated poses, and 13-class semantic annotations for selected scenes.
AI.conclusion: STB enables consistent and reproducible benchmarking of real-world single-photon perception across multiple 3D vision problems (depth estimation, multi-view reconstruction, 3D semantic understanding), bridging the gap between simulated and real-world evaluation.

title: Robust and Interpretable Adaptation of Equivariant Materials Foundation Models via Sparsity-promoting Fine-tuning
summary: Pre-trained materials foundation models, or machine learning interatomic potentials, leverage general physicochemical knowledge to effectively approximate potential energy surfaces. However, they often require domain-specific calibration due to physicochemical diversity as well as mismatches between practical computational settings and those used in constructing the pre-training data. To address this, we propose a sparsity-promoting fine-tuning method that selectively updates model parameters by exploiting the structural properties of E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models. On energy and force prediction tasks across molecular and crystalline benchmarks, our method matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and equivariant low-rank adaptation while updating only $\sim$3~\% of parameters, and in some cases as little as $\sim$0.5~\%. Beyond energy and force calibration, we further demonstrate task generalizability by applying our method to magnetic moment prediction and magnetism-aware total energy modeling. Finally, analysis of sparsity patterns reveals physically interpretable signatures, such as enhanced $d$-orbital contributions in transition metal systems. Overall, our results establish sparsity-promoting fine-tuning as a flexible and interpretable method for domain specialization of equivariant materials foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a sparsity-promoting fine-tuning method for E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models that selectively updates only ~0.5-3% of parameters, achieving competitive or superior performance on energy, force, magnetic moment, and total energy prediction tasks, while revealing physically interpretable sparsity patterns like enhanced d-orbital contributions in transition metals.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained materials foundation models require domain-specific calibration due to physicochemical diversity and mismatches between practical computational settings and pre-training data construction, but existing fine-tuning methods update a large number of parameters.
AI.method: A sparsity-promoting fine-tuning method that selectively updates model parameters by exploiting the structural properties of E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and equivariant low-rank adaptation while updating only ~3% of parameters (as low as ~0.5%), and demonstrates generalizability to magnetic moment prediction and magnetism-aware total energy modeling. Sparsity patterns reveal physically interpretable signatures such as enhanced d-orbital contributions in transition metals.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity-promoting fine-tuning is a flexible and interpretable method for domain specialization of equivariant materials foundation models.

title: OneCanvas: 3D Scene Understanding via Panoramic Reprojection
summary: Existing approaches to 3D scene understanding in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) either rely on complex, model-specific geometry encoders or large training budgets in pursuit of spatial reasoning. Instead, OneCanvas aggregates patch features from all views onto a single equirectangular panoramic canvas. Namely, each patch is unprojected to a 3D world coordinate using its depth and camera pose, then placed on the canvas at the continuous longitude and latitude of that point as seen from the canvas origin, with no rasterization or aggregation across overlapping views. A 3D position embedding of the patch's metric coordinates is added to its feature, restoring the depth lost when collapsing the world position to an angular canvas coordinate. Patches from all frames thus share one spatial coordinate system with no fusion or major architectural modifications of the backbone. The pretrained VLM consumes this representation as if it were an ordinary image. Because the canvas can be centered on any pose of interest, the same representation directly supports situated reasoning from a specific viewpoint, a common requirement in robotics and embodied AI. Thanks to this representation, we can also introduce a spatial pretraining curriculum: by procedurally placing patch features of objects, drawn from real images, at chosen 3D world positions on an otherwise empty canvas, we generate on-the-fly supervision spanning a broad range of spatial reasoning tasks, with answer distributions controlled to reduce spatial reasoning shortcuts. OneCanvas achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on SQA3D and VSI-Bench, and generalizes to out-of-distribution data on SPBench, using an order of magnitude less training compute than the strongest competing methods.

AI.tldr: OneCanvas aggregates multi-view patch features onto a single equirectangular panoramic canvas using depth and camera poses, enabling 3D scene understanding in VLMs without complex geometry encoders or large training budgets, achieving SOTA on SQA3D, VSI-Bench, and SPBench with 10x less compute.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D scene understanding in VLMs relies on complex geometry encoders or large training budgets for spatial reasoning. The authors aim for a simpler, more efficient approach.
AI.method: Patch features from all views are unprojected to 3D world coordinates using depth and camera pose, then placed onto a single equirectangular panoramic canvas by longitude/latitude without rasterization or aggregation. A 3D position embedding restores depth information. Supports spatial pretraining curriculum by procedurally placing objects on empty canvases.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on SQA3D and VSI-Bench, generalizes to out-of-distribution data on SPBench, using an order of magnitude less training compute than strongest competing methods.
AI.conclusion: OneCanvas provides an efficient, lightweight representation for 3D scene understanding in VLMs, enabling strong spatial reasoning with minimal architectural changes and compute requirements.

title: Attention as Frustrated Synchronization
comment: 25 pages, 4 figures. Preliminary report at the 1-10M parameter scale
summary: A network of oscillators that synchronizes perfectly computes nothing further, so an attention architecture built from synchronization must locate its computation in structured departures from agreement. We introduce the Frustrated Synchronization Network (FSN), whose token states are phases on a torus and whose entire value pathway is one learned complex coupling kernel over harmonics and a one-step delay. Each component of the kernel is a frustration in the sense of the synchronization literature. The complex phases are static Kuramoto-Sakaguchi frustration angles, the signed harmonics are repulsive Daido components, and the delay term, which couples each token to the successors of the tokens it attends to, is algebraically identical to Kuramoto-Sakaguchi coupling whose frustration angle is the data's own transition, so next-token prediction is implemented as synchronization frustrated by the data. At matched one-million-parameter and training budgets on character-level text and code, the FSN's validation loss is below a tuned RoPE-SwiGLU transformer's at every epoch measured, and the comparison survives training the baseline to convergence: every thirty-epoch enwik8 seed finishes below the transformer's converged fifty-epoch loss of 1.611, and the FSN's completed fifty-epoch runs converge to 1.5953 +/- 0.0014. A variant with every feed-forward block replaced by mean-field coupling to learned collective modes, leaving no multilayer perceptron in the stack, tracks the transformer.
