On natural text the unfrustrated base layer falls behind the converged transformer at every copy depth, worst on long-range copy events; the kernel reverses the deficit at every depth of four and beyond. Headline comparisons are at the one-million-parameter scale; a scale ladder is complete through four million parameters with the advantage persisting, and remaining arms are marked as in progress.

AI.tldr: The Frustrated Synchronization Network (FSN) replaces transformer attention with a learned complex coupling kernel over phases on a torus, where next-token prediction is implemented as synchronization frustrated by data. At matched 1M-parameter budgets, FSN outperforms a tuned RoPE-SwiGLU transformer on character-level text and code, converging to 1.5953 loss vs 1.611, with advantages persisting through 4M parameters.
AI.motivation: A perfectly synchronized oscillator network computes nothing; therefore, attention architectures built from synchronization must locate computation in structured departures from agreement (frustration).
AI.method: Token states are phases on a torus. The entire value pathway is one learned complex coupling kernel over harmonics and a one-step delay. Components include Kuramoto-Sakaguchi frustration angles, repulsive Daido harmonics, and a delay term algebraically identical to coupling whose frustration angle is the data's own transition. A variant replaces every feed-forward block with mean-field coupling to learned collective modes, removing all MLPs.
AI.result: At matched 1M-parameter and training budgets on character-level text and code, FSN validation loss is below a tuned RoPE-SwiGLU transformer at every measured epoch. Every 30-epoch enwik8 seed finishes below transformer's converged 50-epoch loss of 1.611. FSN's 50-epoch runs converge to 1.5953 ± 0.0014. The unfrustrated base layer falls behind; the kernel reverses the deficit at every depth of four and beyond. Advantage persists through 4M parameters.
AI.conclusion: Frustrated synchronization provides an effective alternative computational framework to standard transformer attention, achieving lower loss with the same parameter budget, and the synchronization-based design can even replace MLP layers entirely via mean-field coupling.

title: CABLE: Cloud-Assisted Bandwidth-efficient LMM-based Encoding for V2X Systems
summary: Cloud-hosted large multimodal models (LMMs) can provide strong open-vocabulary perception for Vehicle-to-Everything systems, but naively transmitting full-resolution frames from edge to cloud causes severe communication overhead and high cloud-side prefill latency. We present CABLE, a cloud-assisted bandwidth-efficient LMM-based encoding framework for edge-cloud perception. CABLE propagates the previous cloud segmentation mask on the edge using ego-motion compensation, refines it with residual-motion cues, and consolidates disconnected regions via a corridor envelope to form a robust region of interest (ROI). Only ROI-masked images are uploaded, while the cloud segmentation output is fed back as the prior for the next frame, forming a mask-to-ROI-to-LMM feedback loop. Experiments on five datasets (nuScenes, WOD-ZB, Waymo, KITTI, and CADC) show consistent communication savings while largely preserving perception, achieving $73$--$87\%$ ROI pixel-coverage reduction with $5$--$8\times$ estimated LMM prefill speedup at a modest detection-quality trade-off relative to full-frame inference.

AI.tldr: CABLE is a cloud-assisted framework that reduces communication overhead and LMM prefill latency in V2X systems by uploading only ROI-masked frames (derived from prior segmentation masks with ego-motion compensation and residual-motion refinement) instead of full-resolution images, achieving 73-87% ROI pixel reduction and 5-8x prefill speedup with minimal detection quality loss.
AI.motivation: Full-resolution frame transmission from edge to cloud in cloud-assisted LMM-based V2X systems incurs high communication overhead and cloud-side prefill latency, motivating a bandwidth-efficient encoding approach.
AI.method: CABLE propagates previous cloud segmentation masks on the edge via ego-motion compensation, refines them with residual-motion cues, and consolidates disconnected regions with a corridor envelope to form a robust ROI. Only ROI-masked images are uploaded; cloud segmentation output feeds back as the prior for the next frame.
AI.result: On five datasets (nuScenes, WOD-ZB, Waymo, KITTI, CADC), CABLE achieves 73-87% ROI pixel-coverage reduction with 5-8x estimated LMM prefill speedup, while largely preserving perception quality.
AI.conclusion: The mask-to-ROI-to-LMM feedback loop effectively balances communication efficiency and perception accuracy, offering a practical solution for edge-cloud collaborative perception in V2X systems.

title: Does VLA Even Know the Basics? Measuring Commonsense and World Knowledge Retention in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Embodied Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are typically obtained by fine-tuning powerful pretrained VLMs on robotics data, yet it is unclear how much commonsense and factual knowledge they retain after adaptation. Failures on knowledge-sensitive tasks are ambiguous, conflating missing knowledge with poor generalization of low-level control. We introduce Act2Answer, a lightweight protocol that adapts VLM knowledge benchmarks to VLA evaluation by requiring agents to answer through action. Each question becomes a short tabletop episode where the agent performs a single object-placement action to select among candidate answers, yielding an action-grounded success rate with reduced control confounds. We curate a test suite of such environments across diverse commonsense and world-knowledge categories and introduce layerwise intent probing to localize answer-relevant information across the VLM backbone and action head. In a large-scale study of 7 VLA models and 9 VLM baselines, we systematically rank models across categories, finding that VLAs show solid performance on simple concepts while exhibiting larger gaps on richer semantic categories relative to their source VLMs, that VQA co-training is associated with better knowledge retention, and that answer-relevant signals peak in middle VLA layers but attenuate in upper layers. Act2Answer is available at https://tttonyalpha.github.io/act2answer/.

AI.tldr: Act2Answer is a lightweight protocol that adapts VLM knowledge benchmarks to VLA evaluation by requiring agents to answer questions through physical object-placement actions in tabletop episodes, revealing that VLAs retain simple knowledge but show gaps on richer semantics, VQA co-training helps retention, and answer-relevant signals peak in middle layers.
AI.motivation: It is unclear how much commonsense and factual knowledge Embodied VLA models retain after fine-tuning from VLMs, and failures on knowledge-sensitive tasks conflate missing knowledge with poor low-level control.
AI.method: Act2Answer protocol: each question becomes a short tabletop episode where the agent performs a single object-placement action to select among candidate answers, yielding action-grounded success rates with reduced control confounds. Layerwise intent probing is used to localize answer-relevant information across VLM backbone and action head.
AI.result: VLAs show solid performance on simple concepts but larger gaps on richer semantic categories vs source VLMs; VQA co-training is associated with better knowledge retention; answer-relevant signals peak in middle layers but attenuate in upper layers.
AI.conclusion: Act2Answer provides a systematic way to evaluate knowledge retention in VLA models, revealing layer-specific knowledge degradation patterns and the benefit of VQA co-training.

title: Visual-OPSD: Cross-Modal On-Policy Self-Distillation for Efficient Unified Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) interleave generated ''visual thoughts'' (VTs) with text reasoning to improve spatial tasks. This incurs roughly an order-of-magnitude inference cost from multi-step diffusion. We find this cost yields limited direct benefit. On ThinkMorph, removing or noising VTs barely changes accuracy across nine benchmarks. Once rendered, attention concentrates on the VT regardless of content. Yet a KL diagnostic shows that conditioning on a privileged VT trace shifts the model's completion distribution. This suggests the generation pathway encodes useful reasoning beyond the rendered pixels. Motivated by this gap, we propose Visual On-Policy Self-Distillation(Visual-OPSD). Teacher and student share identical weights but differ in context: the teacher sees privileged VTs while the student sees only the question. Token-level JSD distillation on on-policy student trajectories transfers the teacher's reasoning to a text-only student. Across nine benchmarks, Visual-OPSD improves over its generative teacher by $+3.40$pp with $14.3\times$ speedup (10.0s vs. 142.8s per sample) and outperforms same-scale VLMs by $+63.83$pp on VSP. A Gaussian-noise control ($+0.40$pp vs. $+10.28$pp for real VTs) and $58.4\%$ closure of the KL gap confirm that gains come from the semantic content of the generation pathway.

AI.tldr: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) use costly "visual thoughts" (VTs) for spatial reasoning. The paper finds VTs' rendered pixels provide limited direct benefit, but the generation pathway encodes useful reasoning. They propose Visual-OPSD, a distillation method that transfers this reasoning from a VT-using teacher to a text-only student, achieving +3.40pp improvement over the teacher with 14.3× speedup.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal models generate "visual thoughts" (VTs) via expensive multi-step diffusion to improve spatial tasks, but this incurs roughly an order-of-magnitude inference cost. The paper investigates whether this cost yields proportional benefits and proposes a more efficient alternative.
AI.method: Visual-OPSD (Visual On-Policy Self-Distillation): Teacher and student share identical weights but differ in context—the teacher sees privileged VTs while the student only sees the question. Token-level JSD (Jensen-Shannon Divergence) distillation is applied on on-policy student trajectories to transfer reasoning from the teacher to a text-only student.
AI.result: Visual-OPSD improves over the generative teacher by +3.40pp with 14.3× speedup (10.0s vs. 142.8s per sample) across nine benchmarks, and outperforms same-scale VLMs by +63.83pp on VSP. A Gaussian-noise control (+0.40pp vs. +10.28pp real VTs) and 58.4% closure of the KL gap confirm gains come from the generation pathway's semantic content.
AI.conclusion: The rendered pixels of visual thoughts provide limited direct benefit, but the generation pathway itself encodes useful reasoning. Visual-OPSD effectively distills this reasoning into a text-only student, achieving significant accuracy improvements and massive speedups, demonstrating that the value of visual thoughts lies in their generation process rather than their visual output.

title: Contextualizing Biological Language Models across Modalities via Logit-Space Contrastive Alignment
summary: Pretrained biological language models expose per-token probability distributions through masked-token prediction, providing the likelihood interface central to sequence design, variant scoring, and mechanistic interpretation. Yet these distributions are learned from broad unlabeled corpora and are not naturally conditioned on task-specific biological contexts such as interaction partners, cellular environments, or therapeutic interventions. Existing contextual matching methods often distort this interface through pooled embeddings, contrastive latent spaces, or task-specific prediction heads. We introduce LOGICA (Logit-space Contrastive Alignment), a framework for context-conditioned prediction that performs contrastive learning directly in output-logit space. Using gated cross-modal adapters compatible with each model's native token head, LOGICA preserves the pretrained likelihood interface and converts contextualized token log-likelihoods into matching scores. Alignment is defined through context-sensitive token probabilities rather than proximity in a shared embedding space, enabling learning from sparse paired data across models with distinct vocabularies, without a shared tokenizer or decoder. LOGICA is particularly effective for mutation-local variant ranking, where comparisons reduce to context-conditioned likelihoods of mutant tokens at perturbed sites. Across protein--ligand binding, TCR--peptide activity, and drug-conditioned resistance prediction, LOGICA improves over prior state-of-the-art methods, including matched latent-contrastive and conditional MLM baselines, while retaining a token-level interface for interpretation and generation. On held-out-gene single-mutation drug-resistance prediction, LOGICA improves AUC from near-random latent-space baselines of $\sim$0.55 to $\sim$0.65.

AI.tldr: LOGICA is a framework that performs contrastive learning directly in output-logit space of pretrained biological language models, enabling context-conditioned prediction while preserving the native token likelihood interface. It uses gated cross-modal adapters to convert contextualized token log-likelihoods into matching scores, achieving state-of-the-art results on protein-ligand binding, TCR-peptide activity, and drug-resistance prediction tasks.
AI.motivation: Pretrained biological language models provide token probability distributions useful for sequence design and variant scoring, but these distributions are learned from unlabeled data and not naturally conditioned on task-specific biological contexts (e.g., interaction partners, cellular environments). Existing methods distort the likelihood interface through pooled embeddings, contrastive latent spaces, or task-specific heads.
AI.method: LOGICA performs contrastive learning directly in output-logit space using gated cross-modal adapters that are compatible with each model's native token head. It preserves the pretrained likelihood interface and converts contextualized token log-likelihoods into matching scores, working across models with distinct vocabularies without requiring a shared tokenizer or decoder.
AI.result: LOGICA achieves state-of-the-art results across protein-ligand binding, TCR-peptide activity, and drug-conditioned resistance prediction tasks. On held-out-gene single-mutation drug-resistance prediction, it improves AUC from ~0.55 (near-random latent-space baselines) to ~0.65.
AI.conclusion: LOGICA demonstrates that contrastive alignment in logit-space preserves the pretrained likelihood interface while enabling effective context-conditioned prediction. It is particularly effective for mutation-local variant ranking and retains a token-level interface for interpretation and generation.

title: UBP2: Uncertainty-Balanced Preference Planning for Efficient Preference-based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Preference-based RL provides an approach to learning reward models from pairwise comparisons of behaviors, bypassing the need for explicit reward design. However, existing methods typically rely on passive data collection and suffer from poor sample efficiency, especially during the early stages of learning. We introduce a model-based approach that actively directs exploration by jointly reasoning over uncertainties in the reward, dynamics, and value functions. Our method, Uncertainty-Balanced Preference Planning (UBP2), uses ensembles of reward, dynamics, and value function models to evaluate candidate trajectories according to a unified score that combines expected reward, terminal value, and epistemic uncertainty. Planning under this objective yields an explicit tradeoff between exploitation and information acquisition without requiring ad hoc exploration heuristics. Under standard regularity assumptions, we establish sublinear regret guarantees for both finite-horizon and infinite-horizon settings. Empirically, experiments on the Meta-World benchmark show UBP2 achieves substantially higher sample efficiency than model-free preference-based methods and non-optimistic model-based baselines.

AI.tldr: UBP2 is a model-based preference-based RL method that actively explores by jointly reasoning over uncertainties in reward, dynamics, and value functions, achieving sublinear regret guarantees and superior sample efficiency on Meta-World benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing preference-based RL methods suffer from poor sample efficiency due to passive data collection, especially in early learning stages.
AI.method: UBP2 uses ensembles of reward, dynamics, and value function models to evaluate trajectories via a unified score combining expected reward, terminal value, and epistemic uncertainty, enabling active exploration without ad hoc heuristics.
AI.result: Sublinear regret guarantees established for finite-horizon and infinite-horizon settings; empirical results on Meta-World show substantially higher sample efficiency than model-free preference-based methods and non-optimistic model-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Jointly reasoning over multiple uncertainties in a model-based framework effectively improves sample efficiency and exploration in preference-based RL.

title: Show, Don't Ask: Generative Visual Disambiguation for Composed Image Retrieval with Turn-Valid Coverage
summary: Composed image retrieval (CIR) uses a reference image and a text modification to search for a target image. However, such queries often describe several possible images rather than one exact target, making the user's intent ambiguous. Recent methods address this by using conformal prediction to estimate ambiguity and by asking users clarifying text questions. However, these methods have two limitations: their coverage guarantee only holds at the first interaction, and text questions are often insufficient for resolving fine-grained visual differences such as appearance, attributes, or viewpoint. We propose CLARA, a clarification framework that resolves ambiguity by showing users a small panel of visual alternatives. Instead of answering text questions, the user simply selects the prototype image closest to the intended target. This provides a direct visual signal and avoids relying on a model to predict the user's answer. To maintain valid conformal guarantees across multiple interaction rounds, CLARA reweights calibration using the likelihood ratio induced by the user's selection. The displayed prototypes are also constrained to represent the current candidate set and are snapped to real corpus images, ensuring that generated images cannot artificially improve coverage. Experiments on open-domain and fashion benchmarks show that CLARA matches single-turn state-of-the-art retrieval performance, maintains nominal coverage across interaction rounds, and finds the intended target in fewer rounds than strong text-question baselines. Its advantage is especially clear when ambiguity involves viewpoint or fine-grained attributes, where visual clarification is more effective than textual questioning.

AI.tldr: CLARA is a visual clarification framework for composed image retrieval that uses image panels (instead of text questions) to resolve ambiguity, with principled conformal prediction guarantees across multiple interaction rounds.
AI.motivation: Existing CIR methods using conformal prediction and text questions for ambiguity resolution have two key limitations: (1) coverage guarantees only hold at the first interaction round, and (2) text questions are insufficient for resolving fine-grained visual differences like appearance, attributes, or viewpoint.
AI.method: CLARA proposes a multi-round clarification framework that: (1) shows users a small panel of visual prototype images instead of asking text questions; (2) reweights calibration data using likelihood ratios based on user selection to maintain valid conformal guarantees across rounds; (3) constrains displayed prototypes to represent the candidate set and snaps them to real corpus images to prevent artificial coverage inflation.
AI.result: Experiments on open-domain and fashion benchmarks show CLARA matches single-turn SOTA retrieval performance, maintains nominal coverage across interaction rounds, and finds the intended target in fewer rounds than text-question baselines. Advantage is especially clear for viewpoint and fine-grained attribute ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Visual clarification (selecting from image panels) is more effective than textual questioning for resolving ambiguity in CIR, and CLARA provides a theoretically grounded multi-round conformal framework to support this interaction paradigm.

title: Trainable Photonic Measurement for Physics-Informed PDE Learning
summary: Photonic quantum machine learning offers a route to trainable physical representations built from phase, interference and measurement. However, its role in scientific machine learning remains largely unexplored. Physics-informed neural fields provide a natural setting, because differential equations require trial spaces that preserve phase, frequency and derivative structure. Here we introduce a photonic quantum neural field in which coordinates become trainable optical phases, are mixed by multi-photon Fock-space interference and are decoded from photon-number measurements. The photonic circuit is optimized as the neural-field representation itself, not as a fixed feature map or hardware accelerator. Photonic measurement is therefore a trainable representation on which the physics-informed residual is minimized. Across seven elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive and inverse PDE benchmarks, we observe a phase-complexity transition: classical coordinate and Fourier-feature networks suffice in smooth regimes, whereas the photonic field is most accurate when residual derivatives amplify phase mismatch. In the hardest regimes it gives the lowest errors, with margins reaching an order of magnitude and about one quarter of the trainable parameters of classical baselines. Frozen and shuffled controls, together with noise stress tests, attribute this gain to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout under compound perturbations. These results identify photonic quantum measurement as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a photonic quantum neural field where optical phases are trainable, mixed via multi-photon interference, and decoded from photon-number measurements. This approach is optimized as the neural-field representation itself and tested on PDE benchmarks, showing a phase-complexity transition where photonic fields outperform classical methods in regimes with high phase mismatch, achieving up to 10x lower error with ~75% fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: To explore the role of photonic quantum machine learning in scientific machine learning, particularly for physics-informed neural fields where differential equations require trial spaces preserving phase, frequency, and derivative structure.
AI.method: Coordinates become trainable optical phases, mixed by multi-photon Fock-space interference, and decoded from photon-number measurements. The photonic circuit is optimized as the neural-field representation itself (not a fixed feature map or hardware accelerator), and the physics-informed residual is minimized on this trainable representation.
AI.result: Across seven PDE benchmarks (elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive, inverse), a phase-complexity transition is observed. In hard regimes with amplified phase mismatch, the photonic field achieves lowest errors with margins up to 10x and ~75% fewer trainable parameters than classical baselines.
AI.conclusion: Photonic quantum measurement serves as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning, with gains attributed to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout under compound perturbations.

title: FlowObject: Flow Steering for Bridging Generative Priors and Reconstruction Fidelity
summary: Recovering complete 3D representations of objects from few casual image captures remains a significant challenge. Recent 3D generative models, particularly those based on Flow-Matching (FM), can synthesize high-quality textured assets; however, they often suffer from ''synthetic bias'' where learned priors override observational evidence, alongside a lack of alignment with the observed instance. Conversely, optimization-based methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provide high fidelity on visible surfaces but fail to reason about unobserved geometry. In this paper, we present FlowObject, a framework that reformulates sparse-view 3D reconstruction as a training-free, guided inverse problem. Our approach applies a dual-space guidance strategy to steer the Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) trajectory of a flow-matching model, enabling the completion of unseen regions through learned generative priors while enforcing strict consistency with real-world observations. By integrating a 3DGS refinement stage, FlowObject further bridges the gap between ''synthetic-looking'' generative outputs and photorealistic reconstructions. Comprehensive benchmarks on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that current state-of-the-art methods often struggle to achieve geometric completeness and observational consistency simultaneously, especially under severe occlusions. In contrast, our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generative models and optimization-based frameworks in both geometric completeness and view-dependent appearance fidelity.

AI.tldr: FlowObject reformulates sparse-view 3D reconstruction as a training-free guided inverse problem, combining flow-matching generative priors with 3DGS refinement to achieve both geometric completeness and observational consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods suffer from a trade-off: generative models (e.g., Flow-Matching) produce high-quality assets but suffer from "synthetic bias" and lack instance alignment, while optimization-based methods (e.g., 3DGS) are faithful on visible surfaces but fail on unobserved geometry.
AI.method: A training-free, guided inverse problem framework using dual-space guidance to steer the ODE trajectory of a flow-matching model, integrating learned generative priors with real-world observation consistency, followed by a 3DGS refinement stage for photorealistic reconstruction.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generative models and optimization-based frameworks in both geometric completeness and view-dependent appearance fidelity on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, especially under severe occlusions.
AI.conclusion: FlowObject effectively bridges the gap between generative priors and observation-driven reconstruction, achieving both geometric completeness and observational consistency without additional training.

title: Graph Grounded Cross Attention Transformer Neural Network for Structurally Constrained Full Event Sequence Generation in Predictive Process Monitoring
summary: Structurally constrained event sequence generation remains challenging because generated paths must preserve transition feasibility, temporal order, termination, and attribute consistency. In predictive process monitoring (PPM), this challenge appears as full event sequence generation, whereas existing work mainly addresses component tasks such as next activity, remaining time, outcome, and attribute prediction. This paper proposes the Graph Grounded Cross Attention Transformer Neural Network (GGATN) for this unified PPM task. GGATN uses a global process graph as structured activity memory, contextualizes sequence positions through Transformer self attention, and injects process topology through graph grounded cross attention. Unlike autoregressive decoding, GGATN generates activities, timestamps, length, and event level and sequence level attributes in a single pass, followed by Viterbi style graph constrained decoding for feasible paths and explicit termination. Experiments on six benchmark event logs show more reliable generation quality than local instruction prompted LLM baselines. GGATN achieves strong performance on sequence similarity, Damerau Levenshtein similarity, bigram based control flow similarity, and duration distribution, while maintaining zero hallucinated activities and zero sequence level attribute inconsistency. Ablation analyses confirm the global graph encoder as a stable structural prior. Interpretability analyses show how graph structure, sequence context, feedback refinement, and constrained decoding shape generation.

AI.tldr: GGATN is a unified model that generates complete event sequences (activities, timestamps, attributes) in one pass using global process graphs and constrained decoding, outperforming LLM baselines on six benchmark logs with zero hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Existing PPM work mainly addresses component tasks (next activity, remaining time, etc.), but full structurally constrained event sequence generation remains challenging due to required transition feasibility, temporal order, termination, and attribute consistency.
AI.method: GGATN combines Transformer self-attention for sequence positions, global process graphs as structured activity memory, and graph grounded cross attention for process topology. It generates all elements in a single pass, then applies Viterbi-style constrained decoding for feasible paths and explicit termination.
AI.result: On six benchmark event logs, GGATN outperforms LLM baselines in sequence similarity, Damerau-Levenshtein similarity, bigram control flow similarity, and duration distribution, with zero hallucinated activities and zero attribute inconsistency.
AI.conclusion: GGATN demonstrates that global graph encoders serve as stable structural priors for reliable event sequence generation. Ablation and interpretability analyses confirm the contributions of graph structure, sequence context, feedback refinement, and constrained decoding.

title: Low-Rank Tensor Completion Based on Fractional Regularization with Ky Fan p-k Norm
summary: This paper addresses low-rank tensor completion (LRTC) by proposing a novel nonconvex surrogate, namely the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Ky Fan p-k norm (TNPK), to accurately approximate the tensor tubal rank. The TNPK possesses appealing properties, including scale invariance, parameter flexibility, and the existence of closed-form solutions under specific choices of p and k. With specific parameter settings of p and k, it reduces to the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Ky Fan k norm (TNK) or the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Frobenius norm (TNF). We construct a LRTC model and, under the tensor null space property (NSP), prove that low-rank tensors are local minimizers of the proposed model. Moreover, we derive the proximal operator of the Ky Fan p-k inverse-norm and further develop an efficient alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm with guaranteed subsequential convergence under mild conditions. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate the superior performance of our method against state-of-the-art competitors.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel nonconvex surrogate (TNPK) for low-rank tensor completion, proves theoretical guarantees under tensor null space property, develops an efficient ADMM algorithm, and demonstrates superior performance on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.motivation: Existing tensor nuclear norm approximations are inaccurate for tensor tubal rank; the authors aim to develop a better nonconvex surrogate that is scale-invariant, flexible, and has closed-form solutions.
AI.method: Proposes TNPK (ratio of tensor nuclear norm to tensor Ky Fan p-k norm); builds LRTC model; proves local minimizer property under tensor NSP; derives proximal operator for Ky Fan p-k inverse-norm; develops ADMM algorithm with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show superior performance over state-of-the-art competitors.
AI.conclusion: TNPK is an effective nonconvex surrogate for tensor tubal rank with strong theoretical properties and practical algorithmic efficiency.

title: Low-Cost Neuromorphic Fall Detection Using Synthetic Event Data and Hybrid SNNs
comment: 4 pages, 6 figures, presented at ICONS 2025 during the Poster Session, but not published
summary: This work presents the development of hybrid models that integrate spiking neural networks (SNNs) with components of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to learn from simulated event-based camera data (Dynamic Vision Sensor, DVS) generated from conventional smartphone videos. Aimed primarily at human fall detection, the approach leverages the energy efficiency and spatio-temporal processing capabilities of SNNs by converting video frames into event-based data. The proposed models are evaluated through simulations on multiple datasets, comparing their performance to that of traditional machine learning models. Results demonstrate significant gains in efficiency without sacrificing accuracy, underscoring the potential of combining SNNs and DVS technology for complex tasks in real-world environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid SNN-CNN models trained on simulated event-based camera data achieve energy-efficient human fall detection with accuracy comparable to traditional machine learning models.
AI.motivation: To leverage the energy efficiency and spatio-temporal processing capabilities of SNNs for human fall detection, by converting conventional smartphone videos into simulated event-based (DVS) data.
AI.method: Hybrid models integrating spiking neural networks (SNNs) with convolutional neural network (CNN) components, trained on simulated event-based camera data generated from conventional video frames.
AI.result: The hybrid models demonstrated significant gains in efficiency while maintaining accuracy comparable to traditional machine learning approaches across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: Combining SNNs with DVS technology shows strong potential for energy-efficient and accurate human fall detection in real-world environments.

title: Benchmarking Large Vision-Language Models on Fine-Grained Image Tasks: From Evaluation to Diagnosis
summary: Recent advancements in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable multimodal perception and reasoning capabilities. While numerous benchmarks have evaluated LVLMs from holistic or task-specific perspectives, their capabilities on fine-grained image tasks-fundamental to computer vision-remain insufficiently understood. To address this gap, we introduce FG-BMK, a comprehensive fine-grained evaluation benchmark containing 1.01 million questions and 0.28 million images, covering diverse scenarios from common object-centric domains to specialized domains. FG-BMK jointly evaluates dialogue-level fine-grained semantic recognition and feature-level visual discriminability through human-oriented and machine-oriented paradigms, enabling diagnostic analysis of whether LVLM failures arise from insufficient visual representations, weak visual-to-semantic grounding, or limited fine-grained knowledge. Through extensive experiments on a diverse set of representative LVLMs/VLMs, we find that current LVLMs remain inadequate fine-grained recognizers, with failures arising from intertwined bottlenecks in visual representations, semantic grounding, modality alignment, and category-level knowledge. We further analyze training design factors for improving fine-grained capabilities and examine how visual and linguistic perturbations affect LVLM predictions. These findings provide diagnostic insights into the limitations of current LVLMs and offer guidance for future data construction and model design in developing more reliable LVLMs for fine-grained visual tasks. Our code is open-source and available at https://fg-bmk.github.io/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FG-BMK, a large-scale benchmark (1.01M questions, 0.28M images) for evaluating fine-grained visual capabilities of LVLMs, revealing that current models are inadequate fine-grained recognizers due to intertwined bottlenecks in visual representations, semantic grounding, modality alignment, and knowledge limitations.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate LVLMs from holistic or task-specific perspectives, but their fine-grained image understanding capabilities remain insufficiently understood.
AI.method: The authors construct FG-BMK, a benchmark with 1.01M questions and 0.28M images covering diverse domains. It evaluates both dialogue-level semantic recognition and feature-level visual discriminability via human-oriented and machine-oriented paradigms, enabling diagnostic analysis of failure sources.
AI.result: Current LVLMs are inadequate fine-grained recognizers, with failures stemming from intertwined bottlenecks in visual representations, semantic grounding, modality alignment, and category-level knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer diagnostic insights into LVLM limitations and provide guidance for future data construction and model design for fine-grained visual tasks.

title: Private Learning with Public Feature Conditioning
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 26 pages, 9 figures
summary: We study differentially private (DP) regression in settings where each data sample includes public, non-sensitive features -- common in applications such as recommendation and advertising systems. While such label-DP or semi-sensitive-feature settings have been primarily explored in the context of classification, effective approaches for regression remain underexplored. We introduce Cond-DP, a conditioned variant of DPSGD that leverages the structure of public feature matrices to improve optimization under privacy constraints. Motivated by the observation that these public features often exhibit rapidly decaying spectra, Cond-DP incorporates a data-driven conditioning matrix to reshape the optimization landscape and accelerate convergence. We provide convergence guarantees for convex, strongly convex, and non-convex settings, and recover standard DPSGD as a special case when the conditioning matrix is the identity. We show how to construct an effective conditioning matrix for Cond-DP directly from public features, enabling provably faster convergence than DPSGD in private linear regression without incurring additional privacy cost. Empirically, Cond-DP with this conditioning matrix consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across a wide range of datasets and model architectures under label DP, demonstrating strong and robust performance in practice.

AI.tldr: Cond-DP is a differentially private regression method that uses public non-sensitive features to condition the optimization landscape, enabling faster convergence than standard DPSGD without additional privacy cost.
AI.motivation: While label-DP and semi-sensitive-feature settings have been explored for classification, effective approaches for regression remain underexplored. Public features often exhibit rapidly decaying spectra, which can be leveraged to improve optimization under privacy constraints.
AI.method: Cond-DP extends DPSGD by incorporating a data-driven conditioning matrix constructed from public feature matrices. This reshapes the optimization landscape to accelerate convergence, with convergence guarantees provided for convex, strongly convex, and non-convex settings. Standard DPSGD is recovered when the conditioning matrix is the identity.
AI.result: Cond-DP achieves provably faster convergence than DPSGD in private linear regression without additional privacy cost. Empirically, it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse datasets and model architectures under label DP.
AI.conclusion: Cond-DP demonstrates that leveraging public non-sensitive features via conditioning matrices is an effective and practical strategy for improving differentially private regression, with strong and robust performance.

title: DREAM: Extending Vision-Language Models with Dual-Objective Encoding for Cross-Modal Retrieval
summary: In today's media-driven world, the exponential growth of video content across domains such as surveillance, education, and entertainment has made retrieving semantically relevant videos via natural language queries increasingly critical. Early video retrieval systems relied on handcrafted features or shallow cross-modal mappings, limiting their ability to capture complex semantics and temporal dynamics. While large-scale vision-language models have improved cross-modal alignment, challenges remain in modeling fine-grained temporal dependencies and nuanced linguistic structures. In this paper, we introduce DREAM: Dual-path Representation Enhancement and Alignment Model, a novel multimodal framework that addresses these limitations through enhanced visual and textual encoding. DREAM incorporates a hybrid language modeling strategy that combines masked and permuted language modeling objectives to capture both local and global linguistic semantics. On the visual side, we design a hierarchical vision encoder with cascaded group attention, which integrates spatial and temporal information through multi-stage token interaction and coarse-to-fine attention refinement. We validate DREAM through comprehensive evaluations on the widely-used MSRVTT, MSVD and LSMDC benchmark datasets, where it achieves new state-of-the-art R1 scores of 49.4%, 49.7% and 27.3%, respectively. Qualitative analyses further show the model's ability to maintain coherent attention across frames and align complex queries with dynamic video content. These findings underscore the effectiveness of hierarchical attention and dual-objective textual modeling in enabling robust, context-aware video retrieval, and pave the way for future research in advancing cross-modal representation learning.

AI.tldr: DREAM is a dual-path video retrieval model using hybrid language modeling (masked + permuted) and a hierarchical vision encoder with cascaded group attention, achieving SOTA results on MSRVTT, MSVD, and LSMDC.
AI.motivation: Existing video retrieval systems struggle with fine-grained temporal dependencies and nuanced linguistic structures due to reliance on handcrafted features or shallow cross-modal mappings.
AI.method: A dual-path framework with (1) hybrid language modeling combining masked and permuted objectives for local/global semantics, and (2) hierarchical vision encoder using cascaded group attention for multi-stage spatial-temporal token interaction.
AI.result: SOTA R1 scores of 49.4% (MSRVTT), 49.7% (MSVD), and 27.3% (LSMDC), with qualitative evidence of coherent cross-modal attention.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical attention and dual-objective textual modeling are effective for robust, context-aware video retrieval, paving the way for future cross-modal representation learning.

title: RouteJudge: An Open Platform for Reproducible and Preference-Aware LLM Routing
comment: Accepted by Pluralistic Alignment Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: We present RouteJudge, an online pairwise preference evaluation framework for LLM routing systems, with a public platform available at https://routejudge.cn. Different from model-level response evaluation, RouteJudge focuses on router-level decision quality. For each user query, multiple routing strategies independently recommend candidate models under the same model pool and budget constraints. The selected model responses are then presented to users through anonymous pairwise comparisons, and the resulting user preferences are attributed back to the routing strategies behind the compared responses. Each evaluation record stores the query, routing decisions, model responses, preference labels, cost, latency, and task metadata, enabling preference-aware, cost-aware, and task-conditioned analysis of LLM routers. To support the continuous expansion of routing methods in RouteJudge, we further release ORBIT (Optimal Routing and Budgeted Inference Toolbox), a modular and extensible toolbox that standardizes the end-to-end workflow of LLM routing. ORBIT provides unified interfaces for benchmark loading, query representation, router implementation, budget-aware evaluation, and method comparison, allowing researchers to develop and evaluate routing algorithms under consistent protocols. It also serves as the submission and integration layer for RouteJudge: researchers can implement routing methods within ORBIT, validate them on existing routing benchmarks, and submit compatible routers for online preference-based evaluation. The code of ORBIT is available at https://github.com/AIGNLAI/LAMDA-ORBIT.

AI.tldr: RouteJudge is an online pairwise preference evaluation framework for LLM routing systems, along with ORBIT, a modular toolbox for developing and evaluating routing algorithms.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation methods focus on model-level response quality rather than router-level decision quality. There is a lack of standardized, preference-based, cost-aware evaluation frameworks for LLM routing systems.
AI.method: RouteJudge uses anonymous pairwise comparisons where multiple routing strategies recommend candidate models for each query. User preferences are attributed back to the routing strategies. ORBIT provides modular interfaces for benchmark loading, query representation, router implementation, and budget-aware evaluation.
AI.result: The framework stores detailed evaluation records including query, routing decisions, model responses, preference labels, cost, latency, and task metadata. ORBIT standardizes the end-to-end workflow of LLM routing and serves as the submission layer for RouteJudge.
AI.conclusion: RouteJudge enables preference-aware, cost-aware, and task-conditioned analysis of LLM routers, and ORBIT facilitates standardized development and evaluation of routing algorithms for the community.

title: Taming I2V models for Image HOI Editing: A Cognitive Benchmark and Agentic Self-Correcting Framework
summary: Current image editing methods excel at static attributes but fail at complex Human-Object Interactions (HOI), a critical challenge unaddressed by existing benchmarks that conflate HOI with static attributes, relying on global metrics incapable of simultaneously assessing dynamic interaction validity and entangled human-object pair preservation. Thus, we first introduce HOI-Edit, a comprehensive benchmark with three progressive cognitive levels, which features an automated metric HOI-Eval that reliably evaluates instance-level interaction by letting VLM Q&A after thinking with images containing grounded Human-Object pairs. Considering the task's essence of remodeling dynamic relationships, we benchmark Image-to-Video (I2V) models, finding them inherently suited for dynamic editing due to their temporal generation capabilities. Crucially, beyond superior performance, this capability provides a "replay of the failure process," offering unique diagnosability into why errors occur. We thus propose SCPE (Self-Correcting Process Editing), a novel, agentic self-correcting framework that constrains the generation of I2V models through iteratively refined prompts, enabling the generated videos to more accurately present the target HOI. Extracted frames from these videos are the final editing results. On HOI-Edit, SCPE achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art (SOTA) editing models like Nano Banana on interaction. Code is available at https://github.com/oceanflowlab/HOI-Edit.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces HOI-Edit, a benchmark for evaluating Human-Object Interaction editing in images, along with HOI-Eval (an automated metric using VLM Q&A) and SCPE (a self-correcting framework leveraging I2V models for dynamic HOI editing).
AI.motivation: Existing image editing methods and benchmarks fail to handle complex Human-Object Interactions (HOI), conflating dynamic interactions with static attributes and lacking metrics that assess both interaction validity and human-object pair preservation.
AI.method: The authors propose: (1) HOI-Edit benchmark with three progressive cognitive levels, (2) HOI-Eval automated metric using VLM Q&A with grounded human-object pairs, (3) benchmarking I2V models for dynamic editing, and (4) SCPE framework that iteratively refines prompts to constrain I2V generation for accurate HOI editing.
AI.result: SCPE achieves performance competitive with SOTA editing models (e.g., Nano Banana) on interaction; I2V models inherently suit dynamic HOI editing and provide diagnosability via "replay of the failure process."
AI.conclusion: I2V models are naturally suited for dynamic HOI editing, and the SCPE self-correcting framework effectively improves editing accuracy; the work fills a gap in HOI-specific image editing evaluation and methodology.

title: Online Distributional Prediction via Latent Cluster Geometry Under Drift and Corruption
summary:
Online learning in non-stationary streams is often formulated as tracking a point estimate, but many applications require predicting the full data-generating distribution. We study online distributional prediction under drift and adversarial corruption. Our approach represents each candidate law through a latent cluster geometry: a variable-size configuration of centers that organizes probability mass and induces a predictive distribution. A Gibbs quasi-posterior over these configurations yields an online predictor by posterior averaging, and the resulting variable-dimensional posterior can be sampled with reversible-jump MCMC. The method therefore avoids specifying a parametric streaming law while retaining a structured latent space for uncertainty, regularization, and comparison.
  We evaluate performance by cumulative Wasserstein-1 regret against the time-varying true law. The analysis separates two effects: corruption perturbs the loss-based posterior update, whereas drift makes long-horizon posterior memory stale. We address the latter with a restarted variant that temporally localizes the same quasi-Bayesian update. The resulting high-probability bounds decompose into a PAC-Bayesian complexity term, a corruption-sensitive posterior perturbation term, and a dynamic optimal-transport term driven by \(A_T^{\mathrm{OT}}=\sum_{t=2}^T W_2^2(p_{t-1}^*,p_t^*)\). Under bounded support, stable latent geometry, predictive-map regularity, oracle realizability, localized restart windows, sublinear transport action, and sublinear corruption budget, the restarted predictor achieves sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret. These guarantees require no parametric model for the stream, drift mechanism, or corruption process.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a fully nonparametric online distributional prediction method for non-stationary streams, handling both drift and adversarial corruption via a Gibbs quasi-posterior over latent cluster geometries, with restarted variants achieving sublinear Wasserstein-1 regret under mild conditions.
AI.motivation: Online learning under non-stationary streams often only tracks point estimates, but many applications require predicting the full data-generating distribution. The authors aim to handle both drift and adversarial corruption without parametric assumptions on the stream, drift mechanism, or corruption process.
AI.method: A Gibbs quasi-posterior over latent cluster geometries (variable-size center configurations) produces online predictors via posterior averaging, sampled with reversible-jump MCMC. A restarted variant temporally localizes the quasi-Bayesian update to address stale posterior memory from drift.
AI.result: High-probability regret bounds decompose into PAC-Bayesian complexity, corruption-sensitive posterior perturbation, and dynamic optimal-transport terms (driven by \(A_T^{\mathrm{OT}}=\sum_{t=2}^T W_2^2(p_{t-1}^*,p_t^*)\)). Under bounded support, stable geometry, regularity, realizability, localized restarts, sublinear transport, and sublinear corruption, sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret is achieved.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a nonparametric, structured approach to online distributional prediction under drift and corruption, with theoretical guarantees and no parametric modeling requirements.

title: PorTEXTO: A European Portuguese Benchmark for Visual Text Extraction
summary: European Portuguese (pt-PT) is largely absent from OCR benchmarks, which skew toward high-resource languages. The few benchmarks that cover pt-PT focus on historical artifacts and literature. This work addresses modern OCR applications, introducing PorTEXTO, the first benchmark for contemporary and culturally relevant pt-PT visual text extraction. To ascertain quality, we employ an annotation pipeline combining transcriptions from a frontier LVLM with exhaustive review by native speakers. We observe a sharp performance drop from synthetic to real world samples in most models, and find that, currently, specialized multilingual data is a better driver for pt-PT performance than model size or resolution budget, motivating the release of open pt-PT OCR resources.

AI.tldr: PorTEXTO is the first OCR benchmark for modern European Portuguese, revealing that specialized multilingual data improves performance more than model size or resolution.
AI.motivation: European Portuguese is largely absent from OCR benchmarks, which focus on high-resource languages or historical texts, leaving modern pt-PT OCR needs unaddressed.
AI.method: Introduces PorTEXTO benchmark; uses an annotation pipeline combining frontier LVLM transcriptions with native speaker reviews for quality assurance.
AI.result: Sharp performance drop from synthetic to real-world samples; specialized multilingual data outperforms model size or resolution budget for pt-PT.
AI.conclusion: Open pt-PT OCR resources should be released; data quality and domain specificity matter more than scale for low-resource language OCR.

title: Bayesian Anytime Pareto Set Identification for Multi-Objective Multi-Armed Bandits
summary: Identifying Pareto optimal solutions is critical to support multi-objective decision-making. We introduce the first anytime Multi-Objective Multi-Armed Bandit algorithm for the Pareto Set Identification problem, taking a Bayesian approach: Top-Two Pareto Front Thompson Sampling (TTPFTS). We benchmark TTPFTS against state-of-the-art fixed-budget Pareto Set Identification algorithms on synthetic environments. Next, we demonstrate its practical utility in a challenging multi-objective molecular discovery setting by efficiently exploring an ultra-large synthesis-on-demand molecular library. Furthermore, we introduce a novel uncertainty quantification metric that estimates our algorithm's confidence in the predicted Pareto set. We demonstrate that this metric effectively proxies true performance, yielding a robust methodology for monitoring learning progress in complex settings. Finally, we complement these empirical findings with a theoretical proof of the algorithm's asymptotic correctness.

AI.tldr: First anytime Multi-Objective Multi-Armed Bandit algorithm for Pareto Set Identification (TTPFTS) with Bayesian approach, demonstrated on synthetic benchmarks and molecular discovery, with uncertainty quantification and asymptotic correctness proof.
AI.motivation: Identifying Pareto optimal solutions is critical for multi-objective decision-making, but existing algorithms are limited to fixed-budget settings; an anytime algorithm is needed.
AI.method: Introduces Top-Two Pareto Front Thompson Sampling (TTPFTS), a Bayesian anytime algorithm for Pareto Set Identification in multi-objective multi-armed bandits, along with an uncertainty quantification metric for monitoring learning progress.
AI.result: Benchmarked against state-of-the-art fixed-budget methods on synthetic environments; demonstrated practical utility in ultra-large molecular library exploration; uncertainty metric effectively proxies true performance.
AI.conclusion: TTPFTS is the first anytime algorithm for Pareto Set Identification with proven asymptotic correctness, supported by both empirical results and theoretical proof.

title: DVANet: Degradation-aware Visual-prior Alignment Network for Image Restoration
comment: All-in-One Image Restoration; Deep Unfolding; Degradation Representation; Visual Prior
summary: All-in-One image restoration aims to develop a unified restoration framework for handling diverse degradation types. Existing end-to-end methods usually regard the restoration process as a black-box mapping, lacking an explicit optimization interpretation. Although deep unfolding provides an interpretable iterative modeling paradigm for image restoration, existing methods mostly rely on fixed degradation assumptions or predefined degradation information, making them difficult to adapt to unified restoration requirements under complex degradations and locally damaged content. This limitation restricts their performance in degradation suppression and structural detail recovery. To address these issues, this paper proposes DVANet, a deep unfolding network inspired by the half-quadratic splitting optimization algorithm, which formulates unified image restoration under complex degradations as a collaborative unfolding process between degradation-aware observation consistency and visual-prior-guided reconstruction. Specifically, in the degradation-aware observation consistency branch, a degradation representation module is employed to extract global degradation attributes and local degradation cues, and degradation-conditioned mapping is used to enhance the model's adaptability to different degradation types. In the visual-prior-guided reconstruction branch, DINOv3 is introduced to provide structural and semantic information as hierarchical visual priors, thereby complementing the missing structural information in damaged regions and improving detail recovery. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DVANet achieves superior or competitive performance on multi-scenario degradation and cross-domain image restoration tasks, showing favorable degradation adaptability and generalization ability.

AI.tldr: DVANet is a deep unfolding network for all-in-one image restoration that jointly optimizes degradation-aware observation consistency and visual-prior-guided reconstruction, outperforming existing methods on multi-scenario and cross-domain tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing all-in-one image restoration methods either lack explicit optimization interpretation (black-box end-to-end) or rely on fixed degradation assumptions, limiting their adaptability to complex, mixed degradations and locally damaged content.
AI.method: DVANet is a deep unfolding network based on the half-quadratic splitting algorithm. It has two collaborative branches: (1) a degradation-aware observation consistency branch that extracts global and local degradation cues via a degradation representation module and uses degradation-conditioned mapping; (2) a visual-prior-guided reconstruction branch that leverages DINOv3 to provide hierarchical structural and semantic priors for detail recovery.
AI.result: DVANet achieves superior or competitive performance on multi-scenario degradation and cross-domain image restoration tasks, demonstrating strong degradation adaptability and generalization ability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed collaborative unfolding framework effectively unifies diverse degradation types, bridging the gap between interpretable optimization and practical all-in-one restoration.

title: Learning from Own Solutions: Self-Conditioned Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven substantial progress in training LLMs for reasoning tasks, but representative methods such as GRPO assign uniform credit across all tokens, wasting gradient on routine tokens while under-crediting pivotal reasoning steps. Existing token-level credit assignment methods require resources beyond the model's own rollouts. GRPO variants rely on process reward models or ground-truth answers. Knowledge distillation assigns credit through per-token divergence but requires external teachers (On-Policy Distillation) or privileged information (On-Policy Self Distillation). However, these dependencies limit applicability in the pure RLVR setting. We observe that conditioning the model on its own verified trajectories induces a measurable per-token KL divergence between the original and conditioned distributions, and prove that distilling from a self-teacher constructed by verified trajectories leads to infeasible weighted-average solutions when multiple verified trajectories exist. We propose SC-GRPO (Self-Conditioned GRPO), which uses KL divergence mentioned before as a multiplicative weight on GRPO gradients. Across five benchmarks spanning math, code, and agentic tasks, SC-GRPO consistently outperforms 8.1% over GRPO and 5.9% over DAPO with stronger OOD performance. Moreover, SC-GRPO achieves higher performance than OPD.

AI.tldr: SC-GRPO improves RLVR for LLMs by using per-token KL divergence from self-conditioned trajectories as multiplicative weights on GRPO gradients, outperforming prior methods by 5-8% across math, code, and agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods (e.g., GRPO) assign uniform credit across all tokens, wasting gradients on routine tokens and under-crediting pivotal reasoning steps. Alternative token-level credit assignment methods require external resources (PRMs, ground-truth answers, or teacher models), limiting applicability in pure RLVR settings.
AI.method: The authors propose SC-GRPO, which uses the per-token KL divergence between the original model distribution and the distribution conditioned on verified trajectories as a multiplicative weight on GRPO gradients. This leverages the model's own rollouts without external resources.
AI.result: SC-GRPO consistently outperforms GRPO by 8.1% and DAPO by 5.9% across five benchmarks (math, code, agentic tasks), with stronger OOD performance. It also achieves higher performance than On-Policy Distillation (OPD).
AI.conclusion: Self-conditioned token-level credit assignment via KL divergence weighting is an effective and resource-efficient approach for RLVR, eliminating the need for external teachers or privileged information while improving reasoning performance.

title: AMALIA-VL: A Native European Portuguese Open-Source Vision and Language Model
summary: Large Vision and Language Models (LVLMs) have advanced rapidly, yet European Portuguese (pt-PT) remains systematically underserved by existing open-source multimodal models, which either conflate it with Brazilian Portuguese or severely under-represent it in their training data mixes. We introduce AMALIA-VL, the first open-source instruction-tuned LVLM built natively for pt-PT, pairing a high-resolution vision encoder with dynamic image tiling and a fully open pt-PT-optimized language model via a learned connector. We contribute with a purposefully designed three-stage training process - vision-language alignment, general visual instruction tuning, and preference optimization - together with a pt-PT-centric multimodal data mix combining curated and translated public datasets with novel datasets that address the near-total absence of European Portuguese multimodal resources. Our evaluation shows that AMALIA-VL establishes a strong baseline for open-source pt-PT LVLMs.We will release model weights, training data, and construction pipelines along with machine-translated pt-PT evaluation benchmarks to help democratize pt-PT LVLM development.

AI.tldr: AMALIA-VL is the first open-source instruction-tuned Large Vision and Language Model built natively for European Portuguese, featuring a high-resolution vision encoder, dynamic image tiling, and a three-stage training process (alignment, instruction tuning, preference optimization) with a curated pt-PT multimodal data mix. It establishes a strong baseline for open-source pt-PT LVLMs.
AI.motivation: European Portuguese (pt-PT) is systematically underserved by existing open-source multimodal LVLMs, which either conflate it with Brazilian Portuguese or under-represent it in training data.
AI.method: High-resolution vision encoder + dynamic image tiling + pt-PT-optimized language model connected via a learned connector; three-stage training: (1) vision-language alignment, (2) general visual instruction tuning, (3) preference optimization; uses a pt-PT-centric multimodal data mix with curated, translated, and novel datasets.
AI.result: AMALIA-VL establishes a strong baseline for open-source European Portuguese LVLMs.
AI.conclusion: The model, training data, construction pipelines, and machine-translated pt-PT evaluation benchmarks will be released to democratize pt-PT LVLM development.

title: Reinforcement Learning Foundation Models Should Already Be A Thing
summary:
Foundation models for language and vision are powered by internet-scale data, while structured domains (tabular prediction, time-series forecasting, graph learning, reinforcement learning) are not. The substitute is synthetic data, which shifts the burden from collection to prior design. Such priors already exist for many structured tasks: TabPFN and its successors solve tabular classification with a transformer pretrained on a synthetic Bayesian prior.
  We make two points. \textbf{First}, reinforcement learning is the conspicuous gap: sampling a synthetic MDP is as feasible as sampling a synthetic tabular dataset, yet no in-context RL work treats prior design as a primary objective. \textbf{Second}, MDPs admit a fixed-size sufficient statistic, independent of the episodes observed and tabular in shape, which makes them directly amenable to the attention-based architectures used for tabular foundation models, with a policy head replacing the supervised target. Together these define the agenda for an RL foundation model.
  As a proof of concept, we train one model entirely on synthetic MDPs and show that, with no task-specific tuning, it solves held-out tabular benchmarks in context, both online and offline: online, in far fewer episodes than UCB-VI and tabular Q-learning, and offline, competitively with VI-LCB.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that reinforcement learning (RL) can be treated similarly to tabular foundation models by training on synthetic MDPs, using a fixed-size sufficient statistic for MDPs that allows attention-based architectures to solve RL tasks in-context without task-specific tuning.
AI.motivation: While foundation models in language and vision leverage internet-scale data, structured domains like RL lack such data. The paper identifies RL as a conspicuous gap where synthetic MDP generation is feasible but prior design hasn't been treated as a primary objective in in-context RL work.
AI.method: The authors propose training a single model entirely on synthetic MDPs (Markov Decision Processes), using a fixed-size sufficient statistic of MDPs that is independent of observed episodes and tabular in shape. This makes MDPs amenable to attention-based architectures (like transformers) used in tabular foundation models, with a policy head replacing the supervised target.
AI.result: The proof-of-concept model, without any task-specific tuning, solves held-out tabular benchmarks in context. Online, it achieves this in far fewer episodes than UCB-VI and tabular Q-learning. Offline, it performs competitively with VI-LCB.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes an agenda for developing an RL foundation model by treating prior design as a primary objective and leveraging the tabular structure of MDPs' sufficient statistics, showing that synthetic MDP training can enable in-context RL problem solving.

title: ProductConsistency: Improving Product Identity Preservation in Instruction-Based Image Editing via SFT and RL
summary:
Recent advances in instruction-based image editing have enabled models to perform complex visual edits from natural language instructions. However, in product-centric scenarios where preserving product features, branding, and textual elements are critical, current open and closed source models often struggle to maintain this fine-grained object identity. This issue is further compounded by the lack of datasets for instruction-based product image editing with text fidelity constraints, leaving it largely treated as an implicit capability of instruction-based image editing models.
  In this work, we introduce the ProductConsistency dataset which is designed to improve product-centric image editing. Our approach includes a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset of 87k samples for product editing, a reinforcement learning (RL) dataset with 869 unique product images, and a new benchmark dataset, the ProductConsistency Benchmark, to allow rigorous and standardized evaluation of editing models. To guide RL training, we propose a Cyclic Consistency reward that enforces semantic preservation of product identity by using caption similarity between the original product description and captions generated from the edited image. We fine-tune both Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 and Flux.1-Kontext-dev using our dataset and demonstrate consistent improvements over baseline models in OCR and Perceptual metrics, and MLLM-based evaluations as well, indicating stronger product consistency, text rendering, and overall visual quality; with the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model achieving a 5x reduction in the character error rate. The code and pipeline is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ProductConsistency-6FCC/README.md

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the ProductConsistency dataset and a Cyclic Consistency reward method to improve instruction-based image editing for product-centric scenarios, achieving significant gains in product identity preservation and text fidelity (e.g., 5x reduction in character error rate).
AI.motivation: Current instruction-based image editing models fail to preserve fine-grained product identity (features, branding, text) in product-centric scenarios, and there is no dedicated dataset to address this gap.
AI.method: The authors create the ProductConsistency dataset (87k SFT samples, 869 RL images, a benchmark), propose a Cyclic Consistency reward leveraging caption similarity for RL training, and fine-tune Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 and Flux.1-Kontext-dev models.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models show consistent improvements in OCR, Perceptual, and MLLM-based evaluations, with Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 achieving a 5x reduction in character error rate.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and Cyclic Consistency reward effectively enhance product identity preservation and text rendering in instruction-based image editing, with code and pipeline publicly available.

title: Maturing Markov Decision Processes: Decision Making under Increasing Information and Shrinking Action Sets
summary: Sequential decision problems often exhibit an asymmetric evolution of information and decision flexibility: as a decision cycle unfolds, the agent receives richer information while feasible actions expire due to operational cutoffs, commitments, or resource constraints. Standard MDP formulations typically flatten this structure into stage-dependent state descriptions and action masks, thereby obscuring the nested information--action asymmetry that determines which decisions are urgent and which can be deferred. We introduce Maturing Markov Decision Processes (MMDPs), a formulation built around this information--action asymmetry. We characterize one of its key consequences through an expiring-action priority principle, which identifies the actions that must be resolved before the next stage. Motivated by this structure, we develop a structure-aware reinforcement learning framework with stage-aware policy design, expiring-action abstraction, and search-augmented learning with distillation. Experiments on a controlled multi-supplier replenishment problem, simplified cash-management environments of increasing complexity, and a production-scale simulator show that explicitly modeling this asymmetry improves learning efficiency and becomes increasingly valuable as decision problems scale.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Maturing Markov Decision Processes (MMDPs) to model asymmetric information-action dynamics in sequential decisions, proposes a structure-aware RL framework leveraging an expiring-action priority principle, and demonstrates improved learning efficiency across several problem domains.
AI.motivation: Standard MDPs flatten the asymmetric evolution of information and decision flexibility (information grows while actions expire), failing to capture which decisions are urgent versus deferrable. The authors aim to formalize and exploit this structure for better learning.
AI.method: Introduces MMDPs with an expiring-action priority principle, and develops a structure-aware RL framework featuring stage-aware policy design, expiring-action abstraction, and search-augmented learning with distillation.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-supplier replenishment, cash-management environments, and a production-scale simulator show that explicitly modeling information-action asymmetry improves learning efficiency, with benefits growing as problem scale increases.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the asymmetric evolution of information and action flexibility in sequential decision problems yields significant improvements in RL learning efficiency, especially for large-scale problems.

title: Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset: A Historical Document Dataset for Offline Urdu Handwritten Text Recognition with CRNN-Based Baseline Evaluation
summary: Automatic Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) is inherently a challenging task, and its complexity is further increased when dealing with cursive scripts. Although significant efforts have been made on various cursive scripts, research regarding Urdu Handwritten Text Recognition (UHTR) has been relatively limited. This lag of research is primarily due to the unique challenges posed by its script, and the scarcity and unavailability of benchmark datasets. Therefore, to advance research in UHTR, this study presents a specialized real dataset called the Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset (UKHD). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first offline Urdu handwritten text lines dataset specifically curated from the materials written by Katibs in historical times. It encompasses a diverse range of flat nib writing variations in the Nastalique calligraphic style. Additionally, the effectiveness of different CRNN-based hybrid models has been evaluated to identify the optimal architecture for Urdu Katib Handwriting Recognition (UKHR). Among the analyzed models, the CNN-BGRU-CTC model showed more robust performance, with low Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER). This research work aims to support and encourage the research community in developing a robust recognition system for preserving Urdu handwritten literature.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces UKHD, the first offline Urdu handwritten text dataset from historical Katib manuscripts, and evaluates CRNN-based hybrid models, finding CNN-BGRU-CTC achieves the best CER and WER for Urdu Katib handwriting recognition.
AI.motivation: HTR for cursive Urdu scripts is under-researched due to unique script challenges and lack of benchmark datasets; this work aims to fill that gap to preserve Urdu handwritten literature.
AI.method: Constructed UKHD (Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset) from historical Katib materials with Nastalique calligraphic style variations; evaluated multiple CRNN-based hybrid models for recognition.
AI.result: CNN-BGRU-CTC model outperformed other architectures, achieving low Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER).
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundational benchmark dataset and optimal model architecture to advance Urdu HTR research and preserve historical Urdu manuscripts.

title: GateMem: Benchmarking Memory Governance in Multi-Principal Shared-Memory Agents
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/rzhub/GateMem and https://huggingface.co/datasets/Ray368/GateMem
summary: Memory benchmarks for LLM agents largely assume single-user settings, leaving shared assistants for hospitals, workplaces, campuses, and households understudied. In these deployments, multiple principals write to a common memory pool and query it under different roles, scopes, and relationships, so memory quality requires governance as well as recall. We introduce GateMem, a benchmark for multi-principal shared-memory agents. GateMem jointly evaluates utility for legitimate long-horizon requests with state updates, access control across contextual authorization boundaries, and agent-facing active forgetting after explicit deletion requests. It spans medical, office, education, and household domains, with long-form multi-party episodes, incremental memory injection, hidden checkpoints, structured judging, and leak-target annotations. Across diverse baselines and backbone models, no method simultaneously achieves strong utility, robust access control, and reliable forgetting. Long-context prompting often yields the best governance score at high token cost, while retrieval-based and external-memory methods reduce cost yet still leak unauthorized or deleted information. These results show current memory agents remain far from reliable shared institutional deployment.

AI.tldr: GateMem benchmarks multi-principal shared-memory LLM agents across medical, office, education, and household domains, finding that no current method simultaneously achieves strong utility, access control, and forgetting.
AI.motivation: Existing memory benchmarks assume single-user settings, overlooking real-world deployments where multiple users share a common memory pool with different roles, scopes, and relationships requiring governance beyond recall.
AI.method: GateMem evaluates agents on joint utility, access control across contextual authorization boundaries, and active forgetting after deletion requests, spanning four domains with long-form multi-party episodes and hidden checkpoints.
AI.result: No method achieves strong utility, robust access control, and reliable forgetting simultaneously. Long-context prompting gives best governance at high token cost; retrieval/external-memory methods cut cost but leak unauthorized/deleted information.
AI.conclusion: Current memory agents remain far from reliable shared institutional deployment.

title: Hand-4DGS: Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting for 4D Hand Reconstruction from Egocentric Videos
summary: Dynamic 3D hand reconstruction from egocentric videos is essential for next-generation computing platforms such as AR/VR and AI glasses. Despite its importance, most prior works focus either on multi-view 3D hand reconstruction or on 4D human body reconstruction. Egocentric 4D hand reconstruction remains challenging due to fast head motion, rapid hand dynamics, severe occlusions, and inherent ambiguity from single-view observations. To address these challenges, we introduce Hand-4DGS, the first feed-forward framework for reconstructing dynamic 4D hands directly from egocentric videos, enabling both fast (~60 FPS) inference and strong generalization. Our approach incorporates a mesh-guided representation for structural priors and temporal convolutions to model dynamic motion.
